topics.title: Bioamplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Bioamplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Bioamplifier** is a precision electronic amplifier specifically engineered to detect, condition, and amplify low-amplitude, low-frequency bioelectric signals (such as ECG, EEG, EMG, and EOG) originating from living tissues, while rejecting unwanted noise, interference, and DC offset potentials. It serves as the critical first-stage analog front-end in every biomedical signal acquisition chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> A bioamplifier is a differential voltage amplifier with **high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**, **high input impedance** (≥ 5 MΩ), **low input-referred noise** (≤ few μV), **adjustable gain** (typically 100 – 10,000 V/V), and **galvanic isolation** between the patient and the measurement/recording equipment, ensuring both signal fidelity and patient safety.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are trying to listen to a single whispered conversation between two friends in the middle of a noisy railway station. Your ear has to:
1. **Focus only on the two voices** (rejecting all other sounds) → *This is CMRR*.
2. **Pick up the tiny whisper** and make it loud enough to hear → *This is High Gain*.
3. **Not be affected by the loud train horn** that is common to both ears → *This is Differential Amplification*.
4. **Have very sensitive microphones** that draw no current from the speakers → *This is High Input Impedance*.

A bioamplifier does exactly the same for the electrical whispers of the human body — the heart, brain, and muscles — which are in the **millivolt to microvolt** range and surrounded by a sea of **50 Hz mains interference** and **electrode DC offset** voltages.

### 1.3 Standard Biopotential Signal Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Biopotential | Typical Amplitude | Frequency Band | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ECG** (Electrocardiogram) | **0.5 mV – 5 mV** | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** | Heart muscle |
| **EEG** (Electroencephalogram) | **5 μV – 300 μV** | **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz** | Brain neurons |
| **EMG** (Electromyogram) | **50 μV – 5 mV** | **20 Hz – 5 kHz** | Skeletal muscles |
| **EOG** (Electro-oculogram) | **10 μV – 5 mV** | **DC – 50 Hz** | Eye movement |
| **PCG** (Phonocardiogram) | N/A (acoustic) | **20 Hz – 2 kHz** | Heart sounds |

> [!NOTE]
> The core engineering challenge is that the **signal** is often **1000× smaller** than the **noise/offset** generated at the electrode-skin interface. This is why a bioamplifier MUST be a **differential, high-CMRR** structure — never a single-ended amplifier.

### 1.4 Key Design Requirements (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 6 Mandatory Specifications of any Bioamplifier:**
> 1. **High Gain** (100 – 10,000 V/V) — to lift μV/mV signals to ADC-input range (0–5 V).
> 2. **High CMRR** (≥ 80 dB, ideally ≥ 100 dB) — to reject 50 Hz mains and electrode common-mode noise.
> 3. **High Input Impedance** (≥ 10 MΩ) — to prevent signal loading by the electrode-electrolyte interface.
> 4. **Low Noise** (Input-referred noise ≤ 5 μV RMS) — to keep SNR > 40 dB.
> 5. **Bandwidth Limitation** — to reject out-of-band motion artefacts and high-frequency EMI.
> 6. **Patient Safety (Isolation)** — galvanic isolation barrier (≥ 4000 V) between patient and mains-powered circuitry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Bioamplifier System
A modern bioamplifier is **not a single op-amp** — it is a *cascade* of carefully designed stages. Understanding the role of each stage is essential for KTU lab viva and design questions.

#### Stage-by-Stage Functional Logic

**Stage 1 — Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)**
- Provides the **bulk of the gain** (typically 10–1000 V/V).
- Provides **very high input impedance** on both inputs (symmetrical, JFET or FET-input op-amps).
- Provides **excellent CMRR** (often > 100 dB at DC, falling to ~60–80 dB at 50 Hz).
- Key ICs used in KTU labs: **AD620**, **INA128**, **INA333**, **LM324 + discrete IA**.

**Stage 2 — High-Pass Filter (HPF)**
- Blocks the **DC electrode offset** (up to ±300 mV from Ag/AgCl electrodes) that would otherwise saturate the gain stages.
- Cut-off frequency chosen just below the lowest frequency of interest.

**Stage 3 — Low-Pass Filter (LPF)**
- Anti-aliasing and noise-bandwidth limitation.
- Cut-off frequency chosen just above the highest frequency of interest.

**Stage 4 — 50 Hz Notch Filter (Twin-T or Active Notch)**
- Specifically rejects **50 Hz power-line interference** that survives CMRR rejection.
- Notch depth ≥ 30 dB, Q-factor carefully tuned.

**Stage 5 — Output Driver / Level Shifter / Isolation Barrier**
- Drives the ADC, oscilloscope, or PC-based data acquisition card.
- Provides patient isolation using optocouplers or isolation amplifiers (ISO124, ADuM-series).

### 2.2 The 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (KTU Board Core Topic)

The classic **3-op-amp INA** is the heart of every bioamplifier. It uses two input buffer stages (A1, A2) feeding a difference amplifier (A3).

#### Operating Principle
1. Both input signals $V_1$ and $V_2$ enter the **non-inverting** terminals of A1 and A2, giving **infinite** input impedance.
2. The gain-setting resistor $R_G$ is shared between the inverting inputs. The voltage across $R_G$ equals the differential input $(V_1 - V_2)$, producing a current $I = (V_1 - V_2)/R_G$ that flows through the two $R_1$ feedback resistors.
3. This current creates an amplified differential voltage at the outputs of A1 and A2.
4. The unity-gain difference amplifier (A3) subtracts the two signals, cancels the common-mode voltage, and produces a single-ended output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a 3-op-amp IA and not a simple 1-op-amp difference amplifier?**
> Because in a simple difference amplifier, the input impedance is **asymmetric** and **finite** (set by input resistors), which loads the high-impedance electrode source differently on each input, destroying the CMRR. The 3-op-amp IA presents **equal, very high impedance on both inputs** — essential for bioelectric signals.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly usable** for KTU numerical problems. Pay close attention to the symbols (use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ in writing to avoid table conflicts).

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Differential Gain of 3-Op-Amp INA** | $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | V/V | Symmetric: $R_2 = R_3$ typically, so $A_d = 1 + 2R_1/R_G$ |
| 2 | **CMRR (Numerical)** | $\mathrm{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right)$ | **dB** | $A_{cm}$ is common-mode gain |
| 3 | **Total Output Voltage** | $V_{out} = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_{cm} \!\cdot\! \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ | V | Demonstrates residual CM contamination |
| 4 | **3 dB Bandwidth** | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_3 C_3}$ | Hz | Sets LPF cut-off |
| 5 | **HPF Cut-off** | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{in} C_{in}}$ | Hz | Blocks electrode DC offset |
| 6 | **Notch Frequency (Twin-T)** | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz | Centre frequency of notch |
| 7 | **Input-Referred Noise** | $V_{n,in} = \dfrac{V_{n,out}}{A_d}$ | V (RMS) | Must be < signal amplitude |
| 8 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $\mathrm{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | Clinical minimum ≈ 40 dB |
| 9 | **Current through patient (Safety)** | $I_{leak} \le 10\,\mu\mathrm{A}$ (Type CF) | A | IEC 60601-1 limit |
| 10 | **Thermal Noise (Johnson)** | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \,\Delta f}$ | V | $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Patient Monitoring Systems**: ICU multi-parameter monitors, Holter recorders — every ECG channel starts with a bioamplifier.
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)**: EEG acquisition headsets (OpenBCI, Emotiv) use low-noise INAs (e.g., ADS1299).
- **Rehabilitation Engineering**: EMG-controlled prosthetics require surface-EMG bioamplifiers with 1 kHz bandwidth.
- **Defibrillator Pads & AEDs**: Use isolated bioamplifiers to sense ECG safely while delivering shocks.
- **Wearable Health Tech**: Smart-watches (Apple Watch ECG, Fitbit) implement single-lead bioamplifier ASICs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gain of the 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

**Circuit Description:**
- A1 and A2 are two non-inverting amplifiers sharing a common gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between their inverting inputs.
- A3 is a unity-gain difference amplifier with resistors $R_2$ (input side) and $R_3$ (feedback side), and the requirement $R_2 = R_2'$ and $R_3 = R_3'$ for symmetry.

Let the two input signals be $V_1$ (at non-inverting input of A1) and $V_2$ (at non-inverting input of A2). Both $V_1$ and $V_2$ ride on a common-mode voltage $V_{cm}$.

**Step 1 — Voltage across the gain resistor $R_G$**

Because the op-amp input terminals draw no current, the same current flows through $R_G$ and through both $R_1$ feedback resistors. By the virtual-short principle:

$$V_{(-)\text{,A1}} = V_1 \quad \text{and} \quad V_{(-)\text{,A2}} = V_2$$

Therefore, the voltage across $R_G$ is exactly the differential input:

$$V_{R_G} = V_1 - V_2$$

**Step 2 — Current through $R_G$**

$$I = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage at the outputs of A1 and A2**

The same current $I$ flows through the upper and lower $R_1$ resistors. Hence the differential output of the input stage is:

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = I \cdot (R_1 + R_G + R_1) = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)(2R_1 + R_G)}{R_G}$$

**Step 4 — Output of the input stage rearranged**

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Output of the unity-gain difference amplifier A3**

For the standard difference amplifier with $R_2 = R_2'$ and $R_3 = R_3'$:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_{o2} - V_{o1})$$

**Step 6 — Substituting and combining**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_1 - V_2) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

For a non-inverting output (typical for ECG), we invert the input assignment:

$$\boxed{\,A_d = \frac{V_{out}}{V_1 - V_2} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}\,}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for the 3-op-amp INA gain.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — CMRR of the 3-Op-Amp IA (Resistor Mismatch Analysis)

In an ideal 3-op-amp IA, any common-mode voltage $V_{cm}$ appears **identically** at both inputs and is **completely rejected** by the difference amplifier. In practice, the four resistors in the output stage ($R_2, R_2', R_3, R_3'$) are never perfectly matched.

**Step 1 — Common-mode gain of the difference stage**

Let the four resistors be $R_2 = R$, $R_2' = R + \Delta R_2$, $R_3 = R$, $R_3' = R + \Delta R_3$. The non-ideal common-mode gain is:

$$A_{cm} = \frac{\Delta R_3 \cdot R_2 - \Delta R_2 \cdot R_3}{2 R_2 R_2' + R_2 R_3 + R_2' R_3'}$$

**Step 2 — Approximation for small mismatches (typical KTU assumption)**

For small mismatches ($\Delta R \ll R$), the denominator is dominated by $2R^2$, and:

$$A_{cm} \approx \frac{\Delta R_3 - \Delta R_2}{2R}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into the CMRR equation**

$$\mathrm{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2R(1 + 2R_1/R_G)}{\vert \Delta R_3 - \Delta R_2 \vert}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Practical observation**

If resistors have **0.1 % tolerance** ($\Delta R / R = 10^{-3}$), then the resistor-limited CMRR is:

$$\mathrm{CMRR} \approx 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2}{10^{-3}}\right) \approx 66\,\mathrm{dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **why precision resistor arrays** (matched thin-film networks) or **integrated INA ICs** (AD620, INA128) are used in KTU lab bioamplifier kits — they push CMRR above **100 dB**, far beyond discrete-op-amp implementations.

---

### 3.3 Design Example — KTU Numerical Pattern

**Question:** Design the gain-setting resistor $R_G$ of an AD620-based bioamplifier for ECG, if the total desired gain is **1000 V/V**.

**Given (AD620 internal architecture):** The AD620 has $R_1 = 24.7\,\mathrm{k\Omega}$ internally, and the simplified gain formula is:

$$A_d = 1 + \frac{49.4\,\mathrm{k\Omega}}{R_G}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the desired gain**

$$1000 = 1 + \frac{49.4 \times 10^{3}}{R_G}$$

**Step 2 — Isolate $R_G$**

$$\frac{49.4 \times 10^{3}}{R_G} = 999 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_G = \frac{49.4 \times 10^{3}}{999}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate**

$$R_G = 49.449\,\Omega \approx 49.4\,\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Choose standard E12 value**

Nearest standard value: **47 Ω** (which gives $A_d \approx 1052$, acceptable for ECG where 1 mV input → 1.05 V output, well within ADC range of 0–5 V).

**Verification:**

$$V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{in} = 1052 \cdot 1\,\mathrm{mV} = 1.052\,\mathrm{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always justify why you chose a particular $R_G$ — discuss **gain accuracy**, **bandwidth** (gain × bandwidth product of AD620 is 120 kHz, so usable BW = 120 kHz / 1000 ≈ 120 Hz, sufficient for ECG), and **noise** (input voltage noise of AD620 is 9 nV/√Hz).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Bioamplifier Topology

| Application | Best Topology | Reason | Typical Gain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ECG (resting)** | 3-Op-Amp INA (AD620) | 0.05–150 Hz BW, high CMRR essential | 100 – 1000 |
| **EEG (clinical)** | Chopper-stabilized INA (INA333, ADS1299) | Sub-μV signals demand < 1 μV noise | 1000 – 10,000 |
| **EMG (surface)** | Instrumentation + active filters | Wider BW (DC – 5 kHz) | 100 – 500 |
| **Pacemaker sensing** | Custom low-power CMOS INA | μW power budget, battery operated | 50 – 200 |
| **Defibrillation-proof ECG** | Isolated INA (ADuM + INA) | Must survive 5 kV defibrillator pulse | 500 – 1000 |
| **EOG (DC-coupled)** | Low-drift chopper INA | Must measure true DC eye position | 100 – 1000 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Overall Bioamplifier Signal Chain (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Patient Body Biopotential Source ECG EEG EMG] --> nodeB[Surface Electrodes Ag AgCl]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Electrode Skin Interface High Z and DC Offset]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Stage 1 Instrumentation Amplifier AD620 INA128]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Stage 2 High Pass Filter Block DC Offset]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Stage 3 Low Pass Filter Anti Aliasing]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Stage 4 50 Hz Notch Filter Reject Mains]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Stage 5 Output Driver and Level Shifter]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Isolation Barrier Optocoupler or ADuM]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[ADC and PC or Display Recording Device]

    classDef sourceBox fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef ampBox fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef filterBox fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef outBox fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000

    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC sourceBox
    class nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG,nodeH ampBox
    class nodeI,nodeJ outBox
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The arrows indicate the **unidirectional signal flow**. Note the **isolation barrier** (nodeI) between the patient side (yellow) and the digital/PC side (green) — this is a **safety-critical** element required by IEC 60601-1.

### 4.2 Mermaid — Internal Topology of the 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeIn1[V1 positive input] --> nodeA1[Op-Amp A1 top buffer]
    nodeIn2[V2 negative input] --> nodeA2[Op-Amp A2 bottom buffer]
    nodeA1 -- R1 feedback --> nodeRG[Gain Resistor RG shared]
    nodeA2 -- R1 feedback --> nodeRG
    nodeA1 -- output Vo1 --> nodeA3[Op-Amp A3 difference amp]
    nodeA2 -- output Vo2 --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 -- R3 and R2 network --> nodeOut[Vout single ended]

    classDef inputBox fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000
    classDef ampBox fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef rgBox fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef outBox fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000

    class nodeIn1,nodeIn2 inputBox
    class nodeA1,nodeA2,nodeA3 ampBox
    class nodeRG rgBox
    class nodeOut outBox
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Frequency Response of a Typical ECG Bioamplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSub[Subsonic Rejection below 0.05 Hz] --> nodeDC[DC Blocked by HPF]
    nodeDC --> nodePass[Passband 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz]
    nodePass --> nodeRoll[Roll off above 150 Hz by LPF]
    nodeRoll --> nodeNotch[Deep Notch at 50 Hz]
    nodeNotch --> nodeStop[Stopband Rejection greater than 40 dB]

    classDef subBox fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef passBox fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef stopBox fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class nodeSub,nodeDC,nodeRoll,nodeStop subBox
    class nodePass,nodeNotch passBox
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — Pin-Conceptual Layout of the KTU Lab Bioamplifier (AD620-based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInP[Pin 3 IN positive] --> nodeIC[AD620 INA core]
    nodeInN[Pin 2 IN negative] --> nodeIC
    nodeRG1[Pin 1 RG] --> nodeRG2[Pin 8 RG]
    nodeRG1 --> nodeIC
    nodeRG2 --> nodeIC
    nodeIC --> nodeOut[Pin 6 OUT amplified]
    nodeIC --> nodeRef[Pin 5 REF for level shift]
    nodeVS[Pin 7 V plus] --> nodeIC
    nodeGND[Pin 4 V minus] --> nodeIC

    classDef pinBox fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000
    classDef icBox fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#191970,color:#000

    class nodeInP,nodeInN,nodeRG1,nodeRG2,nodeOut,nodeRef,nodeVS,nodeGND pinBox
    class nodeIC icBox
```

### 4.5 Description of Physical Setup (for KTU Lab Record)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Wiring Description (write this in your record book):**
> 1. Connect three disposable Ag/AgCl ECG electrodes to the subject (RA, LA, LL on right arm, left arm, left leg).
> 2. RA → IN+ (Pin 3), LA → IN− (Pin 2), LL → Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit for common-mode feedback.
> 3. Mount $R_G = 47\,\Omega$ between Pins 1 and 8 of AD620 to set gain ≈ 1000.
> 4. Power AD620 with ±9 V from a dual regulated supply.
> 5. Connect output (Pin 6) → HPF (0.05 Hz) → LPF (150 Hz) → 50 Hz notch → Oscilloscope/DAQ.
> 6. Observe clean ECG waveform (P-QRS-T) on the display.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a bioamplifier. List any four ideal characteristics that differentiate it from a general-purpose amplifier.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A bioamplifier is a precision differential amplifier used to detect and amplify low-amplitude biopotential signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) originating from living tissues, while ensuring patient safety.

Four ideal characteristics:
1. **High CMRR (≥ 80 dB)** — to reject 50 Hz mains and electrode common-mode noise.
2. **High input impedance (≥ 10 MΩ)** — to prevent loading of the high-impedance electrode-tissue interface.
3. **Low input-referred noise (≤ 5 μV RMS)** — to preserve sub-millivolt signals.
4. **Galvanic isolation** — to electrically separate the patient from mains-powered recording equipment, limiting leakage current to ≤ 10 μA (IEC 60601-1).
*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 × 4 for characteristics = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why is a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier preferred over a simple 1-op-amp difference amplifier for ECG signal conditioning?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. In a simple difference amplifier, the input impedance is **finite and asymmetric** (set by the input resistors), causing **uneven loading** of the two electrode signals and **degradation of CMRR** (1 mark).
2. The 3-op-amp IA uses two **non-inverting buffers** (A1, A2) as input stages, presenting **equal and very high input impedance (GΩ level)** on both inputs (1 mark).
3. It also provides **single-resistor programmable gain** via $R_G$ without affecting the input impedance or CMRR symmetry (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

(a) Draw and explain the functional block diagram of a complete bioamplifier system used for ECG signal acquisition. Describe the role of each block. (7 marks)

(b) For a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier, derive the expression for the closed-loop differential gain. Given $R_1 = 25\,\mathrm{k\Omega}$, $R_2 = R_3 = 10\,\mathrm{k\Omega}$, calculate the value of $R_G$ required to achieve a gain of 500 V/V. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

The complete bioamplifier system for ECG consists of the following cascaded blocks (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Patient + Electrode Interface (1 mark):** Ag/AgCl surface electrodes transduce ionic currents from the body into electronic voltages. The electrode-skin interface introduces a high source impedance (≈ 50 kΩ at 10 Hz) and a DC half-cell potential (±200 mV).
2. **Instrumentation Amplifier Stage (1 mark):** Provides the primary differential gain (typically 100 – 1000 V/V), very high common-mode rejection, and very high balanced input impedance. (Discuss AD620/INA128 with $R_G$ selected for required gain.)
3. **High-Pass Filter (1 mark):** Removes the DC electrode offset (up to ±300 mV) that would otherwise saturate downstream stages. Cut-off $f_L \approx 0.05$ Hz for ECG.
4. **Low-Pass Filter (1 mark):** Limits the noise bandwidth and prevents aliasing at the ADC. Cut-off $f_H \approx 150$ Hz for ECG.
5. **50 Hz Notch Filter (1 mark):** Specifically rejects power-line interference that survives CMRR rejection. Twin-T or active notch topology with $f_n = 50$ Hz and depth ≥ 30 dB.
6. **Isolation Barrier (1 mark):** Optocoupler (e.g., IL300) or digital isolator (ADuM3150) providing 4 kV galvanic isolation between patient and the mains-powered digital system. Ensures patient leakage current < 10 μA.
7. **Output Driver and ADC/Display (1 mark):** Drives the recording/display device. May include a level shifter to map the bipolar ECG to the 0–5 V ADC range.

---

**(b) Gain Derivation and Calculation (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Refer to the complete derivation in Section 3.1. The key steps are:

- Step 1: $V_{R_G} = V_1 - V_2$ (virtual short).
- Step 2: $I = (V_1 - V_2)/R_G$ (Ohm's law).
- Step 3: $V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) \cdot (1 + 2R_1/R_G)$.
- Step 4: $V_{out} = (R_3/R_2) \cdot (V_{o2} - V_{o1})$ (difference amplifier).
- Step 5: $A_d = (1 + 2R_1/R_G) \cdot (R_3/R_2)$.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $A_d = 500$, $R_1 = 25\,\mathrm{k\Omega}$, $R_2 = R_3 = 10\,\mathrm{k\Omega}$.

Substituting $R_2 = R_3$, the gain simplifies to:

$$A_d = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}$$

$$500 = 1 + \frac{2 \times 25 \times 10^{3}}{R_G}$$

$$R_G = \frac{50 \times 10^{3}}{499} \approx 100.2\,\Omega$$

Nearest standard E12 value: **100 Ω** (giving actual gain ≈ 501 V/V, fully acceptable).

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

(a) Explain the concept of CMRR. Why is high CMRR critical for bioamplifiers? Calculate the CMRR in dB for a bioamplifier with differential gain 1000 and common-mode gain 0.01. (7 marks)

(b) With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of the 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. What happens to the CMRR if the four resistors in the difference amplifier stage are not perfectly matched? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CMRR Concept and Calculation (7 marks):**

**Concept (4 marks):**
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is the ability of a differential amplifier to reject signals that appear **simultaneously and in-phase** on both inputs (common-mode signals) while amplifying the **difference** between the two inputs (differential signals) (1 mark).
- Mathematically: $\mathrm{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_d \vert / \vert A_{cm} \vert)$ where $A_d$ is differential gain and $A_{cm}$ is common-mode gain (1 mark).
- **Why critical for bioamplifiers:** The 50 Hz power-line interference, mains-borne hum, and electrode DC half-cell potentials all appear as **common-mode** voltages (identical on both inputs) when measured with a differential electrode pair. A high CMRR ensures these unwanted signals are rejected and do not contaminate the tiny ECG/EEG/EMG signal (1 mark).
- Typical CMRR requirements: ≥ 80 dB for general ECG, ≥ 100 dB for high-precision EEG/EMG (1 mark).

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $A_d = 1000$, $A_{cm} = 0.01$.

$$\mathrm{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1000}{0.01}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10^{5})$$

$$\boxed{\mathrm{CMRR} = 100\,\mathrm{dB}}$$

This is a clinically excellent CMRR — meets the high-end EEG specification.

---

**(b) Circuit and Mismatch Analysis (7 marks):**

**3-Op-Amp IA Circuit (4 marks):** (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2)
- Two input op-amps (A1, A2) configured as non-inverting amplifiers with a shared gain resistor $R_G$ between their inverting inputs. This provides the first-stage gain of $(1 + 2R_1/R_G)$ and presents very high, balanced input impedance.
- The third op-amp (A3) is a unity-gain (or scaled) difference amplifier that subtracts the two buffered outputs, cancels the common-mode voltage, and produces a single-ended output.
- Resistor matching: $R_2 = R_2'$ and $R_3 = R_3'$ is critical for symmetry (1 mark for circuit, 2 marks for operation, 1 mark for matching requirement).

**Mismatch Effect on CMRR (3 marks):**
- If $R_2 \neq R_2'$ or $R_3 \neq R_3'$, the difference amplifier no longer perfectly cancels the common-mode voltage (1 mark).
- The residual common-mode gain is $A_{cm} \approx (\Delta R_3 - \Delta R_2)/(2R)$ (1 mark).
- Even a **0.1 % resistor mismatch** (typical for standard metal-film resistors) limits the practical CMRR to ≈ 66 dB. For CMRR > 100 dB, **integrated INA ICs** (AD620, INA128) with laser-trimmed internal resistors are mandatory (1 mark).

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Bioamplifier questions:**
> 1. **Confusing CMRR formula** — writing $\mathrm{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d)$ instead of $20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$. This is the **#1 most common mistake**. Always include the common-mode gain in the denominator.
> 2. **Forgetting the input-impedance justification** — saying "an IA has high gain" is not enough; you MUST explain *why* the buffered input topology gives high input impedance.
> 3. **Skipping patient safety/isolation** — any bioamplifier answer that omits galvanic isolation is **deducted 2 marks** by the KTU board under the "biomedical context" rubric.
> 4. **Writing the wrong formula for AD620 gain** — remember $A_d = 1 + 49.4\,\mathrm{k\Omega}/R_G$, not $1 + R_1/R_G$.
> 5. **Not stating the units** — for CMRR in dB, always write "dB" explicitly. Writing just "100" without "dB" loses the unit mark.
> 6. **No frequency band mentioned** — always specify the passband (e.g., 0.05–150 Hz for ECG) in any bioamplifier design.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bioamplifier in 60 seconds:**

- **Definition:** Precision differential amp for low-amplitude bioelectric signals.
- **Core formula:** $A_d = (1 + 2R_1/R_G) \cdot (R_3/R_2)$ for 3-op-amp IA.
- **AD620 shortcut:** $A_d \approx 1 + 49.4\,\mathrm{k\Omega}/R_G$.
- **CMRR formula:** $20 \log_{10}(\vert A_d/A_{cm} \vert)$ in dB. CMRR ≥ 80 dB is clinical minimum.
- **Six must-have specs:** High gain (100–10 000), high CMRR (≥ 80 dB), high $Z_{in}$ (≥ 10 MΩ), low noise (≤ 5 μV), proper BW (ECG: 0.05–150 Hz), galvanic isolation.
- **Why 3-op-amp IA?** Symmetric, very high input impedance on both inputs — essential for balanced electrode sources.
- **Why 50 Hz notch?** Surviving 50 Hz mains interference after CMRR rejection is the #1 noise source in real-world ECG/EEG.
- **ECG signal:** 0.5–5 mV, 0.05–150 Hz, 3 electrodes (RA/LA/LL) + Driven Right Leg (DRL) for improved common-mode rejection.
- **Safety:** Patient leakage current ≤ 10 μA (Type CF, IEC 60601-1).
- **Lab ICs to know:** AD620, INA128, INA333, ADS1299, ISO124.
- **Common pitfall:** Forgetting to include $A_{cm}$ in the CMRR denominator. **Don't lose that 1 mark!**
- **KTU favourite MCQ trick:** "The 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier improves CMRR mainly by: balanced high input impedance / common-mode feedback / single resistor gain setting / differential output." → Correct answer: **balanced high input impedance + symmetric resistor matching**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Notch Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Notch Filter # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: NOTCH FILTER ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Notch Filter** (also formally known as a **Band-Stop Filter (BSF)** or **Band-Reject Filter**) is a second-order electronic filter engineered to highly attenuate (reject) a very narrow, specific band of frequencies while allowing frequencies outside that narrow band to pass through with minimal attenuation. In the context of biomedical signal processing under the KTU 2024 Scheme, a notch filter is typically tuned to a center frequency of **50 Hz** to suppress power-line interference (PLI) contaminating bioelectric signals such as the **ECG (Electrocardiogram)**, **EEG (Electroencephalogram)**, and **EMG (Electromyogram)**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A "notch" specifically refers to a sharp, deep, narrow downward spike (infinite or near-infinite attenuation) in the magnitude frequency response plot at the notch frequency (f₀). Unlike a wide band-stop filter, a notch filter's quality factor (Q) is intentionally kept high. The ideal magnitude response $|H(j\omega)|$ of a notch filter is characterized by: - **Pass-band gain** = 0 dB (unity) for all frequencies except $f_0$ - **Stop-band attenuation** = $-\infty$ dB (complete rejection) at $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 50 Hz?** In India (and the Kerala state grid), the AC mains power supply operates at a fundamental frequency of **50 Hz**. Because the human body acts as a large antenna and electrode cables act as capacitive pickup, this 50 Hz noise is capacitively/inductively coupled into delicate bioelectric signals (which themselves are in the millivolt/microvolt range). The notch filter surgically removes this single tone. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are listening to a beautiful orchestra playing in a large hall. Suddenly, a single child in the audience starts whining a constant, loud, monotonous note ("Aaaaaa..."). You cannot simply turn down the volume, because then you would also lose the soft violins and flutes you want to hear. What you need is a surgical tool that listens to the room and says: *"Whenever you hear EXACTLY this one annoying pitch, kill it. For every other pitch, pass it through completely untouched."* That surgical tool is the **Notch Filter**. In the biomedical lab, the "annoying pitch" is almost always the **50 Hz mains hum**. - **Pass-band** = Orchestra music (the ECG/EEG waveform) - **Stop-band** = The annoying child's whine (the 50 Hz power-line interference) - **Q factor** = How precisely the filter can identify and kill ONLY that pitch (high Q = narrow notch = surgical precision) > [!TIP] > Think of the magnitude response graph as a landscape: the pass-band is flat ground, and the notch filter carves out a single, deep, narrow well (the "notch") at exactly the unwanted frequency. --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted) The three universally critical performance parameters of a notch filter in PCBML408 lab evaluations are: - **Center / Notch Frequency (f₀ or ω₀):** The exact frequency that is rejected. Formula: $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ for the standard Twin-T topology. - **Quality Factor (Q):** A dimensionless number that defines the *sharpness* of the notch. A high Q produces a very narrow, deep notch. Formula: $Q = \frac{f_0}{BW}$ where $BW$ is the **-3 dB bandwidth**. - **Notch Depth / Attenuation:** Measured in **dB**. A perfect notch has infinite depth. A practical op-amp-based active notch achieves **40 dB to 60 dB** of attenuation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Twin-T (Parallel-T) Notch Filter — Operational Theory The most commonly implemented, KTU-favored notch filter in PCBML408 is the **Twin-T Notch Filter**, also called the **Parallel-T Network**. It is built from only **two T-networks** placed in parallel: 1. A **Low-Pass T-Network** (series R-R, shunt C/2 to ground) 2. A **High-Pass T-Network** (series C-C, shunt 2R to ground) ### Why Twin-T? - A simple passive RC low-pass filter can attenuate, but **never completely reject** a single frequency. - A simple passive RC high-pass filter can also attenuate, but **never completely reject** a single frequency. - When we *add* their outputs in a clever parallel arrangement, the two filters' responses **sum to zero at exactly one specific frequency** (f₀). At this "magic" frequency, the output of the low-pass arm exactly cancels the output of the high-pass arm, producing the notch. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Cancellation Principle:** At $f_0$, the phase shift of the low-pass arm and the phase shift of the high-pass arm differ by exactly 180°, AND their magnitudes are equal. This produces complete destructive interference — the notch. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic - **Step 1 — Input Signal Splitting:** The input signal $V_{in}$ is fed simultaneously to the *Low-Pass T-Arm* and the *High-Pass T-Arm*. - **Step 2 — Low-Pass Arm Response:** The series R-R resistors and the shunt capacitor (C/2) form a second-order low-pass filter. As frequency rises, its output magnitude decreases at -12 dB/octave beyond the cutoff. - **Step 3 — High-Pass Arm Response:** The series C-C capacitors and the shunt resistor (2R) form a second-order high-pass filter. As frequency falls, its output magnitude decreases at -12 dB/octave below the cutoff. - **Step 4 — Summation at the Output Node:** The two arms are wired in parallel to a common output node. At $f_0$, the low-pass arm current and high-pass arm current are equal in magnitude and opposite in phase → they cancel. - **Step 5 — Op-Amp Buffering (Active Version):** The passive Twin-T network has a high output impedance and very poor Q factor (typically Q < 0.5). To make it practical, a **non-inverting op-amp follower** with positive feedback is added. The feedback resistor network boosts Q from ~0.5 to practically **5, 10, or even 50**, producing a deep, narrow notch. - **Step 6 — Final Output:** Frequencies far from $f_0$ pass with unity gain; only $f_0$ is rejected. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields - **Biomedical Instrumentation (Primary KTU Context):** Removing 50/60 Hz mains interference from ECG, EEG, EMG, and EOG recordings. - **Audio Engineering:** Eliminating the 50/60 Hz hum from guitar pickups, microphones, and PA systems. - **Telecommunications:** Suppressing single-tone interference from carriers or image frequencies in superheterodyne receivers. - **Instrumentation & Control:** Rejecting a known, fixed mechanical resonance frequency in a servo system. - **Power Electronics:** Removing specific switching harmonics from power line signals. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Notch / Center Frequency | $f_0$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | **Hz** | For Twin-T with R, R, 2R and C, C, C/2 | | Angular Notch Frequency | $\omega_0$ | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | **rad/s** | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ | | Quality Factor | $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW_{-3dB}}$ | Dimensionless | Higher Q = Narrower notch | | Q (Active Twin-T with feedback) | $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{1}{4(1 - K)}$ | Dimensionless | Where $K$ is feedback fraction (K < 1) | | Notch Depth | $A_{notch}$ | $20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)\Big\vert_{f=f_0}$ | **dB** | Practical: -40 dB to -60 dB | | Pass-band Gain | $A_p$ | $A_p = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | V/V | For non-inverting op-amp topology | | -3 dB Bandwidth | $BW$ | $BW = f_2 - f_1$ | **Hz** | Where gain drops to 0.707 of peak | | Low-Pass Arm Cutoff | $f_{LP}$ | $f_{LP} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | **Hz** | One pole of the network | | High-Pass Arm Cutoff | $f_{HP}$ | $f_{HP} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | **Hz** | Other pole of the network | | Op-Amp Selection Criterion | Slew Rate | $SR > 2\pi f_0 \cdot V_{peak}$ | **V/µs** | For undistorted 50 Hz output | > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly assume $f_0$ depends on all six components. In a *symmetric* Twin-T (R-R-2R and C-C-C/2), the formula simplifies beautifully to $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$, depending on only **one** R and **one** C. The 2R and C/2 components are matched ratios for proper cancellation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Function of the Passive Twin-T Notch Filter We will derive the transfer function $H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)}$ for the passive Twin-T network. Let us label the components as follows for the symmetric case: - **Low-Pass Arm:** Two series resistors of value $R$, with a shunt capacitor of value $C/2$ to ground. - **High-Pass Arm:** Two series capacitors of value $C$, with a shunt resistor of value $2R$ to ground. ### Step 1: Identify the Two T-Networks and their Individual Transfer Functions **Low-Pass Arm Transfer Function ($H_{LP}(s)$):** Using voltage divider analysis on the series R-R, shunt C/2 network: $$ H_{LP}(s) = \frac{\frac{1}{sC/2}}{R + R + \frac{1}{sC/2}} = \frac{\frac{2}{sC}}{2R + \frac{2}{sC}} $$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $sC/2$: $$ H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC} $$ > *Logic: This is the standard first-order LPF response, as expected.* **High-Pass Arm Transfer Function ($H_{HP}(s)$):** Using voltage divider analysis on the series C-C, shunt 2R network: $$ H_{HP}(s) = \frac{2R}{2R + \frac{1}{sC/1} + \frac{1}{sC/1}} = \frac{2R}{2R + \frac{2}{sC}} $$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $sC/2$: $$ H_{HP}(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC} $$ > *Logic: This is the standard first-order HPF response, as expected.* ### Step 2: Combine the Two Arms in Parallel (Admittance Summation) The two T-networks are connected in parallel between $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$. Their **admittances** add: $$ Y_{total} = Y_{LP} + Y_{HP} $$ The transfer function of a parallel network is the admittance ratio: $$ H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{Y_{LP}}{Y_{LP} + Y_{HP}} $$ Substituting the admittance forms $Y_{LP} = \frac{1}{Z_{LP}}$ and $Y_{HP} = \frac{1}{Z_{HP}}$: $$ H(s) = \frac{\frac{1}{Z_{LP}}}{\frac{1}{Z_{LP}} + \frac{1}{Z_{HP}}} = \frac{Z_{HP}}{Z_{LP} + Z_{HP}} $$ This is equivalent to the standard parallel combination formula for the output node. ### Step 3: Build the Full Expressions Let us define the impedances of each arm as seen from the output node: **Low-Pass Arm Impedance** (from output to ground, with input shorted to ground for analysis): $$ Z_{LP}(s) = \frac{(R + R) \cdot \frac{1}{sC/2}}{R + R + \frac{1}{sC/2}} = \frac{\frac{2R}{sC/2}}{2R + \frac{2}{sC}} = \frac{1}{sC(1 + sRC)} $$ **High-Pass Arm Impedance** (from output to ground, with input shorted): $$ Z_{HP}(s) = \frac{\left(\frac{1}{sC} + \frac{1}{sC}\right) \cdot 2R}{\frac{1}{sC} + \frac{1}{sC} + 2R} = \frac{\frac{4R}{sC}}{2R + \frac{2}{sC}} = \frac{2R}{1 + sRC} $$ ### Step 4: Compute $H(s)$ via the Voltage Divider Form Using the parallel-admittance equivalent — both arms draw current from the source. The transfer function for the unloaded network is given by: $$ H(s) = \frac{1 + (sRC)^2}{1 + 4sRC + (sRC)^2} $$ **Derivation detail:** Multiply numerator and denominator of the parallel form: $$ H(s) = \frac{Z_{HP}}{Z_{LP} + Z_{HP}} = \frac{\frac{2R}{1+sRC}}{\frac{1}{sC(1+sRC)} + \frac{2R}{1+sRC}} $$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $sC(1+sRC)$: $$ H(s) = \frac{2R \cdot sC}{1 + 2R \cdot sC \cdot sC} = \frac{2R sC}{1 + 2RC \cdot s} \ \ \text{(intermediate, needs full expansion)} $$ Carrying the full multiplication to standard form: $$ H(s) = \frac{1 + (sRC)^2}{1 + 4(sRC) + (sRC)^2} $$ ### Step 5: Identify Notch Frequency and Q This is the **canonical biquadratic notch transfer function**. Comparing to the standard form: $$ H(s) = \frac{s^2 + \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \frac{\omega_0}{Q}s + \omega_0^2} $$ We immediately extract: $$ \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} $$ $$ \frac{\omega_0}{Q} = \frac{4}{RC} \cdot \frac{1}{RC} \ \text{(re-arranging)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{passive} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Result:** The passive Twin-T notch filter has a maximum theoretical Q-factor of only **0.25**. This means the notch is extremely broad and shallow — practically useless for biomedical 50 Hz rejection. **This is precisely why we add an op-amp with positive feedback** to boost the Q to clinically useful values (5–50). ## 3.2 Derivation: Active Notch Filter with Op-Amp Positive Feedback The active version uses the passive Twin-T feeding a non-inverting op-amp. Positive feedback through a voltage divider (R_f, R_g) feeds back a portion of the output to the *common node* of the Twin-T, which dramatically sharpens the notch. The new Q becomes: $$ Q_{active} = \frac{1}{4(1 - K)} $$ Where $K$ is the feedback fraction: $$ K = \frac{R_g}{R_f + R_g} $$ > *Note: $K$ must be strictly less than 1, otherwise the circuit oscillates. A typical safe value is $K = 0.95$, yielding $Q = 5$.* ### Stability Check (Barkhausen Criterion) — Why $K < 1$ is Mandatory: For oscillation, the loop gain $A\beta \ge 1$. The positive feedback loop provides a gain that grows as $K \to 1$. At $K = 1$, the loop gain reaches unity exactly at $\omega_0$, and the circuit becomes a **sinusoidal oscillator** at $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. > [!WARNING] > **Common Lab Mistake:** Setting $K$ too close to 1 (e.g., using a potentiometer turned all the way up) causes the op-amp to oscillate at 50 Hz, producing a 50 Hz sine wave at the output. KTU examiners specifically test whether students can troubleshoot this. ## 3.3 Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem Style) **Given:** A Twin-T notch filter is designed with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 318 \text{ nF}$. Find: 1. The notch frequency $f_0$ 2. The passive Q-factor 3. The required feedback fraction $K$ to achieve an active Q of 10 **Solution 1 — Notch Frequency:** $$ f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 318 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.18 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{0.01998} \approx 50.05 \text{ Hz} $$ **Solution 2 — Passive Q:** $$ Q_{passive} = 0.25 $$ **Solution 3 — Feedback fraction for Q = 10:** $$ Q = \frac{1}{4(1 - K)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - K = \frac{1}{4Q} = \frac{1}{40} = 0.025 $$ $$ K = 1 - 0.025 = 0.975 $$ Thus, the feedback network must deliver **97.5%** of the output back to the Twin-T's common node. In practice, a 1 kΩ potentiometer is used with $R_f = 39 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_g = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ to make $K$ adjustable. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Active Twin-T Notch Filter > [!NOTE] > *The mermaid diagram below maps the signal flow and functional interactions of the active Twin-T notch filter. It is rendered as a block-level functional architecture since physical circuit drawings are not supported in this format.* ```mermaid flowchart LR inputNode["Vin INPUT SIGNAL"]:::inputStyle lpfArm["LOW PASS T ARM
R R C by 2"]:::armStyle hpfArm["HIGH PASS T ARM
C C 2R"]:::armStyle commonNode["COMMON OUTPUT NODE"]:::nodeStyle opamp["OP AMP
NON INVERTING
BUFFER AMPLIFIER"]:::opampStyle feedbackNet["POSITIVE FEEDBACK
RESISTOR DIVIDER
Rf Rg"]:::feedbackStyle outputNode["Vout FILTERED SIGNAL"]:::outputStyle inputNode --> lpfArm inputNode --> hpfArm lpfArm --> commonNode hpfArm --> commonNode commonNode --> opamp opamp --> outputNode outputNode --> feedbackNet feedbackNet --> commonNode classDef inputStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef armStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef nodeStyle fill:#FF6347,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef opampStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef feedbackStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Frequency Response Characterization (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TD f0["NOTCH FREQUENCY F0
50 HZ"]:::notchStyle passLow["PASS BAND
BELOW 49 HZ
GAIN APPROX 0 DB"]:::passStyle passHigh["PASS BAND
ABOVE 51 HZ
GAIN APPROX 0 DB"]:::passStyle steepLeft["ROLL OFF
12 DB PER OCTAVE"]:::rollStyle steepRight["ROLL OFF
12 DB PER OCTAVE"]:::rollStyle floor["NOTCH DEPTH
MINUS 40 TO 60 DB"]:::floorStyle passLow --> steepLeft steepLeft --> f0 f0 --> steepRight steepRight --> passHigh f0 -.-> floor classDef notchStyle fill:#FF0000,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef passStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef rollStyle fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef floorStyle fill:#8B0000,stroke:#FF0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture Explanation | **Block** | **Function** | **Key Specification** | |---|---|---| | **Vin Input Stage** | Receives the noisy biomedical signal (e.g., ECG with 50 Hz hum) | Input impedance > 10 MΩ preferred | | **Low-Pass T-Arm** | Passes frequencies BELOW f₀, attenuates frequencies ABOVE f₀ | Time constant = $RC$ | | **High-Pass T-Arm** | Passes frequencies ABOVE f₀, attenuates frequencies BELOW f₀ | Time constant = $RC$ | | **Common Output Node** | Sums currents from both arms; currents cancel at exactly f₀ | Critical node — high impedance | | **Op-Amp Buffer** | Provides low output impedance, isolates the Twin-T from load, and applies positive feedback to boost Q | Slew rate > 0.5 V/µs (e.g., µA741, LM358) | | **Positive Feedback Divider** | Returns a controlled fraction K of Vout to the common node, increasing Q | $K$ is a potentiometer for tunable Q | | **Vout Output Stage** | Delivers the clean, 50-Hz-free signal to the next stage (e.g., ADC, display) | Output impedance < 100 Ω | > [!TIP] > **Lab Tip for PCBML408 Viva:** When asked "What happens if the op-amp is removed?", answer: *"The notch becomes extremely shallow (Q = 0.25) and the filter is practically useless for biomedical applications, because the un-buffered Twin-T is highly sensitive to load impedance and the notch depth rarely exceeds 10 dB."* # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PCBML408]** > Define a notch filter. Why is it called a "notch" filter and not a "band-stop" filter? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A notch filter is a second-order band-stop filter designed to reject a very narrow band of frequencies while passing all other frequencies with unity gain **(1 mark)**. It is called a "notch" filter because, when plotted on a magnitude-vs-frequency graph, the response curve has a sharp, deep, narrow downward spike — resembling a mechanical notch cut into the otherwise flat frequency response curve **(1 mark)**. In contrast, a generic band-stop filter attenuates a wide range of frequencies; a notch filter's bandwidth is intentionally made very narrow (high Q factor), hence the special name **(1 mark)**. --- ### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PCBML408 Lab Viva Compilation]** > State the formula for the notch frequency of a Twin-T filter and explain why a symmetric Twin-T uses component values $R, R, 2R$ and $C, C, C/2$ instead of all equal $R$ and $C$. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The notch frequency is $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ **(1 mark)**. The asymmetric values (2R and C/2) are required to ensure that at $f_0$, the low-pass arm and the high-pass arm have **equal magnitudes** of transfer function. If all components were equal, the two arms would have different time constants and the cancellation at $f_0$ would be incomplete, resulting in a very shallow notch **(1 mark)**. The factor of 2 between the arms balances the impedance scaling so that $\vert H_{LP}(j\omega_0) \vert = \vert H_{HP}(j\omega_0) \vert$ exactly, achieving the deepest possible notch for a passive network **(1 mark)**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry 14 marks. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (Module Internal Choice). Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each. --- ### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper PCBML408]` **A(a).** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an **active Twin-T notch filter** using an op-amp. Derive its transfer function and hence show that the notch frequency is $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):** The active Twin-T notch filter consists of: - A **passive Twin-T network** with the low-pass arm (R₁ = R, R₂ = R, C₁ = C/2) and the high-pass arm (C₂ = C, C₃ = C, R₃ = 2R). - An op-amp (typically µA741) connected as a **non-inverting amplifier** with feedback resistors R_f and R_g. - The output of the Twin-T feeds the non-inverting (+) input; the inverting (−) input receives the feedback voltage divider output. **Step 2 — Transfer Function Derivation (3 marks):** Using the parallel admittance method derived in Section 3.1: $$ H_{passive}(s) = \frac{1 + (sRC)^2}{1 + 4sRC + (sRC)^2} $$ With op-amp positive feedback through a fraction $K = \frac{R_g}{R_f + R_g}$, the effective Q becomes: $$ Q_{active} = \frac{1}{4(1 - K)} $$ **Step 3 — Notch Frequency Extraction (2 marks):** Setting the numerator of $H_{passive}(s)$ to zero: $$ 1 + (sRC)^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \pm j \frac{1}{RC} $$ Therefore: $$ \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \quad \textbf{(Q.E.D.)} $$ --- **A(b).** Design an active Twin-T notch filter to reject exactly **50 Hz** mains interference. Choose $C = 100 \text{ nF}$ and find $R$. If the desired $Q$-factor is 20, find the feedback fraction $K$ and the value of $R_g$ if $R_f = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate R (2 marks):** $$ R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ R = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 31.83 \text{ k}\Omega $$ A standard 33 kΩ resistor is used in practice. **Step 2 — Find K for Q = 20 (2 marks):** $$ Q = \frac{1}{4(1 - K)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - K = \frac{1}{4 \times 20} = 0.0125 $$ $$ K = 1 - 0.0125 = 0.9875 $$ **Step 3 — Find R_g (3 marks):** $$ K = \frac{R_g}{R_f + R_g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.9875 = \frac{R_g}{100{,}000 + R_g} $$ $$ 0.9875(100{,}000 + R_g) = R_g $$ $$ 98{,}750 + 0.9875 R_g = R_g $$ $$ 98{,}750 = R_g(1 - 0.9875) = 0.0125 \cdot R_g $$ $$ R_g = \frac{98{,}750}{0.0125} = 7.9 \text{ M}\Omega $$ > **Practical Lab Note (Bonus 0 mark – for context):** A 7.9 MΩ resistor is very large and noise-sensitive. In practice, students use a **potentiometer** in place of R_g to allow fine adjustment, and values of $K$ closer to 0.95–0.98 are typical in undergraduate labs (yielding Q ≈ 5–12). --- ### **Question B — Option 2 / Internal Choice (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper PCBML408]` **B(a).** Compare the **passive Twin-T notch filter** and the **active Twin-T notch filter** in terms of Q-factor, notch depth, output impedance, and load sensitivity. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):** | **Parameter** | **Passive Twin-T** | **Active Twin-T** | |---|---|---| | Q-factor | Very low ($Q \le 0.25$) | High ($Q = 5$ to $50$, tunable) | | Notch depth | Shallow (≈ -10 to -20 dB) | Deep (≈ -40 to -60 dB) | | Output impedance | High (kilohms) | Very low (≈ 0 Ω, due to op-amp buffer) | | Load sensitivity | Highly sensitive to load | Insensitive (op-amp isolates) | | Tunability | Fixed by passive R, C | Q is tunable via feedback fraction K | | Component count | 6 (R, R, 2R, C, C, C/2) | 6 + 1 op-amp + 2 feedback resistors | | Application | Rarely used standalone | Standard for biomedical 50 Hz rejection | **Verdict (1 mark):** The active version is overwhelmingly preferred in biomedical instrumentation due to its deep notch, low output impedance, and adjustable Q. --- **B(b).** A biomedical ECG signal is contaminated with strong **50 Hz power-line interference** and weak ECG features. Explain with a block diagram how you would use a notch filter to clean the signal, and justify why a **high-Q notch filter** is essential rather than a low-Q one. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — System Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` ECG Electrodes → Instrumentation Amplifier (INA128) → Active Notch Filter (50 Hz, Q=20) → Post-Amplifier → ADC / Display ``` **Step 2 — Why 50 Hz is the target (1 mark):** Indian power mains operate at 50 Hz. This single tone couples capacitively into long electrode cables, dominating the ECG spectrum at the exact frequency of the unwanted noise. **Step 3 — Why a high-Q notch is essential (3 marks):** - The **QRS complex** of an ECG has spectral components in the range of 10 Hz to 40 Hz, very close to 50 Hz. - A **low-Q notch** (broad notch, e.g., Q = 0.5) would have a bandwidth of 100 Hz, which would also attenuate legitimate ECG components near 50 Hz, distorting the waveform and potentially hiding pathological features (e.g., ST-segment changes indicating myocardial infarction). - A **high-Q notch** (Q = 20) has a bandwidth of only 2.5 Hz, meaning it surgically removes only 48.75 Hz to 51.25 Hz, leaving the rest of the ECG spectrum untouched. This preserves diagnostic fidelity. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:** > 1. **Confusing Notch and Band-Stop:** Students often write $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$ (which is the *band-pass* formula) for a notch filter. The notch filter is a **Twin-T RC network**, not an LC circuit. The formula is $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. Using the LC formula will cost you 2 marks. > 2. **Ignoring Component Ratios:** Writing $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ without mentioning the 2R and C/2 ratios loses 1 mark. The examiner expects the explanation of *why* those asymmetric values exist. > 3. **Forgetting Units:** Always carry units in the numerator and denominator. Writing $R = 31.83 \text{ k}\Omega$ is correct; writing $R = 31.83$ is penalized. > 4. **Confusing Q and Bandwidth:** $Q = \frac{f_0}{BW}$ — students sometimes invert this. Memorize: $BW = \frac{f_0}{Q}$. Higher Q means lower bandwidth (narrower notch). > 5. **Stability of Active Notch:** Forgetting the condition $K < 1$ loses marks. The examiner loves to ask "What happens if K ≥ 1?" (Answer: oscillation at $f_0$). > 6. **PCBML408 Lab Practical Pitfall:** In the actual lab, students often wire the 2R resistor and the C/2 capacitor into the *wrong arms*. Double-check: the **shunt** element of the low-pass arm is the capacitor, and the **shunt** element of the high-pass arm is the resistor. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Before the Exam** - **Definition:** A notch filter is a narrow band-reject filter. In PCBML408, it specifically targets **50 Hz mains hum** in bioelectric signals (ECG, EEG, EMG). - **Standard Topology:** **Twin-T (Parallel-T)** — composed of a low-pass T-arm (R-R-C/2) and a high-pass T-arm (C-C-2R) in parallel. - **Notch Frequency Formula:** $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — depends on only **one** R and **one** C. - **Passive Twin-T Q-factor:** $Q = 0.25$ — too low for practical use. - **Active Twin-T Q-factor:** $Q = \frac{1}{4(1-K)}$, where $K = \frac{R_g}{R_f + R_g}$ is the positive feedback fraction. **K must be < 1** to avoid oscillation. - **Transfer Function (Passive):** $H(s) = \frac{1 + (sRC)^2}{1 + 4sRC + (sRC)^2}$ — a canonical biquadratic notch response. - **Active Q Boost:** Achieved by a non-inverting op-amp with positive feedback from output to the common node of the Twin-T. - **Practical Op-Amp Choice:** µA741, LM358, TL081. Slew rate requirement: $SR > 2\pi f_0 V_{peak}$. - **Design Procedure:** Pick $C$ (e.g., 100 nF) → compute $R$ for 50 Hz → choose $K$ for desired $Q$ → compute $R_g$ from $R_f$. - **Lab Viva Essentials:** - Why Twin-T and not just RC LPF/HPF? → Cancellation principle. - Why 2R and C/2? → Magnitude balance for perfect cancellation. - Why op-amp? → Boosts Q and provides low output impedance. - What if $K = 1$? → The circuit oscillates at 50 Hz. - Notch filter vs. high-pass filter? → Notch rejects a *narrow* band; HPF rejects *all* frequencies below cutoff. - **Biomedical Significance:** Removing 50 Hz interference is the *most common* signal conditioning task in ECG/EEG labs, making the notch filter an essential building block of every biomedical instrument. - **Trade-off Reminder:** Very high Q (e.g., > 50) makes the notch extremely narrow but also makes it sensitive to component tolerance and temperature drift. A practical Q of 5–20 is the sweet spot for clinical ECG. - **Always check the magnitude response graph** — the notch should appear as a deep, narrow "V" at exactly 50 Hz, with the rest of the curve flat at 0 dB.

topics.title: Phase Detector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Phase Detector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Phase Detector (PD)** is a non-linear electronic subsystem that accepts two periodic input signals of nominally equal frequency — a **Reference Signal** $v_1(t)$ and a **Comparator/Feedback Signal** $v_2(t)$ — and produces an output signal whose **average (DC) value is a well-defined, single-valued function of the instantaneous phase difference** $\Delta\phi$ between them.

Mathematically, the ideal phase detector enforces:

$$\overline{v_{out}(t)} = f(\Delta\phi)$$

where $\overline{v_{out}(t)}$ denotes the time-averaged (DC) component of the output, and $f(\cdot)$ is the detector's **characteristic function** (cosine for a multiplier, triangle for an XOR, sawtooth for a sequential/flip-flop detector).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Emphasis:** In the Biomedical Electronics Lab, the Phase Detector experiment is performed using the **Analog Multiplier IC AD633 / AD534** and/or the **CD4030 / CD4070 XOR gate**, and the student is expected to plot the transfer characteristic $V_{DC}$ vs. $\Delta\phi$ for both implementations and compare them with the theoretical curves.

## 1.2 Types of Phase Detectors Encountered in the Lab

| # | Type | Core Element | Characteristic Shape | Application |
|---|------|--------------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Multiplier (Analog) PD** | AD633 / AD534 | $V_{DC} = K\cos(\Delta\phi)$ | Lock-in amplifiers, coherent biomedical demodulation |
| 2 | **XOR Gate PD** | CD4030 / CD4070 | Triangle wave ($0 \to V_{CC}$) | Digital PLLs, frequency synthesis |
| 3 | **Phase-Frequency Detector (PFD)** | CD4046 Type-II | Tri-state ($-V_{CC}, 0, +V_{CC}$) | Wide-range PLLs, FSK demodulators |
| 4 | **Sample-and-Hold PD** | LF398 + sampler | $\sin(\Delta\phi)$ | Carrier recovery, Doppler ultrasound |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Two-Metronome Race"**
>
> Imagine two mechanical metronomes ticking at the *same nominal rate* (same frequency). A **Phase Detector is like a referee** standing between them, holding a **stopwatch** and a **scoreboard**:
>
> - When metronome-2 ticks *exactly* when metronome-1 does → the referee writes **0** (in-phase, $\Delta\phi = 0$).
> - When metronome-2 ticks *late* by a quarter of a beat → the referee writes **+90°** on the scoreboard.
> - When metronome-2 ticks *exactly opposite* to metronome-1 → the scoreboard reads **180°** (anti-phase).
>
> The "score" (DC voltage) on the scoreboard is the **referee's continuous measurement of timing difference**. In a PLL, this score is fed back to *slow down or speed up* metronome-2 until both tick together — that is **phase locking**.
>
> In **biomedical electronics**, this "referee" extracts buried signals from noise, e.g., recovering the **fetal heart-rate Doppler shift** or the **respiration-modulated phase** of a reflected ultrasound beam.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Board-Expectation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three quantities the examiner **will test**:
>
> 1. **Phase Detector Gain** $K_{PD}$ — units of **V/rad**, slope of the characteristic at the lock point.
> 2. **Lock Range** — span of $\Delta\phi$ over which the detector produces a *monotonic* output (typically $-\pi/2$ to $+\pi/2$ for a multiplier).
> 3. **Linearity Error** — deviation of the measured $V_{DC}$ vs. $\Delta\phi$ plot from the theoretical cosine/triangle curve, usually expressed in **%** of full-scale deflection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Multiplier-Based Phase Detector

The **Analog Multiplier** (AD633, AD534, MC1495) is the most widely used PD in biomedical instrumentation because it preserves **amplitude information** and is mathematically tractable.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. Two sinusoids of the *same frequency* $\omega$ but with a *phase offset* $\phi$ are applied to the multiplier's two input ports:

$$v_1(t) = V_A \cos(\omega t) \quad \text{and} \quad v_2(t) = V_B \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

2. The multiplier computes the *instantaneous product* scaled by a transfer constant $K$ (typically $K = 0.1\ \text{V}^{-1}$ for AD633, $K = 1/10\ \text{V}^{-1}$):

$$v_{out}(t) = K \cdot v_1(t) \cdot v_2(t)$$

3. The product contains a **low-frequency (DC) term** proportional to $\cos\phi$ and a **high-frequency term** at $2\omega$, which is removed by a **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)**.

4. The remaining DC voltage is the **phase detector output** that drives the PLL or is read by the ADC.

5. The LPF cutoff $f_c$ must satisfy: $f_c \ll 2f$ (where $f = \omega / 2\pi$ is the input signal frequency) to suppress the $2\omega$ ripple to below the ADC's least-significant bit.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — XOR Gate Phase Detector

When the two inputs $A$ and $B$ are square waves of *equal frequency*:

- $A \oplus B = 0$ (LOW) when both are in the **same logic state** (in-phase region).
- $A \oplus B = 1$ (HIGH) when the two are in **opposite states** (out-of-phase region).

As the phase shift $\phi$ between them increases from $0$ to $\pi$:

- The **duty cycle** of the XOR output grows linearly from 0 % to 100 %.
- Hence the **average (DC) value** grows linearly from $0$ to $V_{CC}$.

This gives the characteristic **triangle wave** shape, in stark contrast to the cosine shape of the multiplier.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. Every line is a potential 1- or 2-mark sub-question in the university exam.**

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units | Range / Boundary |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Multiplier PD: instantaneous product | $v_{out}(t) = K \cdot V_A V_B \cos(\omega t)\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | V | Continuous time |
| 2 | DC component after LPF | $V_{DC} = \dfrac{K V_A V_B}{2}\cos(\phi)$ | V | $-\pi \le \phi \le +\pi$ |
| 3 | Multiplier PD gain at lock | $K_{PD} = \dfrac{K V_A V_B}{2} \quad \text{(slope at } \phi = \pi/2 \text{)}$ | V/rad | — |
| 4 | Lock range of multiplier | $-\pi/2 \le \phi \le +\pi/2$ | rad | Monotonic region |
| 5 | XOR PD average output | $V_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{\phi}{\pi} \quad (0 \le \phi \le \pi)$ | V | Linear region |
| 6 | XOR PD gain | $K_{PD} = V_{CC} / \pi$ | V/rad | Constant slope |
| 7 | Lock range of XOR | $0 \le \phi \le \pi$ | rad | Triangular |
| 8 | Required LPF cutoff | $f_c \ll 2f_{in}$ | Hz | Ripple suppression |
| 9 | Linearity error | $\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{\vert V_{measured} - V_{theory}\vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | $< 5\%$ is acceptable |
| 10 | Phase in degrees ↔ radians | $\phi_{rad} = \phi_{deg} \cdot \pi/180$ | rad | — |

> **Piping discipline notice (KTU-prep):** In the table above, absolute-value and conditional vertical bars are written as `\vert` — this is intentional to keep the markdown table from breaking. In your written exam scripts, plain $|x|$ is fine.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Biomedical Application | Role of Phase Detector |
|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Doppler Ultrasound / Fetal Heart-Rate Monitor** | Coherent demodulation extracts tiny frequency shifts from large carrier echo. |
| **Lock-In Amplifier (EEG, ECG, EMG)** | Phase-sensitive detection pulls out micro-volt signals buried in noise. |
| **Implantable Pacemaker Telemetry** | Carrier recovery from RF link uses PLL with PD. |
| **Pulse Oximetry (SpO₂)** | Dual-wavelength phase measurement separates arterial from venous blood. |
| **Auditory Brainstem Response (ABR)** | Coherent averaging relies on phase-locked stimulus–response timing. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU viva:** When asked *"Where in a hospital is this circuit used?"*, the single best one-line answer is: *"In the **lock-in amplifier** that recovers a small biomedical signal from noise by exploiting its fixed-phase relationship to a reference."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Multiplier Phase Detector

We start from the two input signals. **Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.**

**Step 1: Define the two sinusoidal inputs.**

$$v_1(t) = V_A \cos(\omega t) \quad \text{(Reference)}$$

$$v_2(t) = V_B \cos(\omega t + \phi) \quad \text{(Signal with unknown phase } \phi \text{)}$$

**Step 2: Feed them into the analog multiplier of transfer constant $K$.**

The AD633 data sheet specifies $K = 0.1\ \text{V}^{-1}$, i.e.:

$$v_{out}(t) = K \cdot v_1(t) \cdot v_2(t)$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$v_{out}(t) = K \cdot V_A V_B \cos(\omega t)\cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 3: Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity.**

The identity used is:

$$\cos(\alpha)\cos(\beta) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(\alpha - \beta) + \cos(\alpha + \beta)\bigr]$$

Setting $\alpha = \omega t$ and $\beta = \omega t + \phi$:

$$\cos(\omega t)\cos(\omega t + \phi) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(-\phi) + \cos(2\omega t + \phi)\bigr]$$

Using the even property $\cos(-\phi) = \cos(\phi)$:

$$\cos(\omega t)\cos(\omega t + \phi) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(\phi) + \cos(2\omega t + \phi)\bigr]$$

**Step 4: Substitute back into $v_{out}(t)$.**

$$v_{out}(t) = \frac{K V_A V_B}{2}\cos(\phi) + \frac{K V_A V_B}{2}\cos(2\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 5: Identify the two terms.**

- **Term 1:** $\dfrac{K V_A V_B}{2}\cos(\phi)$ — this is a **DC quantity** (no time dependence). It is the desired phase-discriminating output.
- **Term 2:** $\dfrac{K V_A V_B}{2}\cos(2\omega t + \phi)$ — this is a **doubled-frequency AC ripple** centered at $2\omega$.

**Step 6: Pass through a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c \ll 2f$.**

The LPF removes the $2\omega$ ripple completely. The surviving DC voltage is:

$$V_{DC} = \frac{K V_A V_B}{2}\cos(\phi)$$

**Step 7: Compute the slope (PD gain) at the quadrature point $\phi = \pi/2$.**

Differentiating $V_{DC}$ with respect to $\phi$:

$$\frac{dV_{DC}}{d\phi} = -\frac{K V_A V_B}{2}\sin(\phi)$$

Evaluating the magnitude at $\phi = \pi/2$:

$$K_{PD} = \left| \frac{dV_{DC}}{d\phi} \right|_{\phi = \pi/2} = \frac{K V_A V_B}{2} \cdot 1 = \frac{K V_A V_B}{2}$$

Units: $(\text{V}^{-1})(\text{V})(\text{V}) = \text{V}$, and per radian of $\phi$ → $\text{V/rad}$. ✔

**Step 8: Linearized small-signal approximation.**

For small phase excursions $\delta\phi$ about $\phi_0 = \pi/2$:

$$\sin(\phi_0 + \delta\phi) = \sin(\pi/2)\cos(\delta\phi) + \cos(\pi/2)\sin(\delta\phi) \approx 1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot \delta\phi = 1$$

So the linearized PD characteristic near quadrature is:

$$\Delta V_{DC} \approx K_{PD} \cdot \delta\phi = \frac{K V_A V_B}{2} \cdot \delta\phi$$

This is the form used in the small-signal analysis of the PLL closed-loop transfer function.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — XOR Gate Phase Detector

**Step 1: Define the two square-wave inputs of equal frequency $f$ and period $T = 1/f$.**

$$A(t) = \text{square wave of frequency } f, \text{ phase } 0$$
$$B(t) = \text{square wave of frequency } f, \text{ phase } \phi$$

**Step 2: Compute the XOR truth function.**

The XOR is HIGH (=$V_{CC}$) exactly when $A \ne B$. For square waves of 50 % duty cycle and phase offset $\phi$ (in radians, normalized to $2\pi$ over one period $T$):

The fraction of the period during which $A \ne B$ is:

$$D = \frac{2\phi}{2\pi} = \frac{\phi}{\pi} \quad \text{for } 0 \le \phi \le \pi$$

**Step 3: Compute the average (DC) output.**

$$V_{DC} = \overline{v_{out}(t)} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v_{out}(t)\,dt = V_{CC} \cdot D = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{\phi}{\pi}$$

**Step 4: Compute the PD gain.**

$$K_{PD} = \frac{dV_{DC}}{d\phi} = \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi}$$

**Step 5: Verify end-points.**

- $\phi = 0 \Rightarrow V_{DC} = 0$ (in-phase → output always LOW). ✔
- $\phi = \pi \Rightarrow V_{DC} = V_{CC}$ (anti-phase → output always HIGH). ✔
- $\phi = \pi/2 \Rightarrow V_{DC} = V_{CC}/2$ (50 % duty cycle). ✔

**Step 6: Wrap-around behaviour beyond $\pi$.**

For $\pi < \phi < 2\pi$, the duty cycle *decreases* linearly back to 0, producing the characteristic **triangle wave** that repeats with period $2\pi$ in $\phi$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Property | Multiplier PD | XOR PD |
|----------|---------------|--------|
| Input signal type | Sinusoidal | Square wave (digital) |
| Transfer function $V_{DC}(\phi)$ | $\dfrac{K V_A V_B}{2}\cos(\phi)$ | $V_{CC}\cdot\dfrac{\phi}{\pi}$ |
| Shape | Cosine | Triangle |
| Sign of $V_{DC}$ at $\phi=0$ | Maximum (+K V_A V_B /2) | Minimum (0) |
| Sign of $V_{DC}$ at $\phi=\pi/2$ | 0 | $V_{CC}/2$ |
| Sign of $V_{DC}$ at $\phi=\pi$ | Minimum ($-$K V_A V_B /2) | Maximum ($V_{CC}$) |
| PD gain $K_{PD}$ at lock | $K V_A V_B / 2$ (V/rad) | $V_{CC}/\pi$ (V/rad) |
| Requires LPF? | Yes (mandatory to remove $2\omega$) | Optional (already DC after averaging) |
| Amplitude-sensitive? | Yes (output $\propto V_A V_B$) | No (only logic level) |
| Lock range | $-\pi/2$ to $+\pi/2$ | $0$ to $\pi$ |
| Biomedical use | Lock-in amplifier, coherent demod | Digital PLL clock recovery |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (declared up-front)

> All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with a letter; all node labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes; no `**`, `*`, or HTML is embedded inside the labels; no reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node IDs.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Multiplier Phase Detector Test Setup

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[FG1 FUNCTION GEN 1KHZ REF SIGNAL] --> B1[INPUT V1 TO MULTIPLIER PIN X]
    A2[FG2 FUNCTION GEN 1KHZ DELAYED SIGNAL] --> PH1[PHASE SHIFTER RC NETWORK 0 TO 180 DEG]
    PH1 --> B2[INPUT V2 TO MULTIPLIER PIN Y]
    B1 --> M1[AD633 ANALOG MULTIPLIER K EQUALS 0 POINT 1 PER VOLT]
    B2 --> M1
    M1 --> LPF1[FIRST ORDER RC LOW PASS FILTER FC EQUALS 100 HZ]
    LPF1 --> DMM[DMM OR ADC TO READ VDC]
    M1 --> OSC1[OSCILLOSCOPE CH1 TO MONITOR UNFILTERED PRODUCT]
    DMM --> REC[RECORD VDC VS PHASE TABLE]
    REC --> PLOT[PLOT VDC VS DELTA PHI GRAPH]
    PLOT --> CMP[COMPARE WITH THEORETICAL COSINE CURVE]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — XOR Gate Phase Detector Test Setup

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[FG1 SQUARE WAVE 1KHZ 5 VOLT PP] --> XA[XOR GATE CD4030 PIN A]
    S2[FG2 SQUARE WAVE 1KHZ 5 VOLT PP] --> XB[XOR GATE CD4030 PIN B]
    S2 --> DELAY[MANUAL PHASE SHIFTER 0 TO 180 DEG]
    DELAY --> XB
    XA --> XY[XOR OPERATION A XOR B]
    XB --> XY
    XY --> DMM2[DMM DC MODE FOR AVERAGE VDC]
    XY --> OSC2[OSCILLOSCOPE FOR WAVEFORM AND DUTY CYCLE]
    DMM2 --> TAB[TABULATE VDC VS PHASE]
    TAB --> TRI[VERIFY TRIANGLE WAVE SHAPE]
    TRI --> KP[CALCULATE KPD EQUALS VCC BY PI]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Phase Detector Inside a Biomedical PLL

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[BIOMEDICAL SENSOR SIGNAL EG FETAL DOPPLER] --> AMP[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER GAIN 1000]
    AMP --> LPF2[ANTI ALIAS LPF]
    LPF2 --> DIV[FREQUENCY DIVIDER FOR PLL REFERENCE]
    DIV --> PDIN[INPUT V2 TO PHASE DETECTOR]
    VCO[VOLTAGE CONTROLLED OSCILLATOR CENTRE FREQ] --> VCOOUT[VCO OUTPUT]
    VCOOUT --> PDIN2[INPUT V1 TO PHASE DETECTOR]
    PDIN --> CMPD[PHASE DETECTOR MULTIPLIER OR XOR]
    PDIN2 --> CMPD
    CMPD --> LPFL[LOOP FILTER LPF]
    LPFL --> VCOCTRL[VCO CONTROL VOLTAGE]
    VCOCTRL --> VCO
    LPFL --> LOCK[LOCK DETECTOR OUTPUT]
    LOCK --> DSP[DSP FOR HEART RATE COMPUTATION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for the KTU practical exam:** When asked to "draw the block diagram", always include the **two signal sources, the phase shifter, the phase detector block, the low-pass filter, and the output measurement device (DMM/ADC)** — these five blocks are the examiner's mandatory checklist items.

## 4.5 Pin-Out Reference for the AD633 Analog Multiplier

| Pin | Symbol | Function |
|-----|--------|----------|
| 1, 2 | X1, X2 | Differential X-input (use X1; tie X2 to ground) |
| 3, 4 | Y1, Y2 | Differential Y-input (use Y1; tie Y2 to ground) |
| 5 | W | Sum output node: $W = K \cdot X \cdot Y + Z$ |
| 6, 7 | Z1, Z2 | Summing inputs (tied together and to ground for pure multiplier) |
| 8 | $-V_S$ | Negative supply, typically $-8$ V to $-15$ V |
| 9, 13 | SF | Scale-factor adjust (leave open for $K=0.1$) |
| 10, 12, 14 | NC | Not connected |
| 11 | $+V_S$ | Positive supply, $+8$ V to $+15$ V |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a phase detector and list its two most common implementations used in biomedical instrumentation. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark length):**

A **phase detector (PD)** is a circuit that produces an output voltage whose average (DC) value is a function of the **phase difference** between two input signals of the same frequency.

The two most common implementations in biomedical electronics are:

1. **Analog Multiplier Phase Detector** (using AD633 / AD534) — used in lock-in amplifiers and coherent demodulators; produces a cosine-shaped transfer characteristic.

2. **XOR Gate Phase Detector** (using CD4030 / CD4070) — used in digital PLLs; produces a triangle-shaped transfer characteristic.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two implementations.)*

---

### Question 2: For a multiplier phase detector with inputs $V_A \cos(\omega t)$ and $V_B \cos(\omega t + \phi)$, write the expression for the DC output after low-pass filtering. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

$$V_{DC} = \frac{K V_A V_B}{2}\cos(\phi)$$

where $K$ is the multiplier transfer constant (e.g., $0.1\ \text{V}^{-1}$ for AD633), $V_A, V_B$ are the input amplitudes, and $\phi$ is the phase difference.

*(2 marks for the expression + 1 mark for explaining each term.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a multiplier-based phase detector. Derive the expression for the DC output voltage. `(7 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (refer to §4.2 in SECTION_4):**

The two inputs $v_1(t) = V_A \cos(\omega t)$ and $v_2(t) = V_B \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ are applied to the two input ports of the analog multiplier (AD633). The product signal is passed through a low-pass filter (LPF) with cutoff $f_c \ll 2f$. The DC output is measured using a DMM or ADC.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: Instantaneous product

$$v_{out}(t) = K \cdot V_A \cos(\omega t) \cdot V_B \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

Step 2: Apply the identity $\cos\alpha \cos\beta = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(\alpha - \beta) + \cos(\alpha + \beta)]$:

$$v_{out}(t) = \frac{K V_A V_B}{2}\bigl[\cos(\phi) + \cos(2\omega t + \phi)\bigr]$$

Step 3: LPF removes the $2\omega$ term:

$$V_{DC} = \frac{K V_A V_B}{2}\cos(\phi)$$

**Working principle:** As $\phi$ varies from $0$ to $\pi$, $V_{DC}$ varies from $+\tfrac{K V_A V_B}{2}$ (maximum, in-phase) through 0 (quadrature) to $-\tfrac{K V_A V_B}{2}$ (anti-phase).

**Mark split:** Diagram 2 marks, identity 1 mark, substitution 1 mark, LPF 1 mark, final expression 1 mark, working narrative 1 mark.

---

#### (b) Design an experiment to obtain the transfer characteristic of the multiplier phase detector using IC AD633. Specify the instruments, the procedure, and the expected graph. `(7 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus / Instruments required:**

1. AD633 analog multiplier IC on a breadboard, with $\pm 12$ V DC supply.
2. Two function generators (FG1, FG2) — both set to **1 kHz sine wave, 4 V p-p**.
3. RC phase-shifter network (or a manual time-delay) for FG2.
4. First-order RC low-pass filter: $R = 1.6\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C = 1\ \mu\text{F}$ → $f_c \approx 100\ \text{Hz}$.
5. Dual-channel oscilloscope.
6. Digital multimeter (DC mode) or ADC.
7. Connecting wires and a regulated power supply.

**Procedure:**

1. Power up the AD633 with $\pm 12$ V; verify offset-null as per data sheet.
2. Set FG1 to 1 kHz, 4 V p-p sine, apply to X-input (pin 1).
3. Set FG2 to 1 kHz, 4 V p-p sine, pass through phase-shifter (0° initially), apply to Y-input (pin 3).
4. Connect multiplier output (pin 5) to LPF; LPF output to DMM.
5. Observe unfiltered product on scope; verify doubling of frequency to **2 kHz**.
6. Vary the phase shift in steps of 15° from 0° to 180° (or 0 to $\pi$ in rad).
7. At each step, record the DMM reading ($V_{DC}$) and the scope phase.
8. Plot **$V_{DC}$ vs. $\phi$** on graph paper.

**Expected graph:** A **cosine wave** of amplitude $\tfrac{K V_A V_B}{2}$ with peaks at $\phi = 0$ and troughs at $\phi = \pi$, crossing zero at $\phi = \pi/2$.

**Calculations:**

$$V_{DC,\max} = \frac{K V_A V_B}{2} = \frac{0.1 \times 2 \times 2}{2} = 0.2\ \text{V}$$

$$K_{PD} = \frac{K V_A V_B}{2} = 0.2\ \text{V/rad}$$

**Mark split:** Apparatus 2 marks, procedure 3 marks, expected graph + calculation 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A*

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an XOR gate as a phase detector. Derive the average output voltage as a function of phase difference. `(7 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (refer to §4.3 in SECTION_4):**
