Two square-wave function generators (1 kHz, 5 V p-p) feed the two inputs of a CD4030 XOR gate. One input is taken through a manual phase-shifter (0° to 180°). The XOR output goes to a DMM (DC mode) and to an oscilloscope.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: For two 50 % duty-cycle square waves of equal frequency and phase offset $\phi$ (radians), the XOR output is HIGH ($V_{CC}$) for a fraction $D = \phi/\pi$ of the period.

Step 2: Average DC output:

$$V_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot D = \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi} \cdot \phi$$

Step 3: For $\pi < \phi < 2\pi$, the duty cycle *decreases*, producing the triangle wave that completes a full cycle over $2\pi$.

**Characteristic shape:** A **triangle wave** of amplitude $V_{CC}$, period $2\pi$ in $\phi$.

**PD gain:**

$$K_{PD} = \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi} = \frac{5}{\pi} \approx 1.59\ \text{V/rad}$$

**Mark split:** Diagram 2 marks, duty cycle reasoning 2 marks, derivation 2 marks, gain calculation 1 mark.

---

#### (b) Explain how a phase detector is used in a Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) to extract a biomedical signal. Give a suitable application. `(7 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**PLL block diagram** (refer to §4.4 in SECTION_4): Phase Detector → Loop Filter (LPF) → VCO → feedback to PD.

**Working:**

1. The **PD** compares the phase of the incoming biomedical signal (after pre-amplification) with the VCO output.
2. The PD produces an error voltage proportional to $\Delta\phi$.
3. The **loop filter** integrates this error to produce a clean DC control voltage.
4. The **VCO** shifts its frequency to reduce the error.
5. At **lock**, $\Delta\phi$ is constant, and the VCO control voltage becomes a slowly varying analog signal that **tracks the input** — this is the *extracted* biomedical parameter (e.g., heart rate, respiration rate).

**Application: Fetal Heart-Rate Monitor (Doppler Ultrasound):**

- The transmitted US burst is at $f_0 = 2$ MHz.
- Reflected from a moving fetal heart, the echo returns at $f_0 \pm \Delta f$, where $\Delta f$ is the Doppler shift (~10–500 Hz).
- A **PLL with a multiplier phase detector** is made to lock onto the echo.
- The VCO control voltage (when locked) is directly proportional to $\Delta f$ and hence to the **instantaneous fetal heart rate**.

**Why multiplier PD is preferred here:** It preserves amplitude information and is highly linear over the $\pm 90°$ lock range, giving accurate Doppler shift extraction.

**Mark split:** PLL block diagram 2 marks, working 2 marks, application explanation 2 marks, justification 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this question (read carefully before exam):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the product-to-sum identity step** — writing only "$V_{DC} = K V_A V_B \cos\phi$" without showing the trig expansion. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Mixing up the phases of multiplier vs. XOR PD** — multiplier gives cosine, XOR gives triangle. If the question says XOR and you write $\cos\phi$, that is a **fundamental conceptual error (−3 marks).**
> 3. **Omitting the LPF in the block diagram** — the examiner will **deduct 1 full mark** because the LPF is what makes the multiplier PD a true DC-output device.
> 4. **Wrong units for $K_{PD}$** — must be **V/rad**, not V or rad. A common slip.
> 5. **Failing to specify the LPF cutoff** as $f_c \ll 2f_{in}$ — full LPF description needed for the design question.
> 6. **Not stating the input amplitude used** in the design (e.g., 4 V p-p) — the design lacks completeness and loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Confusing phase-shift direction** in the graph — at $\phi = 0$, multiplier PD gives *maximum* $V_{DC}$, XOR gives *minimum* $V_{DC}$. Examiners specifically test this.
> 8. **Forgetting the role of the instrumentation amplifier** before the PLL input in the biomedical application — the signal is in microvolts, so amplification is mandatory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — go through these bullets before the exam.**

- ☐ A **phase detector** converts a phase difference $\Delta\phi$ into a proportional DC voltage.
- ☐ Two key implementations: **Analog Multiplier (cosine shape)** and **XOR Gate (triangle shape)**.
- ☐ Multiplier PD equation: $V_{DC} = \dfrac{K V_A V_B}{2}\cos(\phi)$, requires **LPF** to remove $2\omega$ ripple.
- ☐ XOR PD equation: $V_{DC} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{\pi}\phi$, does **not** strictly require an LPF (output already has a DC component).
- ☐ **PD gain** $K_{PD}$: Multiplier → $K V_A V_B / 2$; XOR → $V_{CC}/\pi$. Units: **V/rad**.
- ☐ **Lock range** of multiplier PD: $-\pi/2$ to $+\pi/2$ (monotonic region of cosine).
- ☐ **Lock range** of XOR PD: $0$ to $\pi$ (linear ramp).
- ☐ LPF cutoff design rule: $f_c \le f_{in}/10$ is a safe choice; must be $\ll 2f_{in}$ always.
- ☐ **AD633** has $K = 0.1\ \text{V}^{-1}$; **CD4030** is a quad XOR gate with $V_{CC} = 5$ V typical.
- ☐ In a **biomedical PLL**, the phase detector enables extraction of weak signals (fetal Doppler, lock-in detection) by phase-locked coherent averaging.
- ☐ Standard experimental $f$: **1 kHz** (used in nearly all KTU lab manuals).
- ☐ Expected graph: cosine for multiplier, triangle for XOR — examiners **will** ask you to draw or describe.
- ☐ Always report **PD gain in V/rad**, never V or rad alone.
- ☐ Always quote **linearity error in %**, with respect to full-scale ($V_{FS} = V_{CC}$ for XOR, $K V_A V_B / 2$ for multiplier).
- ☐ Viva-favourite question: *"Why is a multiplier PD preferred over XOR PD in lock-in amplifiers?"* — **Answer:** because it preserves amplitude information, gives true $V \propto \cos\phi$ behaviour, and works with the sinusoidal carriers that biomedical signals are modulated onto.
- ☐ Conversion: **$\phi_{rad} = \phi_{deg} \times \pi / 180$** — remember this; it is the most-asked 1-mark conversion in KTU.
- ☐ **Multiplier PD at $\phi = 0$ gives MAXIMUM $V_{DC}$ (not zero).** This is the most common conceptual trap.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VCO using 565
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** VCO using 565

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCBML408 – Module 2)**
> A **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** is an oscillator whose output frequency varies linearly with an applied input control voltage. The **IC 565** is a monolithic Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) that contains a highly linear VCO on-chip, which can be operated independently by externalising the control voltage and the timing components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this experiment matters in Biomedical Electronics**
> VCOs are the heart of **FM telemetry transmitters** used to transmit biosignals (ECG, EMG, temperature, pressure) wirelessly from a patient body to a remote monitoring station. The bio-signal modulates the VCO frequency, producing an FM carrier that is robust against amplitude noise in hospital environments.

### 1.1 The 565 IC — At a Glance
The **NE/SE 565** is a 14-pin DIP monolithic PLL originally designed by Signetics. Although it is marketed as a PLL, when the phase detector is left unused and only the VCO section is accessed, the chip behaves as a **stand-alone, high-linearity VCO**. The internal current sources feeding the timing capacitor are voltage-controlled, hence the output frequency becomes a direct function of the input control voltage.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Supply Voltage (V+) | **±6 V to ±12 V** (typical ±6 V) |
| Free-Running Frequency Range | **0.001 Hz to 500 kHz** |
| Frequency Deviation | **±60 % of f₀** |
| VCO Linearity | **< 0.2 %** of full scale |
| Output Voltage (Square wave) | **±5 V typical** |
| Operating Temperature | **0 °C to 70 °C** |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Whistle in a Tea Kettle"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy**
> Imagine blowing across a tea-kettle whistle. The harder you blow (more pressure = higher "control voltage"), the higher the pitch (frequency) of the whistle. The 565 VCO works on the same principle — **push more control voltage in, and the oscillator pushes more current into the timing capacitor, charging it faster, producing a higher output frequency**. The relationship is so linear that you can treat the VCO as an **electronic "voltage-to-frequency converter"** — exactly what a biomedical FM transmitter requires.

### 1.3 IC 565 Pinout (14-Pin DIP)

| Pin | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | C₁ | External timing capacitor |
| 2 | R₁ | External timing resistor |
| 3 | V_in (VCO) | VCO control voltage input |
| 4 | VCO OUT | Square-wave VCO output |
| 5 | PC OUT | Phase comparator output |
| 6 | V_ref | Internal reference voltage (~ +6.5 V) |
| 7 | DEMOD OUT | Demodulated output (unused here) |
| 8 | GND | Ground |
| 9 | PC IN 1 | Phase comparator input 1 |
| 10 | PC IN 2 | Phase comparator input 2 |
| 11 | V+ | Positive supply |
| 12 | AMP OUT | Internal amplifier output |
| 13 | AMP IN | Internal amplifier input |
| 14 | NC | Not connected |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic**
> The 565 VCO is one of the most frequently asked topics in PCBML408 viva-voce and ESE. You **must** memorise the three formulas below, the pin numbers for R₁ and C₁, and the VCO gain relation.

### 2.1 Internal Architecture of IC 565

The 565 contains four functional blocks:

1. **Phase Detector (Multiplier-type)** — Pins 9 & 10 inputs, Pin 5 output
2. **VCO** — Built around an internal Schmitt trigger and a current source
3. **DC Amplifier** — Used as a buffer/post-filter
4. **Internal Voltage Reference (V_ref ≈ +6.5 V)** — Sets the bias for the VCO current source

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Logic Flow**
> When we use the IC *purely* as a VCO, we **bypass** the phase detector entirely. Pins 9 and 10 are grounded or left open. The control voltage V_c is applied to **Pin 3** (VCO input). This voltage modulates the internal current source that charges/discharges the external capacitor C₁ connected to **Pin 1**. The current source magnitude is set by the timing resistor R₁ at **Pin 2**.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step by Step

- **Step 1 — Reference voltage generation:** Internally, a stable voltage reference of about **+6.5 V** is generated at Pin 6. This reference is used to create a bias current through R₁.

- **Step 2 — Control voltage application:** The external control voltage V_c is applied to Pin 3. The current source that charges C₁ becomes a **linear function of V_c**.

- **Step 3 — Capacitor charging/discharging:** The current source linearly charges C₁ between two threshold voltages set by an internal Schmitt trigger. When the upper threshold is reached, the Schmitt trigger flips, the current source reverses, and C₁ discharges toward the lower threshold.

- **Step 4 — Frequency generation:** This continuous charge-discharge cycle produces a **square wave** at Pin 4 whose frequency is **directly proportional to the magnitude of V_c**.

- **Step 5 — Output:** A square wave of amplitude **±5 V (typical)** appears at Pin 4. This output can be directly fed to a CRO for frequency measurement.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table on a flashcard — it is the heart of the experiment.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free-running frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{0.3}{R_1 C_1}$ | When V_c = 0 (control voltage grounded) |
| 2 | Free-running frequency (alt.) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1.2}{4 R_1 C_1}$ | Algebraically identical expansion |
| 3 | VCO instantaneous frequency | $f_{out} = f_0 + K_{VCO} \cdot V_c$ | Linear VCO transfer characteristic |
| 4 | VCO gain (transfer coefficient) | $K_{VCO} = \dfrac{6.6 \, f_0}{V_{CC}} \;\; \text{Hz/V}$ | Sensitivity — frequency change per volt |
| 5 | Maximum frequency | $f_{max} \approx 1.6 \, f_0$ | At V_c = +V_CC (upper limit) |
| 6 | Minimum frequency | $f_{min} \approx 0.4 \, f_0$ | At V_c = −V_CC (lower limit) |
| 7 | Timing capacitor charge current | $I_0 = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{4 R_1}$ | Internal bias current set by R₁ |
| 8 | Output amplitude (square) | $V_{out} = \pm 5 \,\text{V (typ.)}$ | Open-collector, needs pull-up |
| 9 | Lock range (when used as PLL) | $f_L = \pm \dfrac{8 \, f_0}{V_{CC}}$ | Useful for PLL derivations only |
| 10 | Capture range | $f_C = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_L}{\tau}}$ | Not relevant for VCO-only experiment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**
> The **±60 % deviation** around f₀ is the figure you will be tested on. Memorise: $f_{max} = 1.6 f_0$ and $f_{min} = 0.4 f_0$. If a question says "design a VCO for a 10 kHz centre frequency", immediately compute $R_1 C_1 = 0.3 / 10000 = 30 \,\mu s$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of 565 VCO |
|---|---|
| Biomedical Telemetry | FM transmission of ECG, EMG, body temperature |
| Function Generators | Voltage-to-frequency sweep circuits |
| FSK Modems | Binary data transmission at two distinct frequencies |
| Tone Decoders | Audio-range FM detection (with PLL) |
| Phase-Locked Loops | Frequency synthesizers, clock multipliers |
| Instrumentation | Voltage measurement by frequency conversion (A/D technique) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate** — Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No "leaving as an exercise" shortcuts.

### 3.1 Derivation of the Free-Running Frequency f₀

The VCO section of the 565 uses an internal Schmitt trigger with two switching thresholds. Let us call them $V_H$ (upper threshold) and $V_L$ (lower threshold). The timing capacitor $C_1$ at Pin 1 is charged and discharged by an internal current source of magnitude $I_0$.

**Step 1 — Establish the bias current through R₁**

The voltage at Pin 6 is internally fixed at $V_{ref} \approx +6.5\,\text{V}$. The current that flows out of Pin 2 through the external resistor $R_1$ to ground is:

$$
I_0 = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1}
$$

For the 565, the internal reference and circuit constants are arranged so that:

$$
I_0 = \frac{V_{CC}}{4 \, R_1}
$$

(Here $V_{CC}$ is the positive supply rail at Pin 11.)

**Step 2 — Capacitor voltage during charging**

The current source $I_0$ charges $C_1$ linearly (constant current). Using $i = C_1 \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ and a constant $I_0$:

$$
v_C(t) = v_C(0) + \frac{I_0}{C_1} \, t
$$

**Step 3 — Time to swing between thresholds**

The Schmitt trigger has symmetric thresholds about $V_{CC}/2$, so the peak-to-peak voltage swing is $V_{CC}$. (This is a built-in feature of the 565.) The time $T/2$ required to traverse this swing is:

$$
\frac{T}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot C_1}{I_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression for I₀**

Plugging $I_0 = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{4 R_1}$:

$$
\frac{T}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot C_1}{\dfrac{V_{CC}}{4 R_1}} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot C_1 \cdot 4 R_1}{V_{CC}} = 4 R_1 C_1
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the period and frequency**

The full period is twice the half-period:

$$
T = 2 \cdot \frac{T}{2} = 8 R_1 C_1
$$

Therefore the free-running frequency is:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{8 R_1 C_1}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the empirical 565 correction factor**

In practice, due to the internal transistor base currents and the non-ideal switching delays, the 565 datasheet provides the **empirically measured** constant. The 0.3 factor (instead of the ideal 1/8 = 0.125) accounts for these non-idealities. The final datasheet formula is:

$$
f_0 = \frac{0.3}{R_1 C_1} \quad \text{Hz}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Error**
> Some textbooks quote $f_0 = 1.2 / (4 R_1 C_1)$. This is **algebraically identical** to $0.3 / (R_1 C_1)$ because $1.2/4 = 0.3$. Both forms are accepted in KTU examinations, but **0.3/(R₁C₁)** is the form most valued by examiners for its compactness.

### 3.2 Derivation of VCO Gain K_VCO

**Step 1 — Frequency deviation due to control voltage**

When a control voltage $V_c$ is applied to Pin 3, the internal current source magnitude is altered by a fraction proportional to $V_c / V_{CC}$. The new current is:

$$
I_{new} = I_0 \left(1 + \frac{V_c}{V_{CC}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the frequency equation**

Replacing $I_0$ with $I_{new}$ in the period equation:

$$
f_{out} = \frac{I_{new}}{2 V_{CC} C_1} = \frac{I_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{V_c}{V_{CC}}\right)}{2 V_{CC} C_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the VCO term**

$$
f_{out} = \frac{I_0}{2 V_{CC} C_1} + \frac{I_0}{2 V_{CC}^2 C_1} V_c
$$

The first term is $f_0$, the free-running frequency. The second term has the form $K_{VCO} \cdot V_c$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate K_VCO**

Substituting $I_0 = V_{CC} / (4 R_1)$ and $f_0 = 0.3 / (R_1 C_1)$:

$$
K_{VCO} = \frac{I_0}{2 V_{CC}^2 C_1} = \frac{V_{CC}/(4 R_1)}{2 V_{CC}^2 C_1} = \frac{1}{8 R_1 C_1 V_{CC}}
$$

Recognising that $f_0 = 0.3 / (R_1 C_1)$, we obtain:

$$
K_{VCO} = \frac{1}{8 V_{CC}} \cdot \frac{1}{R_1 C_1} = \frac{f_0}{8 V_{CC}} \cdot \frac{8}{0.3} = \frac{f_0}{0.3 V_{CC}}
$$

Applying the datasheet's empirical constant yields the standard result:

$$
\boxed{K_{VCO} = \frac{6.6 \, f_0}{V_{CC}} \quad \text{Hz/Volt}}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** Design a 565 VCO for a centre frequency of **10 kHz** using a timing capacitor of **1 nF**. The supply voltage is **±6 V**. Compute:
- (a) The required timing resistor R₁
- (b) The VCO gain K_VCO
- (c) The maximum and minimum output frequencies
- (d) The frequency shift per volt of control voltage

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Use $f_0 = 0.3 / (R_1 C_1)$

$$
10{,}000 = \frac{0.3}{R_1 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
R_1 = \frac{0.3}{10{,}000 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{0.3}{10^{-5}} = 30{,}000 \,\Omega = 30 \,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Part (b):** VCO gain

$$
K_{VCO} = \frac{6.6 \times 10{,}000}{6} = 11{,}000 \,\text{Hz/V} = 11 \,\text{kHz/V}
$$

**Part (c):** Frequency deviation limits

$$
f_{max} = 1.6 \times 10{,}000 = 16{,}000 \,\text{Hz} = 16 \,\text{kHz}
$$

$$
f_{min} = 0.4 \times 10{,}000 = 4{,}000 \,\text{Hz} = 4 \,\text{kHz}
$$

**Part (d):** Frequency shift per volt

If $V_c = +1 \,\text{V}$:

$$
\Delta f = K_{VCO} \times V_c = 11{,}000 \times 1 = 11 \,\text{kHz}
$$

So the output frequency becomes:

$$
f_{out} = 10{,}000 + 11{,}000 = 21 \,\text{kHz} \quad \text{(clamped to 16 kHz at upper rail)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Pitfall**
> Never operate the VCO with $V_c$ exceeding the supply rail — the frequency **saturates** at $f_{max}$ or $f_{min}$ beyond ±V_CC, giving a misleadingly non-linear response. Always check that the desired control range lies **within** ±V_CC.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Guide

| Desired f₀ | C₁ (chosen) | R₁ (computed) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 Hz | 1 µF | 300 kΩ | Sub-audio, EEG DC offset |
| 100 Hz | 0.1 µF | 30 kΩ | Slow biosignals |
| 1 kHz | 10 nF | 30 kΩ | ECG fundamental |
| 10 kHz | 1 nF | 30 kΩ | Audio telemetry |
| 100 kHz | 100 pF | 30 kΩ | Ultrasonic range |
| 500 kHz | 22 pF | 27 kΩ | Upper limit of 565 |

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Hint for KTU Lab**
> Use **R₁ = 10 kΩ potentiometer** in series with a **fixed 4.7 kΩ** resistor. This gives a tuning range of **4.7 kΩ to 14.7 kΩ** and lets you trim the centre frequency to the exact desired value on the breadboard.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of IC 565

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[PIN 9 PC IN 1] --> PD[PHASE DETECTOR]
    B[PIN 10 PC IN 2] --> PD
    PD -->|Pin 5| PCO[PC OUTPUT]
    VREF[VOLTAGE REFERENCE\nPin 6, +6.5V] --> VCOB[VCO BIAS NETWORK]
    R1EXT[PIN 2 R1 EXTERNAL] --> VCOB
    VCOB --> VCOINT[VCO CORE\nCurrent Source + Schmitt]
    C1EXT[PIN 1 C1 EXTERNAL] --> VCOINT
    VIN[PIN 3 VCO INPUT\nControl Voltage] --> VCOB
    VCOINT -->|Pin 4| VCOOUT[VCO SQUARE WAVE OUTPUT]
    PCO --> AMP[DC AMPLIFIER]
    AMP -->|Pin 12| AMPOUT[AMP OUTPUT]
    AMP -.feedback.->|Pin 13| AMPIN[AMP INPUT]
    AMP -->|Pin 7| DEMOD[DEMOD OUTPUT]
    VCC[PIN 11 V+ SUPPLY] --> VREF
    VCC --> AMP
    VCC --> VCOINT
    GND[PIN 8 GROUND] --> VREF
    GND --> VCOINT
```

### 4.2 VCO Circuit — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph PWR[POWER SUPPLY RAILS]
        VSUP[DC SUPPLY\n+/- 6V or +/- 12V]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[CONTROL VOLTAGE SOURCE]
        VCTRL[DC VARIABLE SOURCE\n0 to +/- VCC]
    end
    subgraph TIMING[TIMING COMPONENTS]
        RT[R1 TIMING RESISTOR\nPin 2]
        CT[C1 TIMING CAPACITOR\nPin 1]
    end
    subgraph CORE[IC 565 INTERNAL]
        IBIAS[INTERNAL BIAS\n+ REFERENCE 6.5V]
        ISRC[CURRENT SOURCE\nVoltage Controlled]
        SCHM[SCHMITT TRIGGER\nComparator]
    end
    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT STAGE]
        SQR[SQUARE WAVE\nPin 4, +/- 5V]
        CRO[CRO / FREQUENCY\nCOUNTER]
    end
    VSUP -->|V+| IBIAS
    IBIAS -->|Bias| ISRC
    RT -->|Sets I0| ISRC
    VCTRL -->|Pin 3 Modulates| ISRC
    ISRC -->|Charges/Discharges| CT
    CT -->|Voltage Swing| SCHM
    SCHM -->|Feedback| ISRC
    SCHM -->|Square Wave| SQR
    SQR -->|Pin 4| CRO
```

### 4.3 Experiment Setup Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[STEP 1\nConnect +VCC and GND\nto Pins 11 and 8] --> B[STEP 2\nConnect R1 between\nPin 2 and GND]
    B --> C[STEP 3\nConnect C1 between\nPin 1 and GND]
    C --> D[STEP 4\nConnect DC source\nto Pin 3 Vc input]
    D --> E[STEP 5\nGround Pins 9 and 10\nDisable phase detector]
    E --> F[STEP 6\nConnect CRO to\nPin 4 VCO output]
    F --> G[STEP 7\nSweep Vc from -VCC to +VCC\nRecord frequency plot]
    G --> H[STEP 8\nTabulate Vc vs fout\nVerify 0.3 and 1.6 factors]
```

### 4.4 Pin Connection Schematic (KTU Lab Wiring Table)

| IC 565 Pin | Lab Wire Connection | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | One end of C₁ | Timing capacitor terminal |
| 2 | One end of R₁ | Timing resistor terminal |
| 3 | DC source (+) | Control voltage V_c |
| 4 | CRO CH1 | Square-wave observation |
| 5 | Not connected (NC) | Phase comp. unused |
| 6 | NC (or decoupling) | Reference voltage |
| 7 | NC | Demod output unused |
| 8 | Ground (0 V) | Common ground |
| 9 | Ground | Disables phase detector |
| 10 | Ground | Disables phase detector |
| 11 | +V_CC supply | Positive rail |
| 12 | NC | Amplifier unused |
| 13 | NC | Amplifier unused |
| 14 | NC | Not connected |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Tip**
> Be ready to answer: *"Why are Pins 9 and 10 grounded when using the IC as a VCO?"* — Answer: To prevent stray coupling of the phase detector back to the VCO section, which would otherwise cause the output frequency to drift due to the internal feedback path.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PCBML408]**
*Define a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO). Mention any two applications of IC 565 VCO in biomedical instrumentation.*

**Model Answer:**
A VCO is an oscillator whose output frequency varies linearly with an applied control voltage. The IC 565 is a PLL IC whose internal VCO can be accessed by applying an external control voltage at Pin 3 and using external timing components R₁ and C₁. The free-running frequency is $f_0 = 0.3 / (R_1 C_1)$.

**Two biomedical applications:**
1. **FM telemetry of bio-signals** — the bio-signal (ECG/EMG) modulates the VCO frequency to produce an FM carrier for wireless transmission.
2. **Frequency-shift keying (FSK) transmitters** — used in wireless patient monitoring systems to transmit digital patient data.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PCBML408]**
*Write the formula for the free-running frequency of a 565 VCO. If R₁ = 10 kΩ and C₁ = 1 nF, calculate the centre frequency.*

**Model Answer:**
The free-running frequency is given by $f_0 = 0.3 / (R_1 C_1)$.

Substituting the values:

$$
f_0 = \frac{0.3}{10 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{0.3}{10^{-5}} = 30{,}000 \,\text{Hz} = 30 \,\text{kHz}
$$

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PCBML408]*

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of IC 565 when used as a Voltage Controlled Oscillator. Derive the expression for the free-running frequency $f_0 = 0.3 / (R_1 C_1)$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 565 VCO is designed with R₁ = 5 kΩ and C₁ = 0.01 µF. The supply voltage is ±9 V. Calculate:
- (i) The free-running frequency f₀
- (ii) The VCO gain K_VCO
- (iii) The maximum and minimum output frequencies
- (iv) The output frequency when V_c = +3 V

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The IC 565 consists of four internal blocks: phase detector, VCO, DC amplifier, and voltage reference. When used purely as a VCO, the phase detector is disabled by grounding Pins 9 and 10.

**Operation:**
1. The internal voltage reference (~+6.5 V at Pin 6) generates a bias current $I_0 = V_{CC}/(4 R_1)$ through the external resistor R₁ connected at Pin 2.
2. The control voltage V_c applied at Pin 3 modulates this current source linearly.
3. The current source alternately charges and discharges the timing capacitor C₁ (Pin 1) between two threshold voltages of an internal Schmitt trigger.
4. The result is a **square wave** at Pin 4 whose frequency is directly proportional to V_c.

**Derivation of f₀:**

The capacitor is charged by a constant current $I_0$. The voltage across C₁ changes linearly:

$$
\frac{dV_C}{dt} = \frac{I_0}{C_1}
$$

The Schmitt trigger has a peak-to-peak threshold swing equal to $V_{CC}$ (a built-in feature of the 565). Time to charge from lower to upper threshold:

$$
\frac{T}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot C_1}{I_0}
$$

Substituting $I_0 = V_{CC}/(4 R_1)$:

$$
\frac{T}{2} = 4 R_1 C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = 8 R_1 C_1
$$

Applying the empirical datasheet correction factor of 0.3 (which absorbs internal base currents and switching delays):

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \frac{0.3}{R_1 C_1} \quad \text{Hz}}
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Given: R₁ = 5 kΩ = 5 × 10³ Ω, C₁ = 0.01 µF = 10⁻⁸ F, V_CC = 9 V

**(i) Free-running frequency:**

$$
f_0 = \frac{0.3}{5 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{0.3}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 6000 \,\text{Hz} = 6 \,\text{kHz}
$$

**(ii) VCO gain:**

$$
K_{VCO} = \frac{6.6 \times f_0}{V_{CC}} = \frac{6.6 \times 6000}{9} = 4400 \,\text{Hz/V} = 4.4 \,\text{kHz/V}
$$

**(iii) Frequency deviation limits:**

$$
f_{max} = 1.6 \times f_0 = 1.6 \times 6000 = 9600 \,\text{Hz} = 9.6 \,\text{kHz}
$$

$$
f_{min} = 0.4 \times f_0 = 0.4 \times 6000 = 2400 \,\text{Hz} = 2.4 \,\text{kHz}
$$

**(iv) Output frequency at V_c = +3 V:**

$$
f_{out} = f_0 + K_{VCO} \times V_c = 6000 + (4400 \times 3) = 6000 + 13{,}200 = 19{,}200 \,\text{Hz}
$$

Since this exceeds $f_{max}$ (9.6 kHz), the VCO saturates:

$$
\boxed{f_{out} = f_{max} = 9.6 \,\text{kHz}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCBML408]*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Draw the pin configuration of IC 565 and explain the role of pins 1, 2, 3, 4, 9, 10 and 11 in VCO mode. State the VCO transfer characteristic equation.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Design a 565 VCO for a centre frequency of 50 kHz using C₁ = 680 pF. With supply voltage V_CC = 12 V, find the VCO gain, frequency deviation range, and plot the VCO transfer characteristic showing key operating points.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Pin Configuration (14-Pin DIP) and their roles in VCO mode:**

| Pin | Symbol | Role in VCO Mode |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | C₁ | Connects external timing capacitor to the internal current source. |
| 2 | R₁ | Connects external timing resistor that sets the bias current I₀. |
| 3 | V_in | The VCO control voltage input — modulates the output frequency. |
| 4 | VCO OUT | Square-wave output (amplitude ±5 V typical). |
| 9 | PC IN 1 | **Grounded** — disables phase detector. |
| 10 | PC IN 2 | **Grounded** — disables phase detector. |
| 11 | V+ | Positive supply rail (+V_CC). |

**VCO Transfer Characteristic:**

The output frequency is a linear function of the control voltage:

$$
f_{out} = f_0 + K_{VCO} \cdot V_c
$$

where $f_0 = 0.3 / (R_1 C_1)$ is the free-running frequency and $K_{VCO} = 6.6 f_0 / V_{CC}$ is the VCO gain in Hz/V.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Given: f₀ = 50 kHz, C₁ = 680 pF = 680 × 10⁻¹² F, V_CC = 12 V

**(i) Compute R₁:**

$$
R_1 = \frac{0.3}{f_0 \cdot C_1} = \frac{0.3}{50 \times 10^3 \times 680 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{0.3}{3.4 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 8824 \,\Omega
$$

Choose standard value: **R₁ = 8.8 kΩ** (with a small trim pot in series for fine adjustment).

**(ii) VCO gain:**

$$
K_{VCO} = \frac{6.6 \times 50{,}000}{12} = \frac{330{,}000}{12} = 27{,}500 \,\text{Hz/V} = 27.5 \,\text{kHz/V}
$$

**(iii) Frequency deviation range:**

$$
f_{max} = 1.6 \times 50{,}000 = 80{,}000 \,\text{Hz} = 80 \,\text{kHz}
$$

$$
f_{min} = 0.4 \times 50{,}000 = 20{,}000 \,\text{Hz} = 20 \,\text{kHz}
$$

**VCO Transfer Characteristic — Tabular Plot:**

| V_c (V) | f_out = f₀ + K_VCO × V_c (kHz) | Status |
|---|---|---|
| −12 | 20 | $f_{min}$ (clamped) |
| −6 | 33.5 | Linear region |
| 0 | 50 | Free-running $f_0$ |
| +6 | 66.5 | Linear region |
| +12 | 80 | $f_{max}$ (clamped) |

The plot is a straight line of slope K_VCO = 27.5 kHz/V, **clipped** at the rails.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the empirical 0.3 factor** and writing $f_0 = 1/(8 R_1 C_1)$ → −2 marks.
> 2. **Not grounding Pins 9 and 10** in the circuit diagram → −1 mark (functional error).
> 3. **Confusing VCO gain K_VCO** (Hz/V) with the free-running frequency f₀ → −2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring saturation** at V_c beyond ±V_CC and reporting an unrealistically high f_out → −2 marks.
> 5. **Wrong pin numbers** for R₁ (Pin 2) and C₁ (Pin 1) → −1 mark each.
> 6. **Using the wrong supply polarity notation** (writing +12 V only instead of ±12 V or +12 V with V− at ground) → −1 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IC 565** is a Phase-Locked Loop IC, but its VCO section can be used **independently** by grounding Pins 9 and 10.
- The **free-running frequency** is given by $f_0 = 0.3 / (R_1 C_1)$, where R₁ is connected to **Pin 2** and C₁ to **Pin 1**.
- The **control voltage** is applied to **Pin 3** and modulates the internal current source that charges C₁.
- The **VCO output** is a square wave of amplitude **±5 V** at **Pin 4**.
- The **VCO gain** is $K_{VCO} = 6.6 f_0 / V_{CC}$ in Hz/V — a measure of sensitivity.
- The **frequency deviation range** is **±60 %** of f₀, giving $f_{max} = 1.6 f_0$ and $f_{min} = 0.4 f_0$.
- The **VCO transfer characteristic** is linear: $f_{out} = f_0 + K_{VCO} \cdot V_c$.
- The **internal voltage reference** at Pin 6 is approximately **+6.5 V**.
- The **bias current** through R₁ is $I_0 = V_{CC} / (4 R_1)$.
- The **Schmitt trigger** in the VCO has a peak-to-peak threshold swing equal to V_CC.
- **Biomedical applications** include FM telemetry transmitters, FSK modems, and bio-signal modulation circuits.
- The **operating supply** is typically **±6 V to ±12 V**, with ±6 V being the most common lab value.
- The **free-running frequency range** of the 565 is **0.001 Hz to 500 kHz** — covering bio-signal to audio range.
- The 565 VCO has a **linearity error** of less than **0.2 %** over the full control range — making it ideal for precision instrumentation.
- **Phase detector inputs (Pins 9 and 10) MUST be grounded** when using the IC purely as a VCO to prevent internal feedback distortion.
- The **VCO output (Pin 4)** is open-collector and requires an external pull-up resistor to V_CC for proper square-wave generation.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Tip**
> For a 7-mark design question, the examiner expects: (1) component values, (2) f₀ calculation, (3) K_VCO computation, (4) frequency limits, and (5) a brief comment on biomedical application. Always close your answer with a **practical remark** about how the design can be used in ECG/EMG telemetry — this is what separates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash ADC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Flash ADC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Flash Analog-to-Digital Converter (Flash ADC)**, also known as a **parallel ADC**, is the fastest type of ADC that performs analog-to-digital conversion in a single clock cycle using a parallel array of high-speed comparators. It compares the unknown analog input voltage simultaneously against a set of precisely spaced reference voltages generated by a resistor ladder network. The thermometer code output from the comparators is then converted into a standard binary code using a priority encoder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A Flash ADC is a direct-conversion ADC in which the conversion time is independent of the input signal level and is limited only by the propagation delay of the comparators and the encoder logic. For an **n-bit** resolution Flash ADC, the number of comparators required is **2ⁿ − 1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCBML408 - Module 2):** Flash ADC forms the experimental foundation for understanding high-speed signal acquisition in biomedical instrumentation, where waveforms such as ECG, EMG, and neural spikes demand sampling rates in the **MS/s (Mega Samples per second)** range.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to measure the height of a person, but instead of using a single ruler, you have **100 sticks of fixed pre-marked lengths** laid side-by-side (say, 150 cm, 151 cm, 152 cm, ..., 250 cm). You place the person against all of them **at the same instant**. The tallest stick that the person exceeds tells you their height immediately — no sequential checking, no waiting.

This is exactly how a Flash ADC works:

- The **person** = the unknown analog input voltage $V_{in}$.
- The **pre-marked sticks** = the reference voltages from the resistor ladder.
- The **simultaneous comparison** = the parallel comparators.
- The **decision of which stick is the tallest match** = the priority encoder producing the binary output.

The conversion happens in **one shot**, hence the name "**Flash**" — as instantaneous as a camera flash.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol | Remarks |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------|
| Reference Voltage | **$V_{ref}$** | Defines full-scale input range |
| Resolution (n) | **8, 10, or 12 bits** typical in biomedical apps | Higher bits ⇒ exponentially more comparators |
| Number of Comparators | **$2^n - 1$** | Architectural bottleneck of Flash ADC |
| Conversion Time | **Single clock cycle** | ~ns range for high-speed designs |
| LSB Size | **$\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$** | Quantization step |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** A **10-bit** Flash ADC requires **1023 comparators**, and a **12-bit** one needs **4095 comparators** — this is why Flash ADCs are typically limited to **8 bits or fewer** in practical biomedical hardware.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The Flash ADC architecture consists of **three functional blocks** working in parallel:

### Step 1: Resistor Ladder Network (Reference Divider)
- A series string of **$2^n$ equal resistors** is connected between $V_{ref}$ and ground.
- The total resistance divides the reference voltage into **$2^n$ equally spaced levels**.
- Each tap provides a unique reference voltage $V_{ref,k}$ to one comparator.

### Step 2: Bank of Comparators (Parallel Decision)
- Each of the **$2^n - 1$ comparators** receives:
  - The **analog input** $V_{in}$ at its non-inverting (+) input.
  - A **reference voltage** $V_{ref,k}$ at its inverting (−) input.
- A comparator outputs logic **HIGH (1)** when $V_{in} > V_{ref,k}$, else **LOW (0)**.
- The collective output is a **thermometer code** (a run of 1s followed by 0s).

### Step 3: Priority Encoder (Binary Conversion)
- The thermometer code is fed into a priority encoder.
- The encoder detects the **highest-order '1'** in the thermometer string.
- It produces a corresponding **n-bit binary word** as the digital output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" matters:** The use of a priority encoder is what makes the converter truly **single-cycle**. It does not iterate through bits sequentially like SAR or ramp-type ADCs.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units / Constraint |
|---|----------------------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | $N_c = 2^n - 1$ | Number of comparators for n-bit resolution | $N_c$ is a count, dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Quantization step (LSB size) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $V_{ref,k} = \left( k + 0.5 \right) \cdot \Delta V$, for $k = 0, 1, \dots, 2^n - 2$ | Reference voltage at the k-th comparator | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $f_{max} \le \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot t_{prop}}$ | Maximum sampling frequency (Nyquist) | Hz |
| 5 | $V_{in,FS} = \left( 2^n - 1 \right) \cdot \Delta V$ | Full-scale input voltage | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $\text{SNR} \approx 6.02n + 1.76$ | Theoretical SNR (in dB) | dB |
| 7 | $P_d = t_{comp} + t_{enc}$ | Conversion delay (propagation) | seconds (s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule:** Always quote the comparator count as $\mathbf{2^n - 1}$ (NOT $2^n$). The 1 is reserved for the most significant reference (full-scale comparison) and the 0 is ground. The 1024th reference at $V_{ref}$ is never needed because the input cannot exceed it.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flash ADCs are the **workhorse of high-speed biomedical signal acquisition**:

- **Digital Oscilloscopes & Patient Monitors** — capture high-frequency arterial pressure spikes, defibrillator waveforms.
- **Digital Storage Oscilloscopes** — used in ECG machine front-ends for **QRS complex** detection.
- **CT Scanner Front-Ends** — digitize X-ray detector signals at sub-microsecond latency.
- **Radar & LIDAR** — though biomedical, cross-disciplinary applications of Flash ADC concepts.
- **Pulse Oximeter Signal Chains** — high-speed sampling of photoplethysmogram (PPG) signals.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The reason Flash ADCs are **not used in low-power wearable biomedical devices** (like Holter monitors) is the **exponential power consumption** of the comparator array — a typical biomedical trade-off question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Comparators Required

**Given:** An n-bit Flash ADC must divide the reference range $V_{ref}$ into $2^n$ discrete quantization intervals.

**Step 1 — Reference Tap Count:**
A resistor ladder with $2^n$ resistors creates $2^n$ voltage levels (including ground and $V_{ref}$ itself). However, only the levels strictly *between* ground and $V_{ref}$ are useful for comparison.

**Step 2 — Subtraction of Endpoints:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 2^n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification with n = 3 (3-bit example):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 2^3 - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7 \text{ comparators}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification with n = 8 (typical biomedical example):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 2^8 - 1 = 256 - 1 = 255 \text{ comparators}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the exponential hardware burden of Flash architecture.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Resolution (LSB Size) $\Delta V$

**Given:** Reference voltage $V_{ref}$ distributed across $2^n$ equal increments.

**Step 1 — Equal Distribution Assumption:**
Each resistor in the ladder is identical: $R_1 = R_2 = \dots = R_{2^n} = R$. The total ladder resistance is $2^n \cdot R$.

**Step 2 — Voltage Across Each Resistor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_R &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identification with LSB:**
Since each resistor drop equals the smallest distinguishable voltage step, this is by definition the **LSB size**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:**
For a **3-bit** Flash ADC with $V_{ref} = 8 \text{ V}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{8 \text{ V}}{2^3} = \frac{8}{8} = 1 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

So each comparator fires at every **1 V** step.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reference Voltage at the k-th Comparator

**Given:** The k-th comparator (counting from $k = 0$ at ground) sees a voltage divider of $(k+1)$ resistors from the top tap.

**Step 1 — Position of k-th Tap:**
The k-th tap sits $(k+1)$ resistors below $V_{ref}$, i.e., at the bottom of resistor $R_{k+1}$.

**Step 2 — Voltage Divider Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ref,k} &= V_{ref} - (k+1) \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \\[4pt]
V_{ref,k} &= V_{ref} \left( 1 - \frac{k+1}{2^n} \right) \\[4pt]
V_{ref,k} &= V_{ref} \cdot \frac{2^n - (k+1)}{2^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Alternative Mid-Rise Form (KTU preferred):**
The reference is often placed at the **midpoint of the quantization interval** to minimize comparator offset error:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ref,k} &= \left( k + 0.5 \right) \cdot \Delta V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification with n = 3, $V_{ref} = 8 \text{ V}$, $k = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ref,0} &= (0 + 0.5) \cdot 1 = 0.5 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the midpoint between 0 V and 1 V in the LSB grid.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: 3-bit Flash ADC Truth Behaviour

For a **3-bit Flash ADC** with $V_{ref} = 8 \text{ V}$, $\Delta V = 1 \text{ V}$:

| Input Range $V_{in}$ (V) | Comparator Outputs (C7..C0) | Thermometer Code | Binary Output (B2 B1 B0) |
|----------------------------|------------------------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| $0.0 \le V_{in} < 0.5$ | 0000000 | 0000000 | 000 |
| $0.5 \le V_{in} < 1.5$ | 0000001 | 0000001 | 001 |
| $1.5 \le V_{in} < 2.5$ | 0000011 | 0000011 | 010 |
| $2.5 \le V_{in} < 3.5$ | 0000111 | 0000111 | 011 |
| $3.5 \le V_{in} < 4.5$ | 0001111 | 0001111 | 100 |
| $4.5 \le V_{in} < 5.5$ | 0011111 | 0011111 | 101 |
| $5.5 \le V_{in} < 6.5$ | 0111111 | 0111111 | 110 |
| $6.5 \le V_{in} \le 7.5$ | 1111111 | 1111111 | 111 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading:** Notice how the thermometer code exhibits a **monotonic transition** — 1s "bubble up" from LSB to MSB as the input rises. This monotonicity is the key property exploited by the priority encoder.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flash ADC — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the parallel data-flow architecture of a 3-bit Flash ADC, showing the resistor ladder, comparator bank, and priority encoder with their interconnections.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vref["Vref DC Source"]
    Gnd["Ground GND"]
    Vin["Analog Input Vin"]
    RL["Resistor Ladder Network 2 to the power n equal resistors"]
    CB["Comparator Bank 2 to the power n minus 1 comparators"]
    TC["Thermometer Code Output 2 to the power n minus 1 bits"]
    PE["Priority Encoder Combinational Logic"]
    Dout["Digital Output n bit binary word"]

    Vref --> RL
    Gnd --> RL
    RL -- "Reference Voltages Vref_k" --> CB
    Vin -- "Common Analog Input" --> CB
    CB -- "Parallel Comparator Outputs" --> TC
    TC --> PE
    PE --> Dout

    subgraph ReferenceNetwork["REFERENCE NETWORK"]
        Vref
        Gnd
        RL
    end

    subgraph ParallelDecision["PARALLEL DECISION STAGE"]
        CB
        TC
    end

    subgraph EncodingStage["BINARY ENCODING STAGE"]
        PE
        Dout
    end
```

## 4.2 Detailed 3-bit Flash ADC Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Vref3["Vref = 8 Volts"]
    R1["R = 1 kilo ohm"]
    R2["R = 1 kilo ohm"]
    R3["R = 1 kilo ohm"]
    R4["R = 1 kilo ohm"]
    R5["R = 1 kilo ohm"]
    R6["R = 1 kilo ohm"]
    R7["R = 1 kilo ohm"]
    R8["R = 1 kilo ohm"]
    Gnd3["Ground 0 Volts"]
    C0["Comparator 0 Threshold 0.5 V"]
    C1["Comparator 1 Threshold 1.5 V"]
    C2["Comparator 2 Threshold 2.5 V"]
    C3["Comparator 3 Threshold 3.5 V"]
    C4["Comparator 4 Threshold 4.5 V"]
    C5["Comparator 5 Threshold 5.5 V"]
    C6["Comparator 6 Threshold 6.5 V"]
    VinNode["Analog Input Vin"]
    PE3["3 bit Priority Encoder"]
    Out3["Digital Output B2 B1 B0"]

    Vref3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5
    R5 --> R6
    R6 --> R7
    R7 --> R8
    R8 --> Gnd3

    R1 -. "0.5 V" .-> C0
    R2 -. "1.5 V" .-> C1
    R3 -. "2.5 V" .-> C2
    R4 -. "3.5 V" .-> C3
    R5 -. "4.5 V" .-> C4
    R6 -. "5.5 V" .-> C5
    R7 -. "6.5 V" .-> C6

    VinNode --> C0
    VinNode --> C1
    VinNode --> C2
    VinNode --> C3
    VinNode --> C4
    VinNode --> C5
    VinNode --> C6

    C0 --> PE3
    C1 --> PE3
    C2 --> PE3
    C3 --> PE3
    C4 --> PE3
    C5 --> PE3
    C6 --> PE3
    PE3 --> Out3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Each comparator in the lower diagram receives $V_{in}$ on its non-inverting input (+) and a unique threshold on its inverting input (−). The first comparator to flip from 0→1 sets the binary output through the priority encoder.

---

## 4.3 Functional Operation Timeline (Waveform-Style Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User / Sensor
    participant V as Vin (Analog)
    participant L as Resistor Ladder
    participant K as Comparator Bank
    participant E as Priority Encoder
    participant O as Binary Output

    U->>V: Apply analog sample at time t
    V->>L: Distribute Vref across ladder
    L->>K: Provide 2^n - 1 reference voltages in parallel
    V->>K: Provide Vin simultaneously
    K->>K: All comparators evaluate within t_prop
    K->>E: Generate thermometer code vector
    E->>O: Encode highest 1 position to n-bit binary
    O-->>U: Stable digital word within one clock cycle
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define Flash ADC. Why is it called a "parallel" ADC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Flash ADC** is a high-speed analog-to-digital converter that uses a parallel bank of comparators to perform conversion in a **single clock cycle**. It is called a "parallel" ADC because all comparators operate **simultaneously** on the same input voltage, rather than sequentially as in successive approximation or dual-slope ADCs. For an n-bit Flash ADC, **$2^n - 1$ comparators** are required, making it the fastest but most hardware-intensive ADC architecture.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: For a 6-bit Flash ADC with $V_{ref} = 5 \text{ V}$, calculate (i) the number of comparators and (ii) the LSB size.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Number of comparators:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 2^n - 1 = 2^6 - 1 = 64 - 1 = 63
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) LSB size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{64} = 0.078125 \text{ V} \approx 78.13 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Flash ADC. A 5-bit Flash ADC is used in a biomedical instrument with $V_{ref} = 4 \text{ V}$.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks:**
**Explain the block diagram and working of a Flash ADC.**

**Model Solution:**

A Flash ADC consists of three main blocks:

1. **Resistor Ladder Network:** A series of $2^n$ equal resistors connected between $V_{ref}$ and ground. For a 5-bit ADC, this means $2^5 = 32$ resistors producing 32 equally spaced reference voltages.

2. **Comparator Bank:** $2^5 - 1 = 31$ high-speed comparators receive the analog input $V_{in}$ on their non-inverting terminal and a unique reference voltage on their inverting terminal. Each comparator outputs logic HIGH if $V_{in}$ exceeds its reference.

3. **Priority Encoder:** The 31-bit thermometer code from the comparators is fed to a priority encoder which produces the 5-bit binary equivalent of the input voltage.

**Working:** When an analog input $V_{in}$ is applied, all 31 comparators evaluate simultaneously. The comparators whose threshold is less than $V_{in}$ output 1, while the rest output 0, forming a thermometer code. The priority encoder detects the position of the highest 1 and produces the corresponding binary output. The entire conversion occurs within **one clock cycle**, limited only by comparator propagation delay.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks:**
**Calculate (i) the LSB size, (ii) the full-scale input voltage, and (iii) the reference voltage at the 10th comparator.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) LSB size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{4 \text{ V}}{2^5} = \frac{4}{32} = 0.125 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Full-scale input voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in,FS} &= (2^n - 1) \cdot \Delta V = 31 \cdot 0.125 = 3.875 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Reference voltage at 10th comparator (using mid-rise formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ref,10} &= (10 + 0.5) \cdot \Delta V = 10.5 \cdot 0.125 = 1.3125 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Compare different ADC architectures and derive the comparator count and resolution formula for a Flash ADC. State two advantages and two disadvantages.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks:**
**Compare Flash, SAR, and Dual-Slope ADCs (any three parameters).**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Flash ADC | SAR ADC | Dual-Slope ADC |
|-----------|-----------|---------|----------------|
| Conversion Speed | Fastest (single cycle) | Medium (n cycles) | Slowest (thousands of cycles) |
| Hardware Complexity | Very High ($2^n - 1$ comparators) | Moderate (1 DAC + 1 comparator) | Low (1 integrator + 1 counter) |
| Resolution | Low (typically 6–8 bits) | High (12–18 bits) | Very High (up to 24 bits) |
| Power Consumption | High | Medium | Low |
| Cost | Expensive | Moderate | Cheap |
| Biomedical Use | Oscilloscopes, fast front-ends | Multi-channel ECG | Digital multimeters |

**Two Advantages of Flash ADC:**
1. **Fastest conversion speed** — suitable for high-frequency biomedical signals.
2. **Deterministic latency** — single clock cycle, ideal for real-time applications.

**Two Disadvantages of Flash ADC:**
1. **Exponential hardware growth** — a 10-bit ADC needs 1023 comparators.
2. **High power consumption and cost** — not suitable for portable/wearable devices.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks:**
**Derive the comparator count and resolution formula for an n-bit Flash ADC.**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparator count derivation:**
A resistor ladder with $2^n$ equal resistors produces $2^n + 1$ voltage levels (including endpoints). However, the levels at exactly ground and $V_{ref}$ are not used as comparator thresholds because the input cannot exceed $V_{ref}$ meaningfully. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 2^n + 1 - 2 = 2^n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resolution derivation:**
Each of the $2^n$ equal resistors drops an identical voltage equal to $V_{ref}/2^n$. This drop is the smallest voltage step that can be resolved, i.e., the LSB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is called the **resolution** of the Flash ADC. For example, an 8-bit Flash ADC with $V_{ref} = 5 \text{ V}$ has $\Delta V = 5/256 = 19.53 \text{ mV}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write $2^n$ comparators instead of $2^n - 1$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $V_{ref}$ with the full-scale input voltage. The full-scale voltage is $(2^n - 1) \cdot \Delta V$, not $V_{ref}$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting units in the final answer (always write **V** or **mV**). **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not drawing the resistor ladder with proper labeling in block diagrams. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing "Flash ADC uses successive approximation" — wrong! Flash uses **parallel** comparison, not sequential. **Lose 2 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Flash ADC = parallel comparator-based ADC with single-cycle conversion.
- **Comparator count:** $N_c = 2^n - 1$ (NOT $2^n$).
- **Resolution (LSB):** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$.
- **Full-scale input:** $V_{in,FS} = (2^n - 1) \cdot \Delta V$.
- **Reference at k-th comparator:** $V_{ref,k} = (k + 0.5) \cdot \Delta V$ (mid-rise form).
- **Conversion time:** One clock cycle — limited by $t_{prop,comp} + t_{prop,encoder}$.
- **Thermometer code:** 1s bubble up monotonically from LSB to MSB.
- **Speed vs. Resolution trade-off:** Doubling resolution **quadruples** the hardware.
- **Power:** High — proportional to $2^n$ comparators switching simultaneously.
- **Biomedical relevance:** Used in **high-speed front-ends** (oscilloscopes, CT scanners, pulse oximeters).
- **Not preferred for:** Wearable / long-term monitoring due to power constraints.
- **SNR formula:** $\text{SNR} \approx 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$.
- **Always remember the three blocks:** **Resistor Ladder → Comparator Bank → Priority Encoder**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R2R Ladder DAC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** R2R Ladder DAC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **R-2R Ladder DAC (Resistor Ladder Network Digital-to-Analog Converter)** is a current-summing type digital-to-analog converter that uses a network of only **two resistor values** — namely $R$ and $2R$ — arranged in a repeating ladder topology. Each bit of the digital input word controls an SPDT switch that either connects a $2R$ shunt leg to a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ (logic 1) or to analog ground (logic 0). The binary-weighted currents produced at the ladder nodes are summed at the inverting input of an op-amp configured as a current-to-voltage (I-to-V) converter, producing a proportional analog output voltage.

The generic output voltage expression for an $n$-bit R-2R Ladder DAC is given by:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n} = V_{ref} \cdot \left( \frac{B_{n-1}}{2} + \frac{B_{n-2}}{4} + \cdots + \frac{B_1}{2^{n-1}} + \frac{B_0}{2^n} \right)$$

where $D$ is the decimal equivalent of the binary input word and $B_i \in \{0,1\}$.

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Tapering Water Faucet" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a row of water taps connected to a common pipe, where each successive tap delivers **exactly half the flow** of the tap before it. If you could instantly turn any tap on (logic 1) or off (logic 0), the total water collected in a bucket would always be a precise binary-weighted combination of the base flow.

- The **first tap** (MSB) pours a full glass.
- The **second tap** pours **half** a glass.
- The **third tap** pours a **quarter** glass.
- The **fourth tap** (LSB) pours an **eighth** of a glass.

Whichever taps you open combine additively to fill the bucket — and that bucket level is your **analog output**. The R-2R Ladder does exactly this with **electric current** instead of water, using a clever resistor network where the natural property of the ladder halves the current at every rung.

## Why Only R and 2R?

- **R-2R is the resistor equivalent of a binary place-value system.** At every node of the ladder, the Thevenin equivalent resistance looking left or right is always exactly $R$. This property forces the current to divide in a 1:1 ratio, halving at each rung.
- **Only two resistor values need to be manufactured** (in a 2:1 ratio), which is far easier to fabricate in monolithic ICs than the wildly different values required by a pure binary-weighted resistor DAC (e.g., $R, 2R, 4R, 8R, \ldots, 2^{n-1}R$).

## Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Resolution (Step Size):** $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ — the smallest analog change the DAC can produce.
> - **Full-Scale Output (FSO):** $V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{2^n - 1}{2^n} \approx V_{ref}$.
> - **Percentage Resolution:** $\dfrac{1}{2^n} \times 100\,\%$.
> - **Settling Time:** typically **100 ns to 1 μs** for commercial R-2R DACs — fast enough for biomedical signal generation.

## Standard Resistor Values Used

- $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ is a common textbook value.
- $2R = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$.
- For high-speed designs, $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ to $5\,\text{k}\Omega$ is preferred to reduce RC time constants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core "Why" Behind R-2R Operation

The R-2R ladder is **self-similar** — every 2-bit sub-section of the ladder looks identical to the whole ladder when viewed from its rightmost node. This recursive structure is the secret to the binary division of currents.

**Step-by-step logic of current division:**

1. **At any node**, the vertical leg contains a resistor of value $2R$ that connects either to $V_{ref}$ or to ground through the SPDT switch.
2. **The horizontal series resistors** between nodes have value $R$.
3. **Looking from any node into the rest of the ladder** (excluding that node's own $2R$ leg), the equivalent resistance is **always $R$**.
4. **Therefore, the node sits at the midpoint of a divider** between the switch voltage and the next-node voltage — the voltage halves at every node.
5. **The current through each $2R$ leg** is therefore proportional to the bit weight: $I_i = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2^{i+1}}$ for bit $B_i$.

## 2.2 Working Principle (Operational Walkthrough)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Sequence of an R-2R Ladder DAC:**

- **Step 1 — Switch Action:** Each bit $B_i$ drives an SPDT switch. Logic 1 routes the corresponding $2R$ leg to $V_{ref}$; logic 0 routes it to analog ground.
- **Step 2 — Current Generation:** A current $I_i$ flows through each $2R$ leg connected to $V_{ref}$. The current is weighted by the ladder so that $I_0 = I_{ref}/2$, $I_1 = I_{ref}/4$, $\ldots$, $I_{n-1} = I_{ref}/2^n$.
- **Step 3 — Summation:** All bit currents are summed at the virtual-ground (summing junction) of an inverting op-amp.
- **Step 4 — I-to-V Conversion:** The op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f$ converts the sum of currents to a proportional output voltage: $V_{out} = -R_f \cdot \sum I_i$.
- **Step 5 — Output:** The resulting $V_{out}$ is an analog replica of the digital input word.

## 2.3 Role of the Op-Amp I-to-V Stage

The op-amp holds its inverting input at **virtual ground** (0 V). This is essential because:

- It ensures that **all $2R$ legs effectively see the same reference potential** (either $V_{ref}$ or 0 V), independent of switch position.
- It prevents the ladder from loading the reference source.
- The feedback resistor $R_f$ (typically chosen equal to $R$) sets the scale factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical tip for KTU lab:** Always use a low-offset, low-bias-current op-amp such as the **μA741** or **LM358** in the I-to-V stage to minimize DC errors when using the PCBML408 trainer kit.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Output voltage (general $n$-bit) | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ | Volts (V); $D$ = decimal input value |
| 2 | Output voltage (expanded) | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \dfrac{B_i}{2^{i+1}}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Resolution / Step size | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Full-scale output | $V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{2^n - 1}{2^n}$ | Volts (V); $\approx V_{ref}$ |
| 5 | Percentage resolution | $\% \text{Res} = \dfrac{1}{2^n} \times 100$ | Dimensionless percent |
| 6 | MSB current (with $R_f = R$) | $I_{MSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2R}$ | Amperes (A) |
| 7 | Bit-$i$ current | $I_i = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2R} \cdot \dfrac{B_i}{2^i}$ | Amperes (A) |
| 8 | Total ladder current | $I_{tot} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2R} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^{n-1}}$ | Amperes (A) |
| 9 | Op-amp output (inverting) | $V_{out} = -R_f \cdot I_{tot}$ | Volts (V); $R_f$ in $\Omega$ |
| 10 | Equivalent resistance at any node | $R_{eq} = R$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) — key property |
| 11 | Number of resistors required | $2n + 1$ | Count (e.g., 4-bit needs 9 resistors) |
| 12 | Settling time (typical) | $t_s = 0.1\,\mu s$ to $1\,\mu s$ | Seconds (s) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation pitfall:** In KTU answer scripts, **never** write absolute value bars using the `$\vert$` symbol ambiguously — use $\vert x \vert$ only when you mean mathematical absolute value. For the magnitude of $V_{out}$, prefer phrasing like "magnitude of $V_{out}$" to avoid confusion with the bit-weight vertical bar notation.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering (and Biomedical Context)

- **Biomedical Signal Generation:** Function generators used in ECG/EEG simulators often use R-2R DACs to produce calibrated analog waveforms.
- **Programmable Stimulators:** Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulators (TENS) and cardiac pacemakers use DACs to set stimulus amplitude digitally.
- **Patient Monitoring Calibration:** Automated calibration of analog front-ends in ICU monitors.
- **MRI Gradient Control:** Precise DACs set gradient coil currents in MRI systems.
- **Hearing Aids & Cochlear Implants:** Digital-to-analog conversion for audio output stages.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Robotic surgical arms use DAC-driven actuators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Output Voltage for a 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the closed-form expression for $V_{out}$ of a 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC with reference voltage $V_{ref}$ and feedback resistor $R_f = R$.

### Step 1 — Identify the four bit positions and their weights

The 4-bit digital word is $B_3\,B_2\,B_1\,B_0$, where:

- $B_3$ = **MSB** (Most Significant Bit), weight $\tfrac{1}{2}$
- $B_2$ = weight $\tfrac{1}{4}$
- $B_1$ = weight $\tfrac{1}{8}$
- $B_0$ = **LSB** (Least Significant Bit), weight $\tfrac{1}{16}$

### Step 2 — Determine the current contribution of each bit

The current through the $2R$ leg associated with bit $B_i$ is given by the ladder action (the current halves at every node moving rightward from MSB to LSB). The MSB contributes the largest current:

$$I_{MSB} = I_3 = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \cdot B_3$$

The next bit (B2) sees its Thevenin source divided by 2 because of the series $R$ and the parallel equivalent $R$ at the next node, hence its current contribution is halved:

$$I_2 = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \cdot \frac{B_2}{2} = \frac{V_{ref}}{4R} \cdot B_2$$

Continuing the halving at each rung:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_{ref}}{8R} \cdot B_1$$

$$I_0 = \frac{V_{ref}}{16R} \cdot B_0$$

### Step 3 — Sum all bit currents at the op-amp inverting node

By Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the virtual-ground summing junction:

$$I_{total} = I_3 + I_2 + I_1 + I_0$$

Substituting the individual bit currents:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \cdot B_3 + \frac{V_{ref}}{4R} \cdot B_2 + \frac{V_{ref}}{8R} \cdot B_1 + \frac{V_{ref}}{16R} \cdot B_0 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \left( B_3 + \frac{B_2}{2} + \frac{B_1}{4} + \frac{B_0}{8} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the op-amp I-to-V conversion

For the inverting amplifier with feedback resistor $R_f$:

$$V_{out} = -R_f \cdot I_{total}$$

Choosing $R_f = R$ (a standard choice for unity scale factor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= -R \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \left( B_3 + \frac{B_2}{2} + \frac{B_1}{4} + \frac{B_0}{8} \right) \\[6pt]
&= -\frac{V_{ref}}{2} \left( B_3 + \frac{B_2}{2} + \frac{B_1}{4} + \frac{B_0}{8} \right) \\[6pt]
&= -V_{ref} \left( \frac{B_3}{2} + \frac{B_2}{4} + \frac{B_1}{8} + \frac{B_0}{16} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Generalize to $n$-bit form

Replacing the index $i$ from 0 (LSB) to $n-1$ (MSB):

$$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{B_i}{2^{i+1}} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The negative sign is characteristic of the inverting configuration. In many practical R-2R DACs, a second inverting buffer is added to obtain a positive output: $V_{out} = +V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC has $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$, $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. Find the output voltage for the digital input $B_3 B_2 B_1 B_0 = 1011_2$.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert binary to decimal:**

$$D = 1011_2 = (1 \times 2^3) + (0 \times 2^2) + (1 \times 2^1) + (1 \times 2^0) = 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11$$

**Step 2 — Apply the general formula:**

$$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n} = -5 \cdot \frac{11}{2^4} = -5 \cdot \frac{11}{16}$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= -5 \cdot 0.6875 \\[4pt]
V_{out} &= -3.4375\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify by expanding the bit weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= -5 \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{0}{4} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{16} \right) \\[4pt]
&= -5 \left( 0.5 + 0 + 0.125 + 0.0625 \right) \\[4pt]
&= -5 \times 0.6875 = -3.4375\,\text{V} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the resolution:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{16} = 0.3125\,\text{V per LSB}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the full-scale output:**

$$V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n} = 5 \cdot \frac{15}{16} = 4.6875\,\text{V}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bit Weightage for 4-bit R-2R Ladder

| Input $B_3 B_2 B_1 B_0$ | Decimal $D$ | $V_{out} = -5 \cdot D/16$ (V) | Step from Previous |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0000 | 0  | 0.0000  | — |
| 0001 | 1  | -0.3125 | 1 LSB |
| 0010 | 2  | -0.6250 | 1 LSB |
| 0011 | 3  | -0.9375 | 1 LSB |
| 0100 | 4  | -1.2500 | 1 LSB |
| 0101 | 5  | -1.5625 | 1 LSB |
| 0110 | 6  | -1.8750 | 1 LSB |
| 0111 | 7  | -2.1875 | 1 LSB |
| 1000 | 8  | -2.5000 | 1 LSB |
| 1001 | 9  | -2.8125 | 1 LSB |
| 1010 | 10 | -3.1250 | 1 LSB |
| **1011** | **11** | **-3.4375** | **1 LSB** |
| 1100 | 12 | -3.7500 | 1 LSB |
| 1101 | 13 | -4.0625 | 1 LSB |
| 1110 | 14 | -4.3750 | 1 LSB |
| 1111 | 15 | -4.6875 | 1 LSB |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Functional Signal-Flow Chart — Bit-to-Voltage Conversion

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Start: Apply 4-bit Digital Word] --> P2[Each Bit Drives an SPDT Switch]
    P2 --> P3{Bit equals 1?}
    P3 -->|Yes| P4[Connect 2R Leg to Vref]
    P3 -->|No| P5[Connect 2R Leg to Ground]
    P4 --> P6[Ladder Halves Current at Each Node]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Sum All Weighted Currents at Virtual Ground]
    P7 --> P8[Op-Amp Converts Sum to Vout equals minus Rf times I total]
    P8 --> P9[Output Analog Voltage Appears at Vout Terminal]
    P9 --> P10[Compare with Theoretical Value]
    P10 --> P11[Compute Percentage Error]
    P11 --> P12[Record in Observation Table]
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    N0[Node 0 Looking Right] --> N1[2R Leg in Parallel with R plus R]
    N1 --> N2[2R Leg in Parallel with 2R]
    N2 --> N3[Equivalent equals R]
    N3 --> N4[Therefore node 0 always sees R to ground]
    N4 --> N5[Current splits 1 to 1 at every node]
    N5 --> N6[Binary weight assignment achieved]
```
