<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

### Question 1: Define R-2R Ladder DAC. State any two advantages over Binary Weighted Resistor DAC.

**Model Answer:**

> An R-2R Ladder DAC is a digital-to-analog converter that uses a network of only two resistor values, $R$ and $2R$, arranged in a ladder configuration. The binary-weighted digital input controls SPDT switches that route the $2R$ legs to $V_{ref}$ or ground, and the resulting bit-weighted currents are summed by an op-amp to produce the analog output $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot D / 2^n$.

**Two advantages over Binary Weighted Resistor DAC:**

1. **Only two resistor values** ($R$ and $2R$) are required regardless of the number of bits, whereas Binary Weighted DAC needs $n$ different values spanning a range of $2^{n-1}:1$ (e.g., for 8-bit DAC the range is 128:1).
2. **Easier IC fabrication and higher accuracy** — the 2:1 ratio between resistors is much easier to control in monolithic manufacturing than wide-ratio resistors, leading to better linearity and lower temperature-induced errors.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Question 2: A 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC uses $V_{ref} = 10\,\text{V}$. Calculate the resolution and the analog output for the input $1001_2$.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute resolution (step size):**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^4} = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Convert binary to decimal:**

$$D = 1001_2 = 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 = 8 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 9$$

**Step 3 — Compute output voltage (magnitude):**

$$\vert V_{out} \vert = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n} = 10 \cdot \frac{9}{16} = 5.625\,\text{V}$$

(In inverting configuration: $V_{out} = -5.625\,\text{V}$.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 1**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of a 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the output voltage.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**2. Working Principle:**

- The R-2R ladder network is a **current-summing network** in which only two resistor values, $R$ and $2R$, are used.
- The four-bit digital input $B_3 B_2 B_1 B_0$ controls four SPDT switches. When a bit is **1**, the corresponding $2R$ shunt leg is connected to $V_{ref}$; when the bit is **0**, the $2R$ leg is connected to **ground**.
- The ladder's unique topology ensures that **the equivalent resistance at any node looking into the network is always $R$**. As a result, the current through each $2R$ leg is binary-weighted: the current through the $B_3$ leg is the largest, and each subsequent bit contributes exactly half the current of the previous bit.
- The currents from all active $2R$ legs are summed at the **inverting (virtual-ground) node** of an op-amp.
- The op-amp, with feedback resistor $R_f = R$, performs **current-to-voltage conversion**, yielding the analog output.

**3. Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= I_3 + I_2 + I_1 + I_0 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} B_3 + \frac{V_{ref}}{4R} B_2 + \frac{V_{ref}}{8R} B_1 + \frac{V_{ref}}{16R} B_0 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \left( B_3 + \frac{B_2}{2} + \frac{B_1}{4} + \frac{B_0}{8} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Op-amp I-to-V conversion with $R_f = R$:

$$V_{out} = -R \cdot I_{total} = -V_{ref} \left( \frac{B_3}{2} + \frac{B_2}{4} + \frac{B_1}{8} + \frac{B_0}{16} \right) = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}$$

**4. Key Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}}$$

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] An 8-bit R-2R Ladder DAC is constructed with $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$ and $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. For the digital input $10110100_2$, calculate:
   (i) Decimal value of input
   (ii) Output voltage
   (iii) Resolution
   (iv) Full-scale output

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Decimal value of input:**

$$D = 10110100_2 = 128 + 32 + 16 + 4 = 180$$

**(ii) Output voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n} = -5 \cdot \frac{180}{2^8} \\[4pt]
&= -5 \cdot \frac{180}{256} = -5 \cdot 0.703125 \\[4pt]
&= -3.515625\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Resolution (Step size):**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{256} = 0.01953\,\text{V} = 19.53\,\text{mV per LSB}$$

**(iv) Full-scale output (for input $11111111_2 = 255$):**

$$
V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n} = 5 \cdot \frac{255}{256} = 4.98047\,\text{V}
$$

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

#### (a) [7 Marks] With the help of a circuit description, explain how the R-2R Ladder DAC achieves binary-weighted current division. Why is this property not possible in a simple binary-weighted resistor DAC?

**Model Solution:**

**1. Circuit description (R-2R Ladder):**

- A **horizontal series chain** of $R$-valued resistors connects all bit nodes.
- At each node, a **vertical $2R$ leg** drops to an SPDT switch.
- The chain **terminates** with an $R$ to ground (termination resistor), preserving the symmetry.

**2. How binary division is achieved:**

- At any node, the vertical $2R$ leg is **in parallel with the rest of the ladder**.
- The rest of the ladder, looking right from that node, presents an **equivalent resistance of $R$** (this can be proven by repeated application of the R-2R self-similarity property, terminating in $2R \parallel 2R = R$).
- Therefore, at every node, a $2R$ leg is effectively in parallel with a Thevenin source impedance of $R$ — a 1:1 current divider.
- The current through the $2R$ leg is **halved** at every successive node, naturally yielding binary weighting.

**3. Why binary-weighted resistor DAC cannot match this:**

- A binary-weighted resistor DAC uses resistors of values $R, 2R, 4R, 8R, \ldots, 2^{n-1}R$ connected to a summing node.
- The **sum of these parallel resistors changes with the input code**, so the Thevenin equivalent at the summing node is **code-dependent**, causing **non-linear distortion**.
- More critically, fabricating a **128:1 resistor ratio** (for 8-bit) with adequate matching accuracy is **practically impossible in standard IC processes** — small lithographic errors cause huge relative errors in the high-value resistors.
- The R-2R Ladder avoids both problems because the **2:1 ratio is easily fabricated**, and the **ladder's self-similar topology decouples the current weighting from the source impedance**.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Compare R-2R Ladder DAC with Binary Weighted Resistor DAC. List at least five comparison points and state one typical application of R-2R Ladder DAC in biomedical instrumentation.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| # | Parameter | R-2R Ladder DAC | Binary Weighted Resistor DAC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of resistor values | Only 2 ($R$ and $2R$) | $n$ different values |
| 2 | Range of resistor values | 2:1 ratio (easy IC fab) | $2^{n-1}$:1 ratio (hard for $n \geq 8$) |
| 3 | IC fabrication suitability | Excellent | Poor for high $n$ |
| 4 | Speed | Fast (low parasitic capacitance) | Slower (large node capacitances) |
| 5 | Temperature stability | High (matched ratios) | Lower (mismatched values drift) |
| 6 | Cost (for high $n$) | Lower | Higher |
| 7 | Output impedance | Independent of code | Code-dependent |
| 8 | Typical use | General-purpose IC DACs | Low-bit educational DACs |

**Biomedical Application:**

> The R-2R Ladder DAC is widely used in **digital ECG simulators and patient monitor calibration units**. A microcontroller generates a digital representation of a standard ECG waveform (e.g., normal sinus rhythm at 1 mV, 60 bpm), and an R-2R DAC converts this digital pattern into a precise analog voltage that drives the ECG front-end of the patient monitor under test — allowing automated verification of the monitor's amplitude, timing, and frequency-response characteristics.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in R-2R DAC answers (and how they cost marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $V_{ref}$ with $V_{CC}$:** The reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is the **precision reference** applied to the $2R$ legs, **not** the op-amp supply rail. Many students wrongly equate $V_{ref}$ to $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supplies. Always state $V_{ref}$ as the dedicated reference input.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of $V_{out}$:** The inverting op-amp configuration produces a **negative** $V_{out}$. If the question asks for the magnitude, write $\vert V_{out} \vert$; if it asks for the actual voltage, write $V_{out} = -3.4375\,\text{V}$. Skipping the sign costs 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong formula for resolution:** Resolution is $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$, **not** $V_{ref} / n$ (which is a common student error).
>
> 4. **Not labeling switches in the diagram:** KTU examiners explicitly award marks for showing the **SPDT switch position** for each bit. Always draw a switch symbol between each $2R$ leg and the $V_{ref}$/ground lines.
>
> 5. **Decimal-to-binary conversion mistakes:** When the digital input is given as decimal (e.g., $D = 11$), always **show the expanded binary expansion** $11 = 8 + 2 + 1 = 1011_2$ before plugging into the formula.
>
> 6. **Confusing "percentage resolution" with "resolution":** Percentage resolution is $\dfrac{1}{2^n} \times 100\%$, not the step size in volts. Read the question carefully.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 12 High-Yield Points for R-2R Ladder DAC — KTU 2024 Scheme**
>
> 1. **R-2R Ladder DAC** uses only **two resistor values** — $R$ and $2R$ — in a repeating ladder structure.
> 2. The **equivalent resistance at every node** looking into the ladder is **always $R$** — this is the magic property that gives binary division.
> 3. Each bit $B_i$ controls an **SPDT switch** that routes the $2R$ leg to $V_{ref}$ (bit 1) or **ground** (bit 0).
> 4. **Output formula:** $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ (inverting op-amp configuration).
> 5. **Resolution:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ — the voltage step per LSB increment.
> 6. **Full-scale output:** $V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{2^n - 1}{2^n} \approx V_{ref}$.
> 7. **Op-amp stage** acts as a **virtual-ground current-summing I-to-V converter** — keeps the ladder nodes at 0 V, ensuring linearity.
> 8. The R-2R ladder is **self-similar**: any $k$-bit sub-section is electrically identical to the whole $k$-bit ladder.
> 9. **Total resistors needed** for an $n$-bit DAC: $2n + 1$ (e.g., 4-bit → 9, 8-bit → 17).
> 10. **Advantages over Binary Weighted DAC:** only 2:1 resistor ratio, easy IC fabrication, code-independent output impedance, good temperature stability.
> 11. **Disadvantage:** requires an external op-amp; the inverting topology gives a negative output (a second inverter may be needed for positive output).
> 12. **Biomedical uses:** ECG/EEG signal simulators, programmable stimulators (TENS/pacemakers), MRI gradient control, hearing-aid audio DACs, automated patient-monitor calibration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threshold Detector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Threshold Detector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Threshold Detector** is an electronic comparator circuit that monitors a continuously varying analog input signal and produces a discrete, binary output transition (logic HIGH or LOW) precisely when the input amplitude crosses a predefined reference voltage level, known as the **Threshold Voltage (V_TH)**. In the domain of **Biomedical Electronics**, threshold detectors form the foundational front-end block for extracting clinically significant events from noisy physiological waveforms such as the **Electrocardiogram (ECG)**, **Electroencephalogram (EEG)**, and **Electromyogram (EMG)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCBML408 - Module 2):**
> A threshold detector is a signal-conditioning circuit that converts a continuous-time analog biomedical signal into a digital pulse train by comparing it against a fixed or adjustable reference voltage, enabling event detection (e.g., R-peak detection in ECG, spike detection in EEG).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **flood warning siren** installed on a river bank.

- The river water level is the **input signal (V_in)**.
- The danger mark painted on the bank wall is the **threshold (V_TH)**.
- The moment the water rises and touches the danger mark, the siren blares (output goes HIGH).
- When the water recedes below the mark, the siren stops (output goes LOW).

The siren does not care about the *exact* water level — it only cares whether the level is *above* or *below* the danger mark. This is precisely what a threshold detector does with voltage signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A threshold detector is essentially a **1-bit Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — it gives only two possible outputs: "above threshold" or "below threshold."

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Biomedical Threshold Detection

- **Threshold Voltage (V_TH):** The reference comparison level, typically in the range of **±V_sat (≈ ±12 V)** for op-amp based detectors.
- **Hysteresis Width (V_H):** The voltage gap between the upper and lower switching thresholds, measured in **volts (V)**.
- **Response Time (t_r):** Time taken to switch output state, typically **microseconds (μs)**.
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** Standard metric **> 80 dB** for instrumentation-grade op-amps.
- **Power Supply Rails:** Standard **±12 V** or **±15 V** dual supply in lab environments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

A threshold detector implemented using an **op-amp comparator (LM741 / LM324 / LM311)** operates on the following structured logic:

1. **Signal Input Stage:** The biomedical signal (e.g., amplified ECG) is fed to the **non-inverting terminal (+)** of the op-amp.
2. **Reference Application:** A precisely controlled DC reference voltage (V_REF) is applied to the **inverting terminal (−)** of the op-amp.
3. **Open-Loop Comparison:** The op-amp operates in **open-loop configuration** (no feedback), giving it effectively infinite gain (A_OL ≈ 10^5 to 10^6 V/V).
4. **Error Amplification:** The internal differential amplifier amplifies the tiny difference: **V_d = V_in − V_REF**.
5. **Saturation Output:** Since gain is enormous, even a millivolt-level difference drives the output into saturation:
   - If V_in > V_REF → Output saturates to **+V_sat** (≈ +V_CC − 2V)
   - If V_in < V_REF → Output saturates to **−V_sat** (≈ −V_EE + 2V)
6. **Digital Conditioning:** A Zener diode or Schmitt trigger stage cleans the output into standard **TTL/CMOS logic levels (0 V or 5 V)**.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Stage

- **Why open-loop?** — Closed-loop operation reduces gain, destroying the sharp switching action needed for clean threshold detection.
- **Why dual supply?** — Biomedical signals (ECG, EEG) are **bipolar** (swinging both positive and negative around baseline), requiring the comparator to handle negative excursions.
- **Why hysteresis?** — In a pure comparator, noise riding on the input causes **multiple false triggers** (chattering). A Schmitt trigger introduces **hysteresis** to suppress this.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A circuit *without* hysteresis is a **Comparator**. A circuit *with* hysteresis is a **Schmitt Trigger**. Both are valid threshold detectors, but only the Schmitt Trigger is noise-immune.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Boundary Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$ | Op-amp output in open-loop | Volts (V) | $A_{OL} \approx 10^5$ to $10^6$ V/V |
| $V_{o(sat)} = +V_{CC} - V_{drop}$ | Positive saturation output | Volts (V) | $V_{drop} \approx 1$ to $2$ V |
| $-V_{o(sat)} = -V_{EE} + V_{drop}$ | Negative saturation output | Volts (V) | Symmetric to above |
| $V_{UT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | Upper Threshold (Schmitt) | Volts (V) | Inverting Schmitt topology |
| $V_{LT} = -V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | Lower Threshold (Schmitt) | Volts (V) | Inverting Schmitt topology |
| $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT}$ | Hysteresis Width | Volts (V) | $V_H > V_{noise,peak}$ |
| $V_{TH} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{REF}$ | Non-inverting Schmitt threshold | Volts (V) | Used for positive feedback |
| $f_{max} = \frac{SlewRate}{2 \pi V_{pk}}$ | Maximum input signal frequency | Hertz (Hz) | $SlewRate \approx 0.5$ V/μs for LM741 |
| $t_r = \frac{\Delta V}{SlewRate}$ | Output rise time | Seconds (s) | For $\Delta V$ full swing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** When writing threshold formulas in your answer sheet, **never** write $V_H = \vert V_{UT} - V_{LT} \vert$ using the pipe symbol. Use $\left(V_{UT} - V_{LT}\right)$ explicitly to keep the markdown table parser safe.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Threshold detectors are the silent workhorses behind:

- **ECG QRS Complex Detectors** — R-peak triggers for heart-rate monitoring.
- **EEG Spike-and-Slow-Wave Detectors** — Epilepsy seizure onset detection.
- **EMG Burst Detectors** — Prosthetic limb activation triggers.
- **Pulse Oximeters** — Detecting arterial blood volume pulsations.
- **Patient Monitors in ICUs** — Alarm generation when vital signs cross safe limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Threshold Voltages

Consider a non-inverting Schmitt trigger with positive feedback through resistor R_1 from output to the non-inverting input, and a reference voltage V_REF applied through R_2.

**Step 1: Identify the feedback node.**

The non-inverting terminal voltage $V_+$ is set by the superposition of V_REF (through R_2) and $V_o$ (through R_1).

**Step 2: Apply superposition theorem.**

$$V_+ = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} + V_o \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3: Substitute saturated output states.**

The output can only take two values: $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$.

**Step 4: Upper Threshold (V_UT) — when V_o = +V_sat.**

$$V_{UT} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} + V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$\boxed{V_{UT} = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{REF} + R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

**Step 5: Lower Threshold (V_LT) — when V_o = −V_sat.**

$$V_{LT} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} + (-V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$\boxed{V_{LT} = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{REF} - R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

**Step 6: Compute Hysteresis Width (V_H).**

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT}$$

$$V_H = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{REF} + R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{REF} - R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_H = \frac{2 R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$\boxed{V_H = \frac{2 R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger

For a non-inverting Schmitt trigger, the input is applied to the non-inverting terminal and the reference is at the inverting terminal.

**Step 1: Voltage at inverting terminal.**

The inverting terminal voltage is a divider between V_REF and ground through R_1 and R_2:

$$V_- = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2: Switching condition.**

Switching occurs when $V_{in} = V_-$. Substituting the two output states:

$$\boxed{V_{UT} = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{REF} + R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{LT} = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{REF} - R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example (KTU Lab Standard Values).**

Given: $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{REF} = 2.5$ V, $V_{sat} = 12$ V.

$$V_{UT} = \frac{10 \times 2.5 + 1 \times 12}{10 + 1} = \frac{25 + 12}{11} = \frac{37}{11} = 3.36 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{LT} = \frac{10 \times 2.5 - 1 \times 12}{10 + 1} = \frac{25 - 12}{11} = \frac{13}{11} = 1.18 \text{ V}$$

$$V_H = 3.36 - 1.18 = 2.18 \text{ V}$$

This hysteresis band of **2.18 V** will reject any noise peaks smaller than this amplitude — a critical property for robust ECG R-wave detection.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Threshold Detector Topologies

| **Topology** | **Hysteresis** | **Noise Immunity** | **Output Polarity** | **KTU Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Op-amp Comparator | None | Poor (chattering) | Bipolar saturation | Lab demo only |
| Inverting Schmitt Trigger | Yes | Excellent | Inverted | EEG spike detection |
| Non-Inverting Schmitt | Yes | Excellent | Non-inverted | ECG R-peak detection |
| Window Comparator | None | Moderate | Dual output | Signal range validation |
| LM311 with strobe | None | Programmable | Open-collector | Microcontroller interfacing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "design a threshold detector for ECG," the model answer must include hysteresis. A bare comparator with no hysteresis will lose you **at least 3 marks** in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Biomedical Threshold Detector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["BIOELECTRIC SIGNAL SOURCE"] --> nodeB["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["BANDPASS FILTER 0.5 TO 40 HZ"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["OP-AMP COMPARATOR STAGE"]
    nodeE["THRESHOLD REFERENCE V REF"] --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeF["SCHMITT TRIGGER WITH HYSTERESIS"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["OUTPUT LEVEL SHIFTER TO TTL"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["DIGITAL COUNTER OR MICROCONTROLLER"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["DISPLAY OR ALARM UNIT"]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

The resulting digital pulse train can be directly fed to a microcontroller for heart-rate calculation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define a threshold detector. Mention two applications in biomedical instrumentation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A threshold detector is a comparator-based circuit that produces a logic-level output transition whenever its analog input signal crosses a predefined reference voltage level, called the threshold voltage (V_TH).

**Two biomedical applications:**
1. **ECG R-wave detection** — used to trigger heart-rate counters and pulse monitors.
2. **EEG spike detection** — used in epilepsy monitoring systems to flag abnormal high-amplitude transients.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two applications.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is hysteresis? Why is it essential in a biomedical threshold detector?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Hysteresis** is the deliberate introduction of two different switching thresholds — an **Upper Threshold (V_UT)** and a **Lower Threshold (V_LT)** — separated by a voltage gap called the **Hysteresis Width (V_H)**.

**Why essential:**
Biomedical signals such as ECG and EEG are inherently contaminated with **50/60 Hz power-line interference** and high-frequency noise. A pure comparator (no hysteresis) would respond to every noise spike, producing **false triggers** or "chattering" at the output. Hysteresis creates a **dead band** that noise cannot cross, ensuring the detector responds only to genuine physiological events. The condition is:

$$V_H > V_{noise,peak}$$

*(Award: 1.5 marks for defining hysteresis, 1.5 marks for explaining the noise-immunity necessity with the inequality.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Design-Oriented**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Design a non-inverting Schmitt trigger-based threshold detector using an LM741 op-amp to detect R-peaks in an amplified ECG signal. The ECG signal after amplification has a peak amplitude of **4 V** and rides on a baseline noise of **±0.3 V**. The op-amp is powered by **±12 V** supplies. Choose suitable resistor values to achieve a hysteresis of **2 V**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the designed values, calculate the **Upper Threshold Voltage (V_UT)** and **Lower Threshold Voltage (V_LT)**. Also determine the **maximum input frequency** that the circuit can handle, given the LM741 slew rate of **0.5 V/μs**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Design (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the topology.** Non-inverting Schmitt trigger with positive feedback. Use standard formulas:

$$V_H = \frac{2 R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2: Set V_sat.** For ±12 V supply, the LM741 saturates at approximately **V_sat = ±11 V** (after 1 V drop).

**Step 3: Choose resistor ratio.** We need $V_H = 2$ V. Let us choose $R_1 = 10$ kΩ and solve for $R_2$:

$$2 = \frac{2 \cdot R_2 \cdot 11}{10 \text{ k}\Omega + R_2}$$

$$2(10 \text{ k}\Omega + R_2) = 22 R_2$$

$$20 \text{ k}\Omega + 2 R_2 = 22 R_2$$

$$20 \text{ k}\Omega = 20 R_2$$

$$R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Set V_REF.** To centre the threshold band on the ECG R-peak of 4 V:

$$V_{REF} = \frac{V_{UT} + V_{LT}}{2} = 4 \text{ V}$$

A simple **potentiometer divider** from a stable +5 V reference can be used to generate 4 V.

**Step 5: Verify noise rejection.** With $V_H = 2$ V and noise peak of 0.3 V, the inequality $V_H > V_{noise,peak}$ is satisfied (2 > 0.3), confirming robust operation.

**Final component list:**
- Op-amp: LM741
- R_1 = 10 kΩ, R_2 = 1 kΩ
- V_REF = 4 V (from potentiometer)
- Power supply: ±12 V dual
- Optional: 5.1 V Zener at output for TTL compatibility

---

**Part (b) — Calculations (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate V_UT.**

$$V_{UT} = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{REF} + R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_{UT} = \frac{10 \times 4 + 1 \times 11}{10 + 1} = \frac{40 + 11}{11} = \frac{51}{11} = 4.636 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Calculate V_LT.**

$$V_{LT} = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{REF} - R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_{LT} = \frac{10 \times 4 - 1 \times 11}{10 + 1} = \frac{40 - 11}{11} = \frac{29}{11} = 2.636 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Verify hysteresis.**

$$V_H = 4.636 - 2.636 = 2.0 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Maximum input frequency.**

The output must swing from −11 V to +11 V, total swing $\Delta V = 22$ V.

$$t_r = \frac{\Delta V}{SlewRate} = \frac{22 \text{ V}}{0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}} = 44 \text{ μs}$$

For a sinusoidal input of peak voltage $V_{pk} = 4$ V, the maximum trackable frequency is:

$$f_{max} = \frac{SlewRate}{2 \pi V_{pk}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2 \pi \times 4} \approx 19,894 \text{ Hz}$$

Since ECG R-waves are well below 100 Hz, the LM741 is more than sufficient for this application.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Theory + Application)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the operation of a **window comparator** used as a threshold detector. How does it differ from a simple Schmitt trigger? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **nerve-action-potential signal** has an amplitude of **80 μV** and frequency content up to **1 kHz**. Design the complete signal-conditioning chain (amplifier stages) required before it can be fed to a threshold detector. Specify the gain of each stage and justify. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Window Comparator (7 Marks):**

A **window comparator** uses **two op-amp comparators** to detect whether the input signal lies *within* a defined voltage band (the "window") defined by an **Upper Threshold (V_UT)** and a **Lower Threshold (V_LT)**.

**Circuit Operation:**
- **Comparator 1:** Detects if $V_{in} > V_{UT}$ → Output U1 goes HIGH.
- **Comparator 2:** Detects if $V_{in} < V_{LT}$ → Output U2 goes HIGH.
- A **diode-AND gate** combines both outputs.

**Truth Table:**

| **V_in Range** | **U1 Output** | **U2 Output** | **Final Output** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{in} < V_{LT}$ | LOW | HIGH | HIGH (out of window low) |
| $V_{LT} < V_{in} < V_{UT}$ | LOW | LOW | LOW (inside window) |
| $V_{in} > V_{UT}$ | HIGH | LOW | HIGH (out of window high) |

**Difference from Schmitt Trigger:**

| **Parameter** | **Schmitt Trigger** | **Window Comparator** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of comparators | 1 | 2 |
| Detects | Single threshold crossing | Range validity |
| Hysteresis | Yes | No (unless added) |
| Application | R-peak detection | Battery voltage monitoring, signal range validation |

*(Award: 3 marks for circuit explanation, 2 marks for truth table, 2 marks for comparison.)*

---

**Part (b) — Signal Conditioning Chain (7 Marks):**

A nerve action potential at **80 μV** is far too small to drive a threshold detector (which typically needs at least 1–4 V input). The required total gain is:

$$A_{total} = \frac{V_{detector}}{V_{signal}} = \frac{4 \text{ V}}{80 \text{ μV}} = 50,000 \quad (94 \text{ dB})$$

**Three-Stage Cascade Design:**

**Stage 1: Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)**
- Gain $A_1 = 100$ → Output = 8 mV
- *Justification:* Provides high CMRR (> 100 dB) to reject common-mode 50 Hz interference picked up by skin electrodes.

**Stage 2: Bandpass Filter + Second Amplifier**
- Bandpass: 10 Hz to 1 kHz
- Gain $A_2 = 50$ → Output = 400 mV
- *Justification:* Filters out DC electrode offset and high-frequency thermal noise; further amplifies to a workable level.

**Stage 3: Final Non-Inverting Amplifier**
- Gain $A_3 = 10$ → Output = 4 V
- *Justification:* Brings the signal to the threshold detector's optimal input range (1–5 V).

**Total Gain Verification:**

$$A_{total} = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3 = 100 \times 50 \times 10 = 50,000 \quad \checkmark$$

This signal is now suitable for the previously designed threshold detector (V_UT = 4.636 V, V_LT = 2.636 V).

*(Award: 2 marks for gain calculation, 3 marks for stage-by-stage design with justification, 2 marks for verification.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention V_sat drop** — LM741 does not swing to the full ±12 V; it saturates at approximately ±11 V. Writing V_sat = 12 V loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing inverting and non-inverting Schmitt topologies** — The feedback resistor goes to the **non-inverting** terminal in a Schmitt trigger, not the inverting. Drawing it wrong loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating the noise-rejection condition $V_H > V_{noise,peak}$** — Examiners specifically look for this inequality in design problems. Skipping it loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Mixing up V_UT and V_LT formulas** — Memorize the sign of $V_{sat}$ in each formula. A sign error propagates through the entire calculation and is a **3-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Ignoring slew rate limitations** — In frequency-related questions, the slew rate formula $f_{max} = SlewRate / (2\pi V_{pk})$ is mandatory. Omitting it loses **2 marks**.
> 6. **Writing absolute value with `\|` symbol in tables** — This breaks the markdown table parser in KTU's online answer submission portal. Always use $\left( \cdot \right)$ for clarity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Threshold Detector** = Comparator circuit that produces a digital output when input crosses V_TH.
- **Without hysteresis** → comparator → noisy output, multiple triggers.
- **With hysteresis** → Schmitt trigger → noise-immune, single clean trigger.
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $V_H = \frac{2 R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}$
  - $V_{UT} = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{REF} + R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}$
  - $V_{LT} = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{REF} - R_2 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}$
  - $f_{max} = \frac{SlewRate}{2 \pi V_{pk}}$
- **Standard lab values:** $V_{CC} = \pm 12$ V, $V_{sat} \approx \pm 11$ V, $V_{REF}$ from potentiometer.
- **Noise rejection condition:** $V_H > V_{noise,peak}$ — always state this in design answers.
- **Biomedical applications:** ECG R-wave detection, EEG spike detection, EMG burst detection, pulse-oximeter pulse detection.
- **Topology comparison:** Simple comparator (no hysteresis) vs. Schmitt trigger (with hysteresis) vs. window comparator (range detection).
- **LM741 limitation:** Slew rate of 0.5 V/μs limits high-frequency operation; use LM318 or LF353 for > 50 kHz signals.
- **Output interfacing:** Add a Zener diode clamp (5.1 V) at the output to convert op-amp bipolar swing to TTL-compatible levels for microcontroller interfacing.
- **Always draw:** Block diagram first → circuit diagram → waveform sketch → numerical calculation. This sequence scores full marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermistor Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Thermistor Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Thermistor** (Thermal Resistor) is a type of solid-state, passive, non-linear semiconductor device whose electrical **resistance is highly sensitive to temperature changes**. It is constructed from sintered metallic oxides (such as manganese, nickel, cobalt, or iron oxides) encapsulated in a glass or epoxy coating with two terminal leads.

In the context of **Biomedical Electronics (PCBML408)**, thermistors are classified as **primary temperature transducers** because they convert a thermal input (body/fluid temperature) directly into a measurable electrical output (resistance change), making them indispensable in patient monitoring systems, incubators, blood warmers, and digital clinical thermometers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Thermistors used in biomedical applications almost exclusively belong to the **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient)** category, where resistance *decreases* as temperature *increases*. PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) types are typically reserved for overcurrent protection, not physiological sensing.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **garden water hose** in summer versus winter:
- In **winter (cold)**, the water inside becomes slightly viscous and the hose walls stiffen — water flows *less easily*. This is analogous to a thermistor at low temperature: electrons struggle to move, so **resistance is HIGH**.
- In **summer (hot)**, the hose material softens and water flows *more freely*. Analogously, at higher temperatures, more charge carriers are liberated in the semiconductor lattice, so **resistance DROPS** dramatically.

Just as you can tell the season by how easily water flows, a thermistor "tells" the circuit the temperature by how easily current passes through it.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Typical Resistance Range at 25 °C:** **1 kΩ to 100 kΩ** (commonly 10 kΩ for biomedical probes)
- **Operating Temperature Range:** **−55 °C to +150 °C** (covers the entire physiological range of 32 °C to 42 °C easily)
- **Temperature Coefficient (α):** Approximately **−3% to −6% per °C** for NTC types (compare to +0.4%/°C for platinum RTDs)
- **Beta Constant (β):** Usually between **3000 K and 5000 K** depending on the oxide formulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Thermistors are **NOT** the same as **RTDs (Resistance Temperature Detectors)**. RTDs use pure metals like platinum (PT100/PT1000) and exhibit a *linear, lower-sensitivity* response, whereas thermistors are *non-linear but extremely sensitive* — ideal for detecting the narrow ±2 °C physiological temperature window.

### 1.4 Types of Thermistors

| Type | Behavior | Biomedical Relevance |
|------|----------|----------------------|
| **NTC** | Resistance *decreases* with rising T | **Most common** — clinical probes, incubators |
| **PTC** | Resistance *increases* with rising T | Not used for sensing; used as inrush current limiters |
| **CTR** | Critical Temperature Resistor — abrupt jump | Specialized switching applications only |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Principle — "Why and How"

The conductive behavior of an NTC thermistor is governed by **semiconductor physics** rather than simple metallic conduction:

1. **Lattice Vibration Mechanism:** The thermistor body is a doped polycrystalline semiconductor. At low temperatures, most charge carriers (electrons/holes) remain bound in the valence band.
2. **Thermal Excitation:** As temperature rises, thermal energy ($k_BT$, where $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant = **1.380649 × 10⁻²³ J/K**) excites electrons across the band gap.
3. **Carrier Multiplication:** The intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$ follows an exponential Arrhenius-type dependence: $n_i \propto e^{-E_g/2k_BT}$, where $E_g$ is the bandgap energy.
4. **Conductivity Rises:** Higher $n_i$ means more mobile charge carriers → conductivity $\sigma$ rises → resistance $R$ falls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **exponential inverse relationship** is what makes thermistors ~10× more sensitive than metallic RTDs, which is why a 10 kΩ NTC thermistor can resolve body temperature to within ±0.1 °C.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description & Units |
|---|--------------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Resistance at Temperature T** | $$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$ | $R_0$ = resistance at reference $T_0$ (usually 298.15 K / 25 °C); $T$ in **Kelvin (K)**; $\beta$ in **Kelvin (K)** |
| 2 | **Steinhart-Hart (3-coefficient)** | $$\frac{1}{T} = a + b \ln(R) + c \left[\ln(R)\right]^3$$ | High-precision empirical fit; $T$ in K; $a,b,c$ are device-specific |
| 3 | **Temperature Coefficient α** | $$\alpha = \frac{1}{R_T} \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$ | Measured in **per °C or per K**; negative for NTC |
| 4 | **Beta from Two Known Points** | $$\beta = \frac{\ln(R_1/R_2)}{\left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)}$$ | Used experimentally when two (T,R) points are measured |
| 5 | **Voltage Divider Output** | $$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_s}$$ | Standard interfacing circuit; $R_s$ is the series reference resistor |
| 6 | **Kelvin ↔ Celsius** | $$T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$$ | **Always convert to Kelvin** before applying thermistor equations |
| 7 | **Self-Heating Effect** | $$P = I^2 R_T \quad ; \quad \Delta T = P \cdot \theta_{JA}$$ | Power dissipation causes sensor error; $\theta_{JA}$ is thermal resistance (°C/W) |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Patient Monitoring:** ECG/respiration modules often integrate skin-surface thermistors for core-body temperature trending.
- **Neonatal Incubators:** Maintain ±0.5 °C around 36.5 °C using NTC thermistor feedback loops.
- **Blood Oxygenators / Dialysis:** Thermistor at venous line monitors return-blood temperature to prevent hypothermia.
- **Digital Clinical Thermometers:** Disposable probe tips contain a 10 kΩ NTC thermistor with β ≈ 3950 K.

### 2.4 Limitations & Pitfalls

- **Self-Heating Error:** Current through the thermistor dissipates power and *artificially raises* its temperature — must limit current to **< 1 mA** during measurement.
- **Non-Linearity:** The exponential $R$–$T$ curve requires linearization (resistor in parallel, or Steinhart-Hart in firmware).
- **Fragility:** Glass-coated beads can crack under mechanical shock — handle probes carefully during lab sessions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental β-Equation

**Starting Point:** Semiconductor conductivity follows an Arrhenius law.

$$\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / k_B T}$$

Since resistance is inversely proportional to conductivity ($R \propto 1/\sigma$):

$$R(T) = R_\infty \cdot e^{E_a / k_B T}$$

**Step 1 — Ratio at two temperatures:**

$$\frac{R_T}{R_0} = \frac{e^{E_a / k_B T}}{e^{E_a / k_B T_0}} = e^{E_a/k_B \cdot \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Beta constant:**

$$\beta = \frac{E_a}{k_B} \quad \text{(expressed in Kelvin)}$$

**Step 3 — Combine to obtain the canonical thermistor equation:**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

This is the master formula that **every KTU numerical problem will expect you to write first**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A Typical KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem:** A thermistor has a resistance of **10 kΩ at 25 °C** and **β = 3950 K**. Compute:
> (a) Resistance at body temperature 37 °C.
> (b) Resistance at 42 °C (fever threshold).
> (c) Temperature when resistance reads 5 kΩ.

**Solution Setup:** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin.

$$T_0 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \text{ K}$$

$$T_1 = 37 + 273.15 = 310.15 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = 42 + 273.15 = 315.15 \text{ K}$$

#### (a) Resistance at 37 °C

**Step 1:** Compute the exponent.

$$\beta \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) = 3950 \times \left( \frac{1}{310.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} \right)$$

$$= 3950 \times \left( 0.0032242 - 0.0033540 \right)$$

$$= 3950 \times (-0.0001298)$$

$$= -0.5128$$

**Step 2:** Apply the master equation.

$$R_{37} = 10000 \times e^{-0.5128}$$

$$R_{37} = 10000 \times 0.5989$$

$$\boxed{R_{37} \approx 5.989 \text{ kΩ}}$$

#### (b) Resistance at 42 °C

$$\beta \left( \frac{1}{315.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} \right) = 3950 \times \left( 0.0031731 - 0.0033540 \right)$$

$$= 3950 \times (-0.0001809) = -0.7146$$

$$R_{42} = 10000 \times e^{-0.7146} = 10000 \times 0.4893$$

$$\boxed{R_{42} \approx 4.893 \text{ kΩ}}$$

**Interpretation:** A fever rise of only **5 °C** drops resistance from **5.99 kΩ to 4.89 kΩ** — an **~18% change** that the voltage divider circuit can easily resolve.

#### (c) Temperature when R = 5 kΩ

**Step 1:** Take natural log of both sides of the master equation.

$$\ln\left(\frac{R_T}{R_0}\right) = \beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{5000}{10000}\right) = 3950 \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{298.15} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the left side.

$$\ln(0.5) = -0.6931$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $1/T$.

$$-0.6931 = 3950 \left( \frac{1}{T} - 0.0033540 \right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T} - 0.0033540 = \frac{-0.6931}{3950} = -1.7547 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = 0.0033540 - 0.0001755 = 0.0031785$$

**Step 4:** Invert and convert back to Celsius.

$$T = \frac{1}{0.0031785} = 314.61 \text{ K}$$

$$T = 314.61 - 273.15 = 41.46 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 41.5 \text{ °C}}$$

This corresponds to a clinically elevated body temperature — validating the thermistor's medical relevance.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Temperature Coefficient α

Starting from $R_T = R_0 e^{\beta/T - \beta/T_0}$, differentiate with respect to $T$ (treating $R_0$, $\beta$ as constants):

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)} \cdot \left( -\frac{\beta}{T^2} \right)$$

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} \cdot R_T$$

Dividing by $R_T$:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \frac{1}{R_T}\frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} \quad \text{(per Kelvin)}}$$

**Numerical Verification at 310.15 K (37 °C):**

$$\alpha = -\frac{3950}{(310.15)^2} = -\frac{3950}{96193} = -0.04106 \text{ per K}$$

This means **~4.1 % resistance drop per °C** — substantially higher than a platinum RTD's 0.00385 /K, confirming NTC's superior sensitivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.2 Voltage Divider Interfacing Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC[Vcc equals 5V DC] --> RS[Rs Reference Resistor 10k]
    RS --> NODEA[Node A Vout]
    NODEA --> RT[NTC Thermistor 10k at 25C]
    RT --> GND1[Ground]
    NODEA --> ADC[ADC Input Pin of MCU]

    style VCC fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#e8590c,color:#000000
    style RS fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#000000
    style NODEA fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000000
    style RT fill:#ffc9c9,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000000
    style GND1 fill:#e9ecef,stroke:#495057,color:#000000
    style ADC fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000000
```

**Working Logic of the Divider:**
- When patient temperature **rises** → $R_T$ **falls** → $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot R_T / (R_T + R_s)$ **falls**.
- The ADC reads a *lower* voltage → MCU applies the inverse Steinhart-Hart equation → displays the corresponding **higher temperature**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define a thermistor. Mention any two advantages and one disadvantage of using NTC thermistors in biomedical temperature measurement.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **thermistor** is a temperature-sensitive semiconductor resistor whose resistance varies predictably with temperature. **Advantages:** (i) High sensitivity (~4 %/°C, ~10× higher than RTDs) enabling detection of small physiological temperature changes; (ii) Compact size (sub-millimeter bead) suitable for catheter-tip and skin-surface probes; (iii) Low cost and fast thermal response time (<1 s in water). **Disadvantage:** Strong non-linearity of the $R$–$T$ characteristic, requiring linearization or Steinhart-Hart compensation; also self-heating error if excitation current is not carefully limited.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between NTC and PTC thermistors. Which type is preferred for body temperature monitoring and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | NTC | PTC |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Behavior | R decreases as T increases | R increases as T increases |
| Material | Sintered metal oxides (Mn, Ni, Co) | Barium titanate doped ceramics |
| Biomedical Use | **Preferred — clinical probes** | Not used for sensing |
| Application | Patient monitors, incubators, digital thermometers | Overcurrent protection, motor starters |

**NTC is preferred for body temperature monitoring** because (i) its high negative temperature coefficient (~−4 %/°C) provides excellent resolution in the narrow physiological band of 35–42 °C, (ii) its resistance at room temperature (~10 kΩ) is convenient for voltage-divider interfacing, and (iii) its small thermal mass ensures rapid equilibration with tissue temperature.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> **Note for students:** KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Both sub-parts must be attempted.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the thermistor resistance–temperature equation $R_T = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ starting from the Arrhenius conductivity law. Define all symbols. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 10 kΩ NTC thermistor (β = 3950 K, $R_0$ at 25 °C) is connected in a voltage divider with $R_s$ = 10 kΩ and $V_{cc}$ = 5 V. Calculate: (i) output voltage at 37 °C, (ii) output voltage at 42 °C, (iii) the sensitivity in mV/°C in the 37–42 °C range. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State Arrhenius conductivity:

$$\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-E_a / k_B T}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Convert conductivity to resistance. Since $R \propto 1/\sigma$ and $L/A$ are geometric constants:

$$R(T) = R_\infty \, e^{+E_a / k_B T}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Form the ratio $R_T / R_0$ at two temperatures $T$ and $T_0$:

$$\frac{R_T}{R_0} = e^{(E_a/k_B)(1/T - 1/T_0)}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Define $\beta = E_a / k_B$ (in Kelvin) and rearrange to the canonical form:

$$\boxed{R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}}$$

where $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically 298.15 K), $T$ = absolute temperature in K, $\beta$ = material constant in K, $E_a$ = activation energy in eV, $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R_0 = 10 \text{ kΩ}$, $T_0 = 298.15$ K, $\beta = 3950$ K, $R_s = 10 \text{ kΩ}$, $V_{cc} = 5$ V.

**Step 1 (2 marks): Compute $R_T$ at 37 °C (310.15 K) using the derived equation.**

$$\beta\left(\frac{1}{310.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right) = -0.5128$$

$$R_{37} = 10000 \times e^{-0.5128} = 5989 \text{ Ω} \approx 5.99 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark): Compute $V_{out}$ at 37 °C.**

$$V_{37} = 5 \times \frac{5.99}{5.99 + 10} = 5 \times \frac{5.99}{15.99} = 5 \times 0.3746$$

$$\boxed{V_{37} \approx 1.873 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Compute $R_{42}$ and $V_{42}$.**

$$R_{42} \approx 4.893 \text{ kΩ (from earlier derivation)}$$

$$V_{42} = 5 \times \frac{4.893}{4.893 + 10} = 5 \times 0.3285$$

$$\boxed{V_{42} \approx 1.643 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Sensitivity in 37–42 °C range.**

$$\Delta V = V_{42} - V_{37} = 1.643 - 1.873 = -0.230 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta T = 42 - 37 = 5 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{-0.230}{5} = -46 \text{ mV/°C}}$$

**Physical Interpretation (bonus mark):** The negative sign confirms that *output voltage falls* as *temperature rises* — characteristic of an NTC in a voltage divider. A 46 mV/°C swing is easily resolved by a 10-bit ADC over a 5 V range (≈ 4.9 mV/LSB), giving **~9–10 LSBs per °C**, sufficient for ±0.1 °C clinical accuracy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat labeled circuit diagram, explain the voltage-divider method of interfacing an NTC thermistor to measure temperature. State the equation for output voltage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the experimental procedure to obtain the temperature–resistance characteristics of an NTC thermistor in the lab. List the precautions and tabulate a typical observation format. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution Outline

**(a) Circuit Explanation (7 marks):**
- Draw circuit: $V_{cc}$ → $R_s$ → Node A ($V_{out}$) → $R_T$ (NTC) → GND.
- $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_s}$.
- Explain non-inverting behavior: as T ↑, $R_T$ ↓, $V_{out}$ ↓.
- Discuss choice of $R_s \approx R_T$ at the midpoint of the operating range (e.g., $R_s = 10$ kΩ when $R_0 = 10$ kΩ at 25 °C) for **maximum linearity window** around 37 °C.
- Mention ADC interfacing and LUT-based linearization in firmware.

**(b) Experimental Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Apparatus:** NTC thermistor (10 kΩ), digital multimeter, water bath, mercury thermometer (reference), connecting wires, beaker.
2. **Setup:** Immerse thermistor bead and mercury thermometer bulb together in the water bath, ensuring they do not touch the beaker walls.
3. **Procedure:**
    - Heat water gradually from 30 °C to 80 °C in 5 °C steps.
    - At each step, allow 60 s for thermal equilibrium.
    - Record $T_{ref}$ (mercury thermometer) and $R_T$ (multimeter).
4. **Precautions:** (i) Limit current to <1 mA to avoid self-heating; (ii) Maintain uniform bath temperature by gentle stirring; (iii) Do not allow water to contact the multimeter terminals; (iv) Allow full thermal equilibration before recording.

**Sample Observation Table:**

| Sl. No. | $T_{ref}$ (°C) | $T$ (K) | $R_T$ (Ω) | $\ln(R_T)$ | $1/T$ (K⁻¹) |
|---------|----------------|---------|-----------|------------|--------------|
| 1 | 30 | 303.15 | — | — | — |
| 2 | 35 | 308.15 | — | — | — |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

**Plot:** $R_T$ vs $T$ (°C) on semi-log paper gives a straight line whose slope yields $\beta$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Mark Deduction in PCBML408 Lab Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** — the β-equation is undefined for $T$ in Celsius. This single error loses **2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Using $|R|$ style absolute value bars in markdown answers** — examiners flag inconsistent notation; write magnitudes explicitly as "R_T equals X ohms".
> 3. **Conflating α (temperature coefficient) with β (material constant)** — α depends on $T$, β does not. Examiners often ask the relationship, and mixing these up costs full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring self-heating** in the experimental write-up — always mention the use of low excitation current (<1 mA) to score the "precautions" mark.
> 5. **Plotting R vs T on linear graph paper** instead of semi-log — you will obtain a curve that hides β. **Always plot $\ln(R)$ vs $1/T$** to extract β as the slope.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Thermistor = Temperature-sensitive semiconductor resistor; NTC is dominant in biomedical use.
- **Master Equation:** $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ — **always convert T to Kelvin**.
- **β-Constant Range:** ~3000–5000 K for typical biomedical NTCs.
- **Sensitivity:** ~−4 %/°C (NTC) vs +0.385 %/°C (Pt RTD) — thermistors are ~10× more sensitive.
- **Steinhart-Hart Equation:** $\frac{1}{T} = a + b\ln(R) + c[\ln(R)]^3$ — used for high-precision firmware lookup.
- **Voltage Divider:** $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot R_T/(R_T + R_s)$ — choose $R_s$ near $R_T$ at mid-range for best linearity.
- **Self-Heating:** Limit current to **<1 mA** to avoid sensor self-warming artifact.
- **Range:** Thermistor comfortably covers 0 °C–100 °C, with the physiological window 32 °C–42 °C lying in its highest-sensitivity region.
- **Lab Procedure:** Stepwise heating with thermal equilibration, plot $\ln(R)$ vs $1/T$ to extract β as slope × (−1).
- **Pitfall:** NEVER mix Celsius and Kelvin inside the exponent — it is a mathematically undefined operation.
- **Real-World Tie-in:** Every clinical thermometer probe you will use in hospitals is essentially this experiment packaged in a stainless-steel sheath.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLL using 4046
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** PLL using 4046

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Phase Locked Loop (PLL)** is a closed-loop negative feedback control system that synchronizes the output frequency and phase of a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) with the frequency and phase of an incoming reference signal. The **CD4046** is a monolithic CMOS Micropower PLL IC containing two phase detectors (a Type-I XOR PD and a Type-II edge-triggered JK-flipflop PD), a VCO, a source follower, and a Zener diode reference — making it a self-contained PLL building block widely used in **biomedical instrumentation** for FSK demodulation, signal conditioning, frequency synthesis, and noise rejection.

The fundamental goal of any PLL is to make the feedback path generate an internal signal that is exactly **coherent** (matched in phase) with the external reference. When this condition is satisfied, the loop is said to be in a state of **"Lock"**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Oarsman & River Bank" Model

Imagine a **blindfolded rower (VCO)** sitting in a boat. He cannot see, but he can hear the rhythm of waves hitting the river bank (the **reference signal**). He keeps adjusting the speed of his oars (the **control voltage**) based on whether the splash sound is in sync with his own stroke (the **feedback signal**).

- If he strokes *too fast*, the splash sound falls behind → he slows down.
- If he strokes *too slow*, the splash sound races ahead → he speeds up.
- When both the oar splash and the bank splash hit at the **same instant**, the rower is **"locked in"**.

The rower is the VCO, the oar splash is the feedback, the bank splash is the input, the ears+judgment form the **Phase Detector + Loop Filter**, and the arms adjusting the oars form the **VCO control**.

In CD4046, the **"ears"** are one of the two on-chip phase detectors, and the **"arms"** are the internal VCO whose frequency is set by a single external timing capacitor $C_1$ and external timing resistors $R_1, R_2$.

### Standard Engineering Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The CD4046 is specified for a supply range of **$V_{DD} = 3\,\text{V}$ to $18\,\text{V}$** (typical **$V_{DD} = 5\,\text{V}$ or $9\,\text{V}$** or **$15\,\text{V}$**), with a typical operating frequency up to **$1.4\,\text{MHz}$ at $V_{DD} = 10\,\text{V}$** and very low quiescent power consumption of approximately **$70\,\mu\text{W}$** at $V_{DD} = 5\,\text{V}$ — making it ideal for battery-powered biomedical wearable devices.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | **5 V / 9 V / 15 V** |
| Max VCO Frequency | $f_{max}$ | 0.6 MHz (at 5 V), 1.4 MHz (at 10 V) |
| Power Dissipation | $P_D$ | **~70 µW @ 5 V** |
| Operating Temperature | $T_{op}$ | **-55 °C to +125 °C** |
| Input Impedance | $Z_{in}$ | > 10⁹ Ω (CMOS) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block Diagram Decomposition of the CD4046 PLL

The CD4046 internally provides the following functional blocks. The external user only needs to add a **Loop Filter (LF)** between Pin 2/13 and Pin 9 to complete the loop.

- **Phase Detector 1 (PD1) — XOR Type (Type-I)**
  - Simple exclusive-OR gate producing an output that is HIGH when the two inputs differ and LOW when identical.
  - Locks when the two inputs are **90° out of phase** (a square-wave XOR outputs a signal at **twice the input frequency** when locked).
  - Provides a wide lock range but exhibits a **static phase error** of 90° at the centre frequency.

- **Phase Detector 2 (PD2) — Edge-Triggered JK Flip-Flop (Type-II / PFD)**
  - Uses four flip-flops with an AND-gate reset to act as a tri-state output.
  - Locks when the two inputs are **exactly in phase (0° error)**.
  - Eliminates static phase error; preferred for FSK / frequency synthesis applications.

- **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)**
  - Generates a square wave at Pin 4 whose frequency is linearly controlled by the voltage at Pin 9.
  - Frequency increases monotonically with control voltage $V_{C}$.

- **Source Follower (SF)**
  - A unity-gain buffer with high input impedance on Pin 10; used as a demodulator output (e.g., recovering FSK audio).

- **Zener Diode (Pin 15)**
  - 5.2 V / 6.2 V / 7.2 V Zener reference for supply regulation; optional external connection.

### 2.2 Detailed VCO Operation (Pin 9 → Pin 4)

The VCO free-running characteristics are governed by an external capacitor $C_1$ and external resistors $R_1, R_2$:

- $R_1$ and $C_1$ set the **minimum frequency** $f_{min}$ (when $V_C = 0$).
- $R_2$ and $C_1$ set the **maximum frequency** $f_{max}$ (when $V_C = V_{DD}$).
- $R_2$ alone controls the **gain / sensitivity** $K_V$ of the VCO.

The output frequency vs control voltage is a **piecewise-linear** characteristic:

- For $V_{CIN} < V_{SS}$: $f_{out} = 0$ (cut off).
- For $V_{SS} < V_{CIN} < V_{DD}$: linear region — $f_{out} = f_{min} + (V_{CIN} - V_{SS}) \cdot K_V$.
- For $V_{CIN} > V_{DD}$: saturated at $f_{max}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The thresholds $V_{SS}$ and $V_{DD}$ in the VCO characteristic refer to the **internal threshold voltages** of the VCO input (Pin 9), which are derived from $V_{DD}$ and $V_{SS}$ rails and not exactly equal to the supply voltages. For a clean linear region, $V_{CIN}$ must always remain between **$\sim 1.5\,\text{V}$ and $\sim V_{DD} - 1.5\,\text{V}$**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the formulae below — they constitute **~70% of Part-B marks** in any PLL-with-CD4046 derivation question.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | VCO min frequency | $f_{min} = \dfrac{1}{R_2 (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})}$ | Hz (32 pF is internal stray) |
| 2 | VCO max frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{R_1 (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})} + f_{min}$ | Hz |
| 3 | VCO free-running (centre) frequency | $f_o = \dfrac{f_{max} + f_{min}}{2}$ | Hz — for PD1 (XOR) |
| 4 | VCO free-running (centre) frequency | $f_o = f_{min}$ | Hz — for PD2 (PFD) |
| 5 | VCO gain / sensitivity | $K_V = \dfrac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD} - V_{SS}} \approx \dfrac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD}}$ | Hz/V |
| 6 | Total Lock range | $2 f_L = f_{max} - f_{min}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Capture range (2 $f_C$) | Depends on Loop Filter; for first-order lag: $2 f_C = 2 f_L \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\zeta}}$ (approx.) | Hz |
| 8 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_\phi \, K_V}{N \, \tau_1}}$ | rad/s |
| 9 | Damping factor | $\zeta = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{K_\phi \, K_V}{N \, \tau_1}} \cdot (\tau_2 + N/K_V)$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Multiplied output frequency | $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{in}$ | Hz — for frequency multiplier |
| 11 | Divide ratio | $N$ | integer (counter modulus) |
| 12 | PD1 gain (XOR at $V_{DD}$) | $K_\phi = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{\pi}$ | V/rad |
| 13 | PD2 gain (PFD at $V_{DD}$) | $K_\phi = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{4\pi}$ | V/rad |

Where:
- $K_\phi$ — Phase detector gain (V/rad)
- $K_V$ — VCO gain (rad/(V·s))
- $N$ — Frequency division ratio in feedback
- $\tau_1, \tau_2$ — Time constants of the loop filter
- $V_{DD}$ — Supply voltage (also equal to high-level PD output)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Biomedical Electronics

PLL using CD4046 finds extensive applications in biomedical signal acquisition chains:

- **FSK Demodulation in Wireless Biomedical Telemetry** — Pacemaker data, patient monitoring, and ICU bedside telemetry use **Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)** at sub-MHz carrier frequencies. The CD4046 acts as a cheap, low-power FSK demodulator (audio recovered at Pin 10 source follower).
- **Tone-Decoding for Biomedical Instrumentation Buses** — Multi-channel patient monitors use FSK tones to address individual beds; a CD4046 with a 2nd-order loop filter acts as a high-Q tone decoder.
- **Frequency Synthesis for ECG / EEG Stimulators** — A CD4046 with a divide-by-N counter in the feedback synthesizes precisely controlled sub-Hz to kHz stimulation frequencies for cardiac pacemakers and neuro-stimulators.
- **Spike-Noise Rejection** — Locked PLL acts as a **very narrow bandpass filter** (Q can be made > 1000), useful for rejecting 50/60 Hz mains interference when synchronising to a stable reference.
- **PLL-Based Motor Speed Control for Infusion Pumps** — Phase error from a Hall-effect sensor is locked to a reference, giving very accurate peristaltic pump flow rates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the VCO Free-Running Characteristic

**Statement:** The VCO inside CD4046 produces a frequency inversely proportional to the external resistor and capacitor, with the 32 pF representing the internal stray capacitance of the IC.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing time constant.**
The VCO in CD4046 operates on a current-starving Schmitt-trigger principle. The charging current $I$ into capacitor $C_1$ is set by the input control voltage $V_{CIN}$ and the external resistor $R$:

$$
I = \frac{V_{CIN}}{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the ramp time across $C_1$.**
A linear ramp on $C_1$ from the lower Schmitt threshold $V_{T-}$ to the upper threshold $V_{T+}$ (typically $V_{DD}/3$ to $2V_{DD}/3$ for the on-chip inverters) takes time $t$:

$$
t = \frac{C_1 \cdot (V_{T+} - V_{T-})}{I} = \frac{C_1 \cdot (V_{T+} - V_{T-}) \cdot R}{V_{CIN}}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that one full period requires two ramps.**
The VCO output is a symmetric square wave, so one period $T$ corresponds to **two ramp events** (charge + discharge):

$$
T = 2t = \frac{2 C_1 R (V_{T+} - V_{T-})}{V_{CIN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the threshold difference.**
For the CD4046 internal Schmitt, $(V_{T+} - V_{T-}) \approx V_{DD}/2$. Substituting:

$$
T = \frac{2 C_1 R \cdot (V_{DD}/2)}{V_{CIN}} = \frac{C_1 R \, V_{DD}}{V_{CIN}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to frequency and account for internal stray capacitance.**

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{V_{CIN}}{R \, C_1 \, V_{DD}}
$$

Replacing $C_1$ with the practical expression $C_1 + 32\,\text{pF}$ (the on-chip stray) yields the datasheet equation:

$$
\boxed{\,f = \frac{1}{R \,(C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})} \cdot \frac{V_{CIN}}{V_{DD}}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Extract the two boundary conditions.**

When $V_{CIN} = 0 \implies f = f_{min}$ and the controlling resistor is $R_2$:

$$
f_{min} = \frac{1}{R_2 (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})}
$$

When $V_{CIN} = V_{DD} \implies f = f_{max}$ and the controlling resistor is $R_1$:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{R_1 (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Lock Range (Tracking Range)

**Statement:** The lock range is the band of input frequencies over which the PLL, once locked, will *stay* locked — i.e., the range of *tracking* without losing synchronisation.

**Step 1 — Define VCO gain.**
The VCO converts a control-voltage change into a frequency change. The conversion factor $K_V$ (in Hz/V) is:

$$
K_V = \frac{\Delta f}{\Delta V_{CIN}} = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define Phase Detector gain.**
The phase detector converts a phase error $\Delta\phi$ (in radians) into a voltage $V_d$:

$$
K_\phi = \frac{V_{DD}}{\pi} \quad \text{(for PD1 / XOR)} \quad\quad K_\phi = \frac{V_{DD}}{4\pi} \quad \text{(for PD2 / PFD)}
$$

**Step 3 — Open-loop DC gain.**
The total forward path DC gain is:

$$
K = K_\phi \cdot K_V = \frac{V_{DD}}{\pi} \cdot \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD}} = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{\pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Lock range derivation.**
For a Type-I first-order PLL, the maximum tracking deviation equals $K$. Therefore the total lock range (both above and below $f_o$) is:

$$
2 f_L = f_{max} - f_{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta f_L = \pm\,\frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{2}\,}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Capture Range (Acquisition Range)

**Statement:** The capture range is the band of input frequencies into which a *free-running* PLL will *pull* and acquire lock. It is **always smaller than the lock range** because the loop filter attenuates the beat-note needed for acquisition.

**Step 1 — Start from the generic first-order capture equation.**
For a PLL with a single-pole RC low-pass loop filter of time constant $\tau = R_3 C_2$:

$$
\Delta\omega_C = \sqrt{2 \pi \, \Delta f_L \, K_\phi \, K_V \, \frac{1}{\tau}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express in Hz form.**

$$
\Delta f_C = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_L \, K_\phi \, K_V}{2\pi \, R_3 \, C_2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $K_\phi K_V = (f_{max} - f_{min})/\pi$ and simplifying** (after algebraic reduction):

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta f_C \approx \Delta f_L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{2 \pi R_3 C_2 \cdot \Delta f_L}}\,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta f_C \approx \sqrt{\frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{2\pi \, R_3 \, C_2}}\,}
$$

The **engineering conclusion**: Capture range can be widened by:
- Decreasing $R_3$ (increasing filter bandwidth), or
- Decreasing $C_2$ (faster filter response), or
- Increasing $f_{max} - f_{min}$ (larger VCO tuning range).

### 3.4 Derivation of Frequency Multiplication

**Statement:** When a divide-by-$N$ counter is inserted in the feedback path, the CD4046 acts as an integer-N frequency multiplier.

**Step 1 — In steady-state lock, the PD forces the two inputs to be at the same frequency:**

$$
f_{PD1} = f_{PD2} = f_{in}
$$

**Step 2 — The signal reaching PD input (Pin 14) is the VCO output divided by $N$:**

$$
f_{in} = \frac{f_{VCO}}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$
\boxed{\,f_{VCO} = N \cdot f_{in}\,}
$$

This is the basis of every **integer-N PLL frequency synthesizer** used in biomedical FSK transmitters, doppler-ultrasound systems, and implanted RF telemetry.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Design a PLL using CD4046 (XOR PD) with $V_{DD} = 10\,\text{V}$. The required lock range is **$f_{min} = 1\,\text{kHz}$** and **$f_{max} = 10\,\text{kHz}$**. Choose $C_1 = 1\,\text{nF}$ and find $R_1, R_2$.

**Step 1 — Compute $R_2$ from $f_{min}$.**

$$
R_2 = \frac{1}{f_{min} (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})} = \frac{1}{1000 \times (1 \times 10^{-9} + 32 \times 10^{-12})}
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{1}{1000 \times 1.032 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{1.032 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 969\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R_1$ from $f_{max} - f_{min}$.**

$$
f_{max} - f_{min} = \frac{1}{R_1 (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})}
$$

$$
R_1 = \frac{1}{(f_{max} - f_{min})(C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})} = \frac{1}{9000 \times 1.032 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 107.6\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Compute centre frequency and lock range.**

$$
f_o = \frac{f_{max} + f_{min}}{2} = \frac{10\,\text{kHz} + 1\,\text{kHz}}{2} = 5.5\,\text{kHz}
$$

$$
2 f_L = f_{max} - f_{min} = 9\,\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute VCO gain.**

$$
K_V = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD}} = \frac{9000}{10} = 900\,\text{Hz/V}
$$

**Step 5 — Select a first-order loop filter $R_3 C_2$ to set capture range.**
For a desired capture range $2 f_C = 1\,\text{kHz}$:

$$
R_3 C_2 = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{2\pi f_C^2} = \frac{9000}{2 \pi \times (500)^2} \approx 5.73 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}
$$

Choosing $C_2 = 100\,\text{nF}$:

$$
R_3 = \frac{5.73 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 57.3\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Final Design Summary** (perfectly valid KTU lab design):

$$
\boxed{R_1 \approx 107.6\,\text{k}\Omega,\quad R_2 \approx 969\,\text{k}\Omega,\quad R_3 \approx 57.3\,\text{k}\Omega,\quad C_1 = 1\,\text{nF},\quad C_2 = 100\,\text{nF},\quad V_{DD} = 10\,\text{V}}
$$

    Pin13Out -.Alt.-> LPFFilter
    LPFFilter --> Pin9In([Pin 9: VCO_IN])

### 4.4 Lock Acquisition Waveform (Block-Level Timing Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Fs as Input f_s
    participant PD as Phase Detector
    participant LPF as Loop Filter R3 C2
    participant VCO as VCO inside 4046
    participant Fo as Output f_o
    Note over Fs,Fo: STAGE 1 Free Running
    Fs->>PD: f_in = 1 kHz
    PD->>LPF: Beat Note f_in minus f_o
    LPF->>VCO: Vc ramps slowly
    VCO->>Fo: f_o drifts toward f_in
    Note over Fs,Fo: STAGE 2 Capture
    Fs->>PD: Beat frequency decreases
    PD->>LPF: Beat note enters LPF bandwidth
    LPF->>VCO: Vc slew rate limited
    VCO->>Fo: f_o gets pulled in
    Note over Fs,Fo: STAGE 3 Lock
    Fs->>PD: Phase difference stable
    PD->>LPF: DC error voltage Vc
    LPF->>VCO: Vc steady
    VCO->>Fo: f_o = f_in exactly locked
```
