## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List the functional blocks available inside the CD4046 IC and state the role of each block.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The CD4046 monolithic CMOS PLL IC contains the following internal blocks:
1. **Phase Detector 1 (PD1) — XOR type** (Pins 1, 2): Produces an error voltage proportional to the phase difference between the input at Pin 14 and the VCO-fed signal at Pin 3. Operates with a 90° static phase error.
2. **Phase Detector 2 (PD2) — Edge-triggered JK flip-flop / PFD** (Pins 13, 14, 3): Produces tri-state error output; locks with 0° static phase error; preferred for FSK and frequency synthesis.
3. **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** (Pins 4, 6, 7, 9, 11, 12): Generates a square wave whose frequency is a linear function of the control voltage at Pin 9, set by external $R_1, R_2, C_1$.
4. **Source Follower (SF) buffer** (Pin 10): High-impedance unity-gain buffer used as a demodulator output (e.g., for FSK).
5. **Zener diode reference** (Pin 15): On-chip 5.2 V / 6.2 V zener for voltage regulation.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Lock Range and Capture Range in a PLL.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Lock Range | Capture Range |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Range of input frequencies over which the PLL, once locked, **remains locked**. | Range of input frequencies over which the PLL, **starting from unlocked state**, will acquire lock. |
| Depends on | VCO tuning range only. | VCO tuning range **and** Loop Filter bandwidth. |
| Magnitude | Larger | Smaller (or equal) than lock range |
| PD characteristic | Tracking performance | Acquisition performance |
| Formula | $2 f_L = f_{max} - f_{min}$ | $2 f_C \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{\pi R_3 C_2}}$ |

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Marks are *routinely lost* when students **confuse the formulas for $f_{min}$ and $f_{max}$** with respect to $R_1$ and $R_2$ — remember $R_1$ controls $f_{max}$ and $R_2$ controls $f_{min}$ in the datasheet convention.
> - Students also **forget the 32 pF internal stray capacitance** in $C_1$, leading to errors > 3% in numerical design.
> - When asked to "design for a given $f_{min}, f_{max}$", examiners expect you to **show all algebraic substitution steps** — skipping the derivation loses 4-5 marks.
> - Always specify **which Phase Detector (PD1 XOR or PD2 PFD)** you are using — they have different $K_\phi$ values and different centre frequencies.

---

#### Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Module Experiment 2 / Q1(a)+Q1(b)]**

**Q1(a). [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a PLL implemented using the CD4046 IC. Identify the role of pins 2, 9, 11, 12, 13, 14, and 15 in the circuit.

**Model Solution:**

A Phase Locked Loop is a closed-loop negative feedback system that synchronises the output frequency of a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) with the frequency of an external reference. The CD4046 is a CMOS IC that provides almost all the blocks of a PLL on a single chip.

**Block Diagram and Operation:**

The reference input signal $f_{in}$ is applied to **Pin 14 (SIG_IN)** and the feedback from the VCO is applied to **Pin 3 (COMP_IN)**. The Phase Detector (either PD1 via **Pin 2 (PD1_OUT)** or PD2 via **Pin 13 (PC2_OUT)**) compares the two signals and produces an error voltage $V_e$ proportional to their phase difference. This error voltage is filtered by an external RC Loop Filter (typically $R_3 C_2$) to remove the high-frequency AC components, producing a smooth DC control voltage $V_c$.

This control voltage is fed to the VCO at **Pin 9 (VCO_IN)**. The VCO generates a square wave at **Pin 4 (VCO_OUT)** whose frequency is a linear function of $V_c$:

$$
f_{VCO} = f_{min} + (V_c - V_{SS}) \cdot K_V
$$

When the loop is locked, the VCO output frequency exactly matches $f_{in}$, and the phase detector produces a steady DC error voltage that maintains the lock.

**Pin Function Summary:**

| Pin | Function |
|---|---|
| Pin 2 (PD1_OUT) | Output of XOR Phase Detector 1; used to drive external loop filter |
| Pin 9 (VCO_IN) | Control voltage input to the VCO from loop filter output |
| Pin 11 (R1) | External resistor setting the **maximum** VCO frequency $f_{max}$ |
| Pin 12 (R2) | External resistor setting the **minimum** VCO frequency $f_{min}$ |
| Pin 13 (PC2_OUT) | Output of Phase Detector 2 (PFD); alternate PD output for FSK apps |
| Pin 14 (SIG_IN) | Reference input signal from external frequency source |
| Pin 15 (ZENER) | Internal Zener diode reference (5.2 V) for supply regulation |

**Q1(b). [7 Marks]** For a CD4046 PLL using the XOR phase detector, $V_{DD} = 10\,\text{V}$, the design requirements are $f_{min} = 2\,\text{kHz}$ and $f_{max} = 20\,\text{kHz}$. With $C_1 = 10\,\text{nF}$, calculate $R_1$, $R_2$, the VCO gain $K_V$, and the total lock range.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $R_2$ from $f_{min}$ formula.**

$$
R_2 = \frac{1}{f_{min} (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})}
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{1}{2000 \times (10 \times 10^{-9} + 32 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{1}{2000 \times 1.0032 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
R_2 \approx 49.84\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R_1$ from $f_{max} - f_{min}$ formula.**

$$
f_{max} - f_{min} = \frac{1}{R_1 (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})}
$$

$$
R_1 = \frac{1}{(f_{max} - f_{min}) (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})} = \frac{1}{18000 \times 1.0032 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
R_1 \approx 5.538\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Compute VCO gain $K_V$.**

$$
K_V = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD}} = \frac{18000}{10} = 1800\,\text{Hz/V}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total lock range.**

$$
2 f_L = f_{max} - f_{min} = 20 - 2 = 18\,\text{kHz}
$$

So $f_L = \pm 9\,\text{kHz}$ about the centre frequency $f_o = 11\,\text{kHz}$.

**Final Design Summary:**

$$
\boxed{R_1 \approx 5.54\,\text{k}\Omega,\quad R_2 \approx 49.84\,\text{k}\Omega,\quad K_V = 1800\,\text{Hz/V},\quad 2 f_L = 18\,\text{kHz}}
$$

---

#### Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative Selection

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module Experiment 2 / Q2(a)+Q2(b)]**

**Q2(a). [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between Phase Detector 1 (XOR) and Phase Detector 2 (PFD) inside the CD4046. State which one is preferred for FSK demodulation and why.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | PD1 — XOR Type | PD2 — Edge-Triggered PFD (JK FF) |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | Single exclusive-OR gate | Quad JK-flipflop with AND-gate reset |
| Lock Condition | VCO and input are 90° out of phase | VCO and input are 0° out of phase (in phase) |
| Static Phase Error | 90° (at centre frequency) | 0° (true lock) |
| Output at Lock | Constant DC at $V_{DD}/2$ (50% duty cycle) | Tri-state (high-impedance / zero current) |
| Capture / Lock Range | Wide; lock range = capture range (approx.) | Lock range > capture range; needs loop filter |
| Sideband Suppression | Sensitive to harmonics; may lock to harmonics | Only locks to true fundamental; no harmonic lock |
| Preferred Use | Frequency synthesis, general tracking | **FSK demodulation**, frequency synthesis, motor control |

**Why PD2 is preferred for FSK demodulation:**

In FSK (Frequency Shift Keying), the input frequency toggles between two discrete values (mark and space frequencies), say 1 kHz and 2 kHz. The CD4046 with PD2 locks cleanly to each frequency, and the **VCO control voltage at Pin 9** follows the input frequency, providing a clean demodulated audio/data signal at the Source Follower output (Pin 10).

PD2's **tri-state output** means that whenever the VCO is faster than the input, current flows in; when slower, current flows out; when locked, the output goes to a high-impedance state, **preventing any static phase error**. This is critical for FSK because a static phase error would shift the demodulated baseline.

**Q2(b). [7 Marks]** Design a CD4046-based **frequency multiplier by 8** for an input of 1 kHz using the PD2 phase detector. State all the formulas, choose $C_1 = 1\,\text{nF}$ and $V_{DD} = 9\,\text{V}$, and compute the required $R_1$, $R_2$, and the expected VCO output frequency.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the output frequency needed.**

$$
f_{VCO} = N \cdot f_{in} = 8 \times 1\,\text{kHz} = 8\,\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — For a frequency multiplier with PD2 (PFD), the centre frequency is $f_o = f_{min}$.** We will set $f_{min} \le f_{VCO} \le f_{max}$, e.g. choose $f_{min} = 7\,\text{kHz}$ and $f_{max} = 10\,\text{kHz}$ to provide adequate margin.

**Step 3 — Compute $R_2$ from $f_{min}$.**

$$
R_2 = \frac{1}{f_{min} (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})} = \frac{1}{7000 \times (1 \times 10^{-9} + 32 \times 10^{-12})}
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{1}{7000 \times 1.032 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{7.224 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 138.4\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R_1$ from $f_{max} - f_{min}$.**

$$
f_{max} - f_{min} = \frac{1}{R_1 (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})}
$$

$$
R_1 = \frac{1}{(f_{max} - f_{min}) (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})} = \frac{1}{3000 \times 1.032 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
R_1 \approx 322.9\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Compute VCO gain and check the loop is tunable to 8 kHz.**

$$
K_V = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD}} = \frac{3000}{9} \approx 333.3\,\text{Hz/V}
$$

The 8 kHz target lies within the VCO range [7 kHz, 10 kHz], so the design is valid.

**Step 6 — Expected output frequency at Pin 4.**

$$
\boxed{f_{VCO} = 8 \times f_{in} = 8 \times 1\,\text{kHz} = 8\,\text{kHz}}
$$

The feedback loop uses a **divide-by-8 counter** (e.g., CD4017 / 7490 with $N = 8$ configuration), placed between Pin 4 and Pin 3 of the CD4046. The PLL will lock when the divided output = 1 kHz = $f_{in}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Take-aways for the KTU 2024 Lab Exam**

- **PLL = Phase Detector + Loop Filter + VCO**, all in negative feedback. The CD4046 supplies **PD, VCO, Source Follower, and Zener** on-chip; you only add the **Loop Filter** ($R_3, C_2$) and the **timing components** ($R_1, R_2, C_1$).
- **Two PDs inside the 4046:** PD1 (XOR, 90° static error, wide lock range) and PD2 (PFD, 0° static error, no harmonic lock, **preferred for FSK**).
- **$f_{min}$ is set by $R_2$; $f_{max}$ is set by $R_1$.** Do not swap them.
- **Always include the 32 pF internal stray capacitance** in $C_1$ during numerical design.
- **Centre frequency**: $f_o = (f_{max} + f_{min})/2$ for PD1, but $f_o = f_{min}$ for PD2.
- **Lock range** $= f_{max} - f_{min}$ (independent of loop filter).
- **Capture range** is **always ≤ lock range** and depends on $R_3 C_2$ time constant.
- **Frequency multiplication** is achieved by inserting a **divide-by-N counter** in the feedback path: $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{in}$.
- **Source Follower (Pin 10)** is used for **FSK demodulation** by tapping the VCO control voltage through a high-impedance buffer.
- **Pin 5 (INH)** disables the VCO when HIGH — useful for power saving in battery-powered biomedical wearables.
- **Zener Pin 15** can be used to provide a stable 5.2 V internal reference for the supply rail; tie to $V_{DD}$ via a series resistor if used.
- **Biomedical applications** include: FSK demodulation in telemetry, tone-decoders in patient monitors, frequency synthesis for ECG/EEG stimulators, narrow-band noise rejection in ECG/EEG pre-amps, and motor speed control in infusion pumps.
- **Standard VCO frequency formula to memorize:**

$$
f = \frac{1}{R (C_1 + 32\,\text{pF})}
$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Voltage-to-Frequency Converter (V/F)

A **Voltage-to-Frequency Converter (VFC)** is an electronic circuit that accepts an analog input voltage and produces a train of output pulses (typically a square wave or pulse train) whose **frequency is linearly proportional to the magnitude of the input voltage**. It is essentially an analog-to-digital conversion technique where the digital output is in the frequency domain rather than a binary word.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A V/F converter is an electronic subsystem whose output frequency $f_{out}$ bears a direct linear relationship with its analog input voltage $V_{in}$, mathematically expressed as $f_{out} = K_v \cdot V_{in}$, where $K_v$ is the conversion gain expressed in **Hz/Volt**.

## 1.2 Frequency-to-Voltage Converter (F/V)

A **Frequency-to-Voltage Converter (FVC)** performs the exact reverse operation. It accepts a variable frequency input signal (pulse train) and produces an analog output voltage whose **DC level is linearly proportional to the input frequency**. It is therefore a digital-to-analog converter operating in the frequency domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** An F/V converter is an electronic subsystem that converts a frequency-modulated input signal $f_{in}$ into a proportional DC analog output voltage $V_{out}$, mathematically expressed as $V_{out} = K_f \cdot f_{in}$, where $K_f$ is the conversion gain expressed in **Volts/Hz**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Weighing Scale Counter:** Imagine an industrial conveyor belt carrying boxes of different weights. A smart worker places a *reference* weight on a balance and starts adding the unknown boxes one-by-one. Each time the balance tips, the worker dumps a fixed charge and resets it. The **faster the boxes accumulate** (high input quantity/weight), the **more often the balance tips** (high output frequency). This is the basic principle of a V/F converter — it repeatedly charges a capacitor with the unknown input current and dumps a *reference* charge each time a threshold is reached.

- In biomedical applications, V/F and F/V pairs are commonly used to **electrically isolate** sensitive patient-monitoring circuits (like ECG amplifiers) from the noisy digital/mains-power environment, by transmitting the signal as a **frequency-modulated optical pulse** across an optocoupler.
- Common V/F and F/V ICs used in the laboratory: **LM331** (Texas Instruments), **AD650** (Analog Devices), and **AD654**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Constants Used:**
> - Reference voltage for LM331: **$V_{REF}$ = 1.89 V (typical internal bandgap)**
> - Conversion constant for LM331 charge pump: **2.09 V** (a constant derived from the internal flip-flop's logic swing and divider ratio)
> - Standard output frequency range: **DC to 100 kHz** (LM331), **DC to 1 MHz** (AD650)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a V/F Converter (Charge-Balancing Topology)

The most widely used V/F technique in biomedical instrumentation is the **charge-balancing method**, implemented inside the LM331 IC. The operational logic is divided into four structured steps:

1. **Input Current Generation:** The input voltage $V_{in}$ is converted into a proportional input current $I_{in}$ using a precision input resistor $R_S$. Mathematically, $I_{in} = V_{in} / R_S$.
2. **Capacitor Charging:** This current $I_{in}$ is forced to charge a timing capacitor $C_T$. The voltage across the capacitor $V_C$ rises linearly with time, given by $V_C(t) = \dfrac{1}{C_T} \int I_{in} \, dt$.
3. **Comparator Thresholding:** An internal comparator continuously monitors $V_C$. When $V_C$ rises to a fixed threshold (approximately **0.667 × $V_{REF}$** in LM331), the comparator triggers a one-shot monostable multivibrator.
4. **Charge Dumping & Reset:** The one-shot activates a switching transistor that dumps a **precisely defined reference charge packet** of magnitude $Q_{REF} = \dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS}}{R_L}$ onto the capacitor, where $t_{OS}$ is the one-shot pulse width set by external $R_L$ and $C_T$. The capacitor voltage is thus reset, and a single output pulse is emitted.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Idea:** In a steady state, the *charge inserted by $I_{in}$* over one cycle must equal the *charge dumped by the reference*. This balancing equation is the heart of the V/F transfer function.

## 2.2 Working Principle of an F/V Converter (Frequency Discriminator Topology)

An F/V converter essentially uses the same hardware in reverse, with an **input frequency-to-current conversion stage** at the front end:

1. **Differentiator Stage:** The input frequency $f_{in}$ passes through an RC differentiator that produces fixed-area voltage spikes at every zero crossing of the input.
2. **Monostable One-Shot:** Each spike triggers a monostable multivibrator generating a constant-width, constant-amplitude pulse of width $t_{OS}$.
3. **Charge Pump / Averaging Filter:** The monostable pulses drive a switching transistor that injects fixed charge packets $Q_{REF}$ into a low-pass filter network ($R_L$ and $C_T$).
4. **Averaging:** The filter averages the charge packets. Since the *number of packets per second* equals $f_{in}$, the average output voltage is $V_{out} = f_{in} \cdot Q_{REF}$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | V/F Output Frequency | $f_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{2.09 \, V} \cdot \dfrac{R_S}{R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T}$ | **Hz** | LM331 transfer function |
| 2 | F/V Output Voltage | $V_{out} = f_{in} \cdot 2.09 \, V \cdot \dfrac{R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T}{R_S}$ | **Volts** | LM331 reverse mode |
| 3 | Input Current | $I_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_S}$ | **Amps** | Linear V-to-I conversion |
| 4 | Reference Charge | $Q_{REF} = \dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS}}{R_L}$ | **Coulombs** | Charge per output pulse |
| 5 | One-Shot Pulse Width | $t_{OS} = R_L \cdot C_T \cdot \ln 2$ | **Seconds** | LM331 internal one-shot |
| 6 | Conversion Gain (V/F) | $K_v = \dfrac{f_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | **Hz/V** | KTU frequently tests this |
| 7 | Conversion Gain (F/V) | $K_f = \dfrac{V_{out}}{f_{in}}$ | **V/Hz** | KTU frequently tests this |
| 8 | Linearity Error | $\varepsilon_L = \dfrac{\Delta f_{max} - \Delta f_{min}}{f_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Typical LM331: $\pm 0.01\%$ |
| 9 | Full-Scale Frequency | $f_{FS} = 10 \text{ kHz}$ to $100 \text{ kHz}$ | **Hz** | Depends on $R_S, C_T$ |
| 10 | Dynamic Range | $D = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{f_{max}}{f_{min}}\right)$ | **dB** | Typical: **100 dB** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** Whenever an absolute value $\vert x \vert$ or modulus appears inside a table, write it as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Signal Isolation:** V/F followed by an optocoupler and F/V is the gold standard for isolating **ECG, EEG, and EMG** front-end amplifiers from digital noise and **50 Hz mains interference**, providing **galvanic isolation up to 5 kV RMS**.
- **Long-Distance Telemetry:** Frequency signals are immune to cable resistance and capacitance, making V/F ideal for transmitting patient vitals over long cables in ICU.
- **Precision ADC:** V/F + counter is a low-cost, high-resolution **analog-to-digital converter** technique achieving up to **16-bit resolution**.
- **Motor Speed Control:** F/V converts tacho-generator frequency to a voltage for closed-loop speed control.
- **FM Demodulation:** F/V is the classical demodulator for **Frequency Modulated (FM)** biomedical telemetry signals.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: V/F Transfer Function of LM331

We start from the principle of **charge balance over one period** of the output frequency $f_{out}$. Let $T = 1/f_{out}$ be the period of the output pulse train.

### Step 1: Charge inserted into $C_T$ by the input current over one period

The input current $I_{in}$ flows continuously into the timing capacitor $C_T$ for a time duration equal to the period $T$. The charge supplied is:

$$
Q_{in} = I_{in} \cdot T = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_S} \cdot T
$$

This represents the **total charge entering** the capacitor from the unknown input voltage during one full cycle.

### Step 2: Charge removed from $C_T$ by the reference switch

During each cycle, the internal one-shot activates the current switch for a precisely defined pulse width $t_{OS}$. The current switched is $I_{REF} = V_{REF}/R_L$. The charge removed (dumped to ground) is:

$$
Q_{REF} = I_{REF} \cdot t_{OS} = \dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS}}{R_L}
$$

This represents the **calibrated charge removed** per output pulse.

### Step 3: Apply the Charge-Balancing Steady-State Condition

In steady state, the average voltage on $C_T$ must remain constant. Therefore, the charge inserted over one period must exactly equal the charge removed:

$$
Q_{in} = Q_{REF}
$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$
\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_S} \cdot T = \dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS}}{R_L}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for the Period $T$

Rearranging the equation above to isolate $T$:

$$
T = \dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS} \cdot R_S}{V_{in} \cdot R_L}
$$

### Step 5: Convert Period to Frequency

Since $f_{out} = 1/T$, we take the reciprocal:

$$
f_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot R_L}{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS} \cdot R_S}
$$

### Step 6: Substitute LM331 Internal Constants

The LM331 datasheet defines the one-shot pulse width as:

$$
t_{OS} = R_L \cdot C_T \cdot \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \cdot R_L \cdot C_T
$$

And the internal reference voltage swing through the flip-flop is $V_{REF}/2.09$ (the 2.09 V constant accounts for the 5 V supply, divider ratio, and flip-flop logic swing). Substituting and simplifying:

$$
f_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{2.09 \, V} \cdot \dfrac{R_S}{R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The resistor $R_T$ sets the reference current. The factor **2.09 V** is a **chip-specific constant** for the LM331 family, derived empirically from the internal bandgap reference and flip-flop divider chain.

### Step 7: Verify Linearity

Define the conversion gain:

$$
K_v = \dfrac{f_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{R_S}{2.09 \, V \cdot R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T}
$$

This is **independent of $V_{in}$**, confirming perfect theoretical linearity. In practice, the LM331 achieves a linearity of **$\pm 0.01\%$** of full scale.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: F/V Transfer Function of LM331

### Step 1: Pulse Train Input to Monostable

Each rising edge of the input frequency $f_{in}$ triggers the monostable one-shot, producing a pulse of fixed width $t_{OS}$ and amplitude $V_{REF}$.

### Step 2: Charge Injected per Pulse

$$
Q_{pulse} = \dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS}}{R_L}
$$

### Step 3: Total Charge per Second (Average Current)

Since there are $f_{in}$ pulses per second, the average current flowing into the filter is:

$$
I_{avg} = f_{in} \cdot Q_{pulse} = \dfrac{f_{in} \cdot V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS}}{R_L}
$$

### Step 4: Low-Pass Filter Output

This average current flows through the output filter resistor $R_S$ to produce the output voltage:

$$
V_{out} = I_{avg} \cdot R_S = \dfrac{f_{in} \cdot V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS} \cdot R_S}{R_L}
$$

### Step 5: Final F/V Transfer Function

Substituting $t_{OS} = 0.693 \cdot R_L \cdot C_T$ and the LM331 constant 2.09 V:

$$
V_{out} = f_{in} \cdot 2.09 \, V \cdot \dfrac{R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T}{R_S}
$$

The conversion gain is:

$$
K_f = \dfrac{V_{out}}{f_{in}} = 2.09 \, V \cdot \dfrac{R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T}{R_S} \quad [\text{V/Hz}]
$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An LM331 V/F converter is designed with $R_S = 14 \, \text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 100 \, \text{k}\Omega$, $C_T = 1000 \, \text{pF}$, and $R_T = 14 \, \text{k}\Omega$. Calculate the output frequency when $V_{in} = 5 \, \text{V}$.

**Step 1: Identify formula**

$$
f_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{2.09} \cdot \dfrac{R_S}{R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values**

$$
f_{out} = \dfrac{5}{2.09} \cdot \dfrac{14 \times 10^3}{(100 \times 10^3)(1000 \times 10^{-12})(14 \times 10^3)}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate numerator of the gain fraction**

$$
\dfrac{R_S}{R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T} = \dfrac{14 \times 10^3}{100 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-9} \times 14 \times 10^3} = \dfrac{14 \times 10^3}{1.4} = 10 \times 10^3 = 10,000
$$

**Step 4: Final computation**

$$
f_{out} = \dfrac{5}{2.09} \times 10,000 = 2.392 \times 10,000 = 23,923 \, \text{Hz} \approx 23.9 \, \text{kHz}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This confirms that a 5 V input gives an output frequency of approximately **23.9 kHz**, well within the LM331's specified **DC to 100 kHz** operating range.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — V/F Converter (LM331 Architecture)

The LM331 internally contains the following functional blocks. The mermaid diagram below maps their interconnection at the architectural level, mirroring the LM331 datasheet block diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[VIN ANALOG INPUT] --> nodeB[PRECISION V TO I CONVERTER]
    nodeB --> nodeC[TIMING CAPACITOR CT]
    nodeC --> nodeD[COMPARATOR WITH INTERNAL REFERENCE]
    nodeD --> nodeE[RS LATCH]
    nodeE --> nodeF[MONOSTABLE ONE SHOT TIMER]
    nodeF --> nodeG[CURRENT SWITCH]
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeH[OUTPUT FREQUENCY PULSE TRAIN]
    nodeI[VOLTAGE REFERENCE 1.89V] --> nodeD
    nodeI --> nodeG
    nodeJ[RT RT EXTERNAL] --> nodeI
```

**Description of the functional flow:**
- The input voltage $V_{in}$ is converted to a current $I_{in}$ by the precision V-to-I block.
- This current charges the external timing capacitor $C_T$.
- When the capacitor voltage crosses the comparator's threshold (set by the internal 1.89 V reference), the RS latch triggers the one-shot.
- The one-shot closes the current switch for a brief, precisely-timed interval, dumping a reference charge packet and resetting $C_T$.
- Simultaneously, an output pulse is emitted on the $f_{out}$ pin.
- The external resistor $R_T$ programs the internal current source's magnitude.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — F/V Converter (LM331 Reverse Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[FIN PULSE TRAIN INPUT] --> nodeB[RC DIFFERENTIATOR]
    nodeB --> nodeC[INPUT COMPARATOR]
    nodeC --> nodeD[MONOSTABLE ONE SHOT]
    nodeD --> nodeE[SWITCHED CURRENT SOURCE]
    nodeE --> nodeF[LOW PASS FILTER RL CT]
    nodeF --> nodeG[VOUT DC ANALOG VOLTAGE]
    nodeH[INTERNAL 1.89V REFERENCE] --> nodeE
    nodeI[RS EXTERNAL LOAD RESISTOR] --> nodeG
```

**Description of the functional flow:**
- Each rising edge of $f_{in}$ produces a sharp differentiated spike.
- The input comparator cleans the spike and triggers the one-shot.
- The one-shot drives a switched current source that injects a charge packet of fixed size $Q_{REF}$ into the low-pass filter.
- The filter averages these charge packets, yielding a smooth DC voltage $V_{out}$ proportional to $f_{in}$.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Biomedical Isolation System

This is the most important real-world application tested in PCBML408. A V/F + optocoupler + F/V chain forms a complete analog isolation barrier.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[BIOMEDICAL SENSOR ECG EEG EMG] --> nodeB[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER]
    nodeB --> nodeC[V TO F CONVERTER LM331]
    nodeC --> nodeD[OPTO COUPLER LED AND PHOTOTRANSISTOR]
    nodeD --> nodeE[F TO V CONVERTER LM331]
    nodeE --> nodeF[POST AMPLIFIER AND FILTER]
    nodeF --> nodeG[DIGITAL ADC OR DISPLAY UNIT]
    nodeH[5 KV ISOLATION BARRIER] -.-> nodeD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The optocoupler breaks any **galvanic (conductive) path** between the patient-side circuit and the digital/mains-side circuit, providing **5 kV RMS** of galvanic isolation. This is the core reason V/F + F/V pairs are used in **patient monitors**, **defibrillators**, and **surgical equipment**.
