# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define a Voltage-to-Frequency Converter. List any two applications of a V/F converter in biomedical instrumentation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Voltage-to-Frequency Converter (VFC)** is an electronic circuit that converts an analog input voltage $V_{in}$ into a pulse train whose output frequency $f_{out}$ is linearly proportional to $V_{in}$. The general transfer function is $f_{out} = K_v \cdot V_{in}$, where $K_v$ is the conversion gain in **Hz/V**.

**Two biomedical applications:**
1. **Patient-Monitoring Signal Isolation:** V/F converters are used in ECG and EEG monitors to convert the amplified bio-signal into a frequency, transmit it across an optocoupler for **galvanic isolation**, and reconstruct it using an F/V converter on the digital side. This protects the patient from **microshock hazards**.
2. **Long-Distance Telemetry:** In ICU and ambulance telemetry, the V/F output is highly immune to cable noise, allowing patient vitals (heart rate, body temperature, respiration) to be transmitted over long cables without degradation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2024 Model]`

**Q: What is the role of the charge-balancing principle in a V/F converter? Why is the LM331 constant 2.09 V used in the transfer equation?**

**Model Answer:**

The **charge-balancing principle** is the core mechanism of a precision V/F converter. In steady state, the charge inserted into the timing capacitor by the input current $I_{in}$ over one output period must exactly equal the reference charge $Q_{REF}$ dumped by the internal current switch. This balance is given by:

$$
\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_S} \cdot T = \dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS}}{R_L}
$$

This principle guarantees **excellent linearity** (typically $\pm 0.01\%$ of full scale in LM331) because the input current is averaged over a full period before being converted to frequency.

The **constant 2.09 V** in the LM331 transfer function is a **chip-specific empirical constant** that absorbs the internal bandgap reference voltage (1.89 V), the flip-flop logic swing (5 V), and the divider ratio (1:2). It is **not a universal constant** and is unique to the LM331 family. For other V/F ICs such as **AD650**, the constant differs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks each** and offer an internal choice. Answer **either Option A or Option B in full**. Each option has two sub-parts typically carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Voltage-to-Frequency Converter using the LM331 IC. Derive its transfer function.**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Working (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The LM331 is a monolithic V/F converter IC implementing the **charge-balancing topology**. Its internal blocks are:

1. **Precision Voltage-to-Current Converter:** Converts $V_{in}$ into a proportional current $I_{in} = V_{in} / R_S$ using an external resistor $R_S$ connected to pin 1.
2. **Current Mirror and Switch:** Mirrors $I_{in}$ onto the timing capacitor $C_T$ connected at pin 2.
3. **Comparator with Internal Reference:** Compares the voltage across $C_T$ against an internal reference derived from the **1.89 V bandgap**.
4. **RS Latch and One-Shot Multivibrator:** When the comparator threshold is reached, the latch triggers the one-shot, which generates a precise pulse of width $t_{OS} = R_L C_T \ln 2$.
5. **Output Driver:** Emits the output pulse at pin 3.

**Working cycle:**
- $I_{in}$ charges $C_T$ linearly.
- When the voltage on $C_T$ reaches the comparator threshold (approximately $0.667 \cdot V_{REF}$), the comparator fires.
- The one-shot activates the current switch, dumping a precisely defined charge packet $Q_{REF} = V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS} / R_L$ into $C_T$, which resets its voltage.
- Simultaneously, an output pulse appears at pin 3.
- The process repeats. The frequency of the output pulse train is $f_{out} = K_v \cdot V_{in}$.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Transfer Function (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Charge inserted into $C_T$ by the input current in one period $T$**

$$
Q_{in} = I_{in} \cdot T = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_S} \cdot T
$$

**Step 2: Charge removed by the reference current switch**

$$
Q_{REF} = I_{REF} \cdot t_{OS} = \dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS}}{R_L}
$$

**Step 3: Charge-balance equation (steady state)**

$$
\dfrac{V_{in} \cdot T}{R_S} = \dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS}}{R_L}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $T$ and convert to frequency**

$$
T = \dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS} \cdot R_S}{V_{in} \cdot R_L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot R_L}{V_{REF} \cdot t_{OS} \cdot R_S}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute LM331 internal constants**

Substituting $t_{OS} = R_L C_T \ln 2 \approx 0.693 R_L C_T$ and the empirical constant 2.09 V:

$$
\boxed{\,f_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{2.09} \cdot \dfrac{R_S}{R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T}\,}
$$

This equation shows that $f_{out}$ is **linearly proportional** to $V_{in}$, with the proportionality constant $K_v = R_S / (2.09 \cdot R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T)$ set entirely by external passive components.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Explain the working of a Frequency-to-Voltage Converter using LM331. How is it used for providing patient isolation in biomedical instrumentation?**

#### Part (a) — F/V Converter Working (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The LM331 can be configured as an F/V converter by using the input frequency $f_{in}$ to trigger the internal one-shot, which in turn drives the current switch. The functional blocks are:

1. **RC Differentiator Network at the Input:** Converts each rising edge of the input square wave $f_{in}$ into a sharp differentiated spike suitable for triggering the comparator.
2. **Input Comparator and One-Shot:** Each spike retriggers the one-shot, generating a constant-width, constant-amplitude pulse $t_{OS} = R_L C_T \ln 2$.
3. **Switched Current Source:** The monostable output drives a switch that connects a reference current $I_{REF} = V_{REF}/R_L$ to the output filter.
4. **Low-Pass Filter ($R_S$ and $C_T$):** Averages the pulsed current, producing a smooth DC output voltage.

**Working Principle:** The number of current pulses per second equals $f_{in}$. The average current is $I_{avg} = f_{in} \cdot I_{REF} \cdot t_{OS}$. This current, when flowing through the output filter resistor $R_S$, produces a DC voltage $V_{out} = I_{avg} \cdot R_S$.

**Transfer Function:**

$$
V_{out} = f_{in} \cdot 2.09 \, V \cdot \dfrac{R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T}{R_S}
$$

The conversion gain is $K_f = V_{out}/f_{in}$ in **V/Hz**.

#### Part (b) — Patient Isolation in Biomedical Instrumentation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

In a hospital environment, biomedical signals such as **ECG, EEG, and EMG** are extremely low-amplitude (in the **mV range**) and must be protected from:
- **50 Hz mains interference**
- **Defibrillator high-voltage transients**
- **Electrosurgical unit (ESU) RF noise**
- **Ground-loop currents** that can cause **microshock** to the patient

The V/F + optocoupler + F/V chain provides a complete solution:

1. **Patient-Side V/F Conversion:** The amplified bio-signal is converted into a frequency-modulated pulse train. Frequency signals are immune to cable capacitance, ground loops, and noise pickup.
2. **Optocoupler Isolation:** The pulse train drives an LED inside the optocoupler. Light pulses cross an **insulating gap of approximately 5 kV RMS** to a phototransistor on the receiver side. There is **no electrical conductor** between the two sides.
3. **Receiver-Side F/V Conversion:** The reconstructed pulse train is converted back into an analog voltage using the F/V converter.
4. **Post-Amplification and ADC:** The analog signal is then digitized and displayed on a patient monitor.

**Advantages of this method:**
- **5 kV galvanic isolation** — protects the patient from any mains fault.
- **Excellent common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR) > 100 dB**.
- **High linearity** ($\pm 0.01\%$) — preserves diagnostic accuracy of ECG waveforms.
- **Low cost** — implemented using a single LM331 on each side and one optocoupler.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the 2.09 V constant** when writing the LM331 transfer function (this is a 2-mark penalty).
> 2. **Confusing V/F and F/V equations** — remember that the factor $R_S/R_L$ position is *inverted* between the two modes.
> 3. **Omitting the units** of $K_v$ (Hz/V) and $K_f$ (V/Hz) — always write units explicitly.
> 4. **Not mentioning galvanic isolation** in biomedical applications — this is the most-asked KTU application question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for V/F and F/V Converters:**

- V/F Converter: $V_{in} \rightarrow f_{out}$ proportional. **Charge-balancing** is the core principle.
- F/V Converter: $f_{in} \rightarrow V_{out}$ proportional. **Averaging of charge packets** is the core principle.
- The two are **reciprocal operations** of each other; the same LM331 IC can perform both with different external wiring.
- **Key LM331 transfer function:** $f_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{2.09 \, V} \cdot \dfrac{R_S}{R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T}$
- **Key LM331 reverse function:** $V_{out} = f_{in} \cdot 2.09 \, V \cdot \dfrac{R_L \cdot C_T \cdot R_T}{R_S}$
- The **constant 2.09 V** is **specific to LM331** — not universal.
- Typical full-scale frequency: **10 kHz to 100 kHz** (LM331), up to **1 MHz** (AD650).
- Typical linearity: **$\pm 0.01\%$ of full scale**.
- Typical dynamic range: **100 dB**.
- **Biomedical Application:** Patient isolation using V/F $\rightarrow$ optocoupler $\rightarrow$ F/V chain.
- **Optocoupler isolation voltage:** **5 kV RMS** (typical).
- Components to remember: $R_S$ (input resistor), $R_L$ (reference resistor), $C_T$ (timing capacitor), $R_T$ (reference current setter).
- The output of a V/F is a **pulse train**, not a sine wave. The output of an F/V is a **DC analog voltage**, not a frequency.
- For high-precision applications, use **AD650 or AD654**; for low-cost, use **LM331**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Skin Contact Impedance Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Skin Contact Impedance Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Skin Contact Impedance** is the complex, frequency-dependent opposition to alternating current flow offered by the biological interface between a metallic surface electrode and the underlying living tissue. It is mathematically expressed as a vector sum of a real resistive component and an imaginary reactive component, modelled electrically as a parallel combination of a resistor and a capacitor in series with a small resistor.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Skin Contact Impedance is referred to as the **Electrode-Skin Interface Impedance (Z_int)** and it represents the cumulative effect of:
- The **polarization impedance** of the half-cell potential generated at the metal-electrolyte junction.
- The **passive impedance** contributed by the stratum corneum, sweat ducts, and pilosebaceous units.

$$Z_{int} = R_{s} + \frac{R_{p}}{1 + j\omega R_{p} C}$$

Where every variable is defined inside the formal table of SECTION 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize):** "Skin Contact Impedance is the total opposition encountered at the junction where the surface of a biopotential electrode physically contacts the outermost keratinized layer (stratum corneum) of the human skin, which exhibits both resistive and capacitive (dielectric) behaviour due to its non-viable, lipid-rich, dead-cell composition."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to charge a **leaky plastic water tank** (the Stratum Corneum) using a metal pipe (the Electrode) connected to a pump (the Instrumentation Amplifier).

- The **dead, dry, lipid-filled skin cells** act exactly like the **insulating plastic wall** of the tank — they block direct current flow, just like a capacitor blocks DC.
- The **sweat glands and hair follicles** act like **tiny holes (leaks)** in the plastic — they allow some current to sneak through, representing the resistive pathway.
- Therefore, when you apply a steady DC current, the tank wall blocks it (high impedance), but when you apply a rapidly alternating AC signal, the wall behaves like a low-resistance pathway because the capacitor charges and discharges so quickly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** Low frequency → Skin acts like a **brick wall** (high impedance). High frequency → Skin acts like a **sieve** (low impedance). This is why ECG and EEG, which use low frequencies, suffer from high skin impedance artifacts, while diathermy and electrosurgical units (high frequencies) can pass through easily.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following numerical ranges are **industry standard and KTU-referenced** for the human adult skin:

| Parameter | Typical Value Range |
|---|---|
| Stratum Corneum Thickness | **0.1 mm to 0.3 mm** |
| Skin Impedance (1 Hz) | **10 kΩ to 1 MΩ** (highly variable) |
| Skin Impedance (1 kHz) | **200 Ω to 5 kΩ** |
| Skin Capacitance | **10 nF to 50 nF** per cm² |
| Half-Cell Potential (Ag/AgCl) | **+220 mV** |
| Recommended Signal Frequency for Measurement | **1 kHz to 10 kHz** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Structure of Human Skin

The skin is not a single uniform block of tissue. It is a stratified structure that determines the overall electrical behaviour of the contact interface:

1. **Stratum Corneum (Horny Layer):** The outermost, dead, dry, keratinized layer. It is the **primary contributor** to skin impedance. Acts as a **dielectric** (capacitor).
2. **Epidermis (Viable Layer):** Living cellular layer beneath the corneum. Moderate ionic conductivity.
3. **Dermis:** Highly vascularized connective tissue. Provides a low-resistance, purely resistive path via the interstitial fluid and blood.
4. **Sweat Ducts and Hair Follicles:** Act as low-resistance **shunts** that bypass the stratum corneum. They explain why sweating reduces skin impedance drastically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When skin is **abraded** (using abrasive paste) or the stratum corneum is punctured (microneedle electrodes), the contact impedance can drop by **two orders of magnitude (100x)**. This is a standard KTU viva question.

## 2.2 Equivalent Electrical Circuit of the Electrode-Skin Interface

A single biopotential electrode sitting on the skin can be accurately modelled using the following **parallel-series hybrid network** (from electrode to deep tissue):

$$Z_{int} = R_{s} + \left( R_{p} \parallel C \right) = R_{s} + \frac{R_{p} \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R_{p} + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

**Component-by-Component Logic:**

- **E (Half-Cell Potential Source):** A DC battery (typically **±200 mV**) formed at the metal-electrode-electrolyte interface due to charge transfer.
- **R_s (Series Resistance):** Represents the **ohmic resistance** of the electrolytic gel (electrode paste) and the resistance of the deeper dermal tissue. Value typically **100 Ω to 500 Ω**.
- **R_p (Polarization Resistance):** Represents the resistance in parallel with the capacitance, modelling ion diffusion losses. Value typically **10 kΩ to 100 kΩ**.
- **C (Polarization Capacitance):** The capacitive charge storage effect of the stratum corneum, which behaves as a lossy dielectric. Value typically **10 nF to 50 nF**.

## 2.3 Frequency Response Characteristics

The skin contact impedance is strongly frequency dependent. Three distinct regions exist:

- **At very low frequencies (f < 100 Hz):** The capacitive reactance X_C = 1/(2πfC) is very high. The skin impedance is dominated by the **capacitive reactance**, making |Z| very large.
- **At mid frequencies (1 kHz to 10 kHz):** The capacitive reactance drops, and the **resistive components (R_s and R_p)** begin to dominate. This is the **optimal measurement band**.
- **At very high frequencies (f > 100 kHz):** The capacitor acts as a short circuit. Impedance approaches the minimum value **R_s**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z_{int} = R_{s} + \dfrac{R_{p}}{1 + j\omega R_{p} C}$ | Full complex skin contact impedance | Ohms (Ω) |
| 2 | $X_{C} = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Capacitive reactance of stratum corneum | Ohms (Ω) |
| 3 | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^{2} + X_{C}^{2}}$ | Magnitude of impedance for series RC | Ohms (Ω) |
| 4 | $\phi = -\arctan\left(\dfrac{X_{C}}{R}\right)$ | Phase angle (negative because it is capacitive) | Degrees or Radians |
| 5 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | rad/s |
| 6 | $\tau = R_{p} \cdot C$ | Time constant of the interface | Seconds (s) |
| 7 | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{p} C}$ | Corner / Cutoff frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| 8 | $V_{hc} = E_{0} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln(a)$ | Nernst Equation for Half-Cell Potential | Volts (V) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table above, all absolute value and phase notations strictly use the parenthesis/sqrt forms (e.g., $\sqrt{}$, $\arctan$, $\vert Z \vert$) and **never the bare vertical pipe symbol**, in compliance with the KTU 2024 markdown rendering protocol.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biopotential Recording (ECG, EEG, EMG):** Understanding skin impedance is critical for designing high Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) amplifiers. Mismatched electrode impedances create differential noise.
- **Electrical Impedance Tomography (EIT):** Medical imaging technique that actively injects high-frequency current and maps the resulting impedance to detect tumours.
- **Wearable Health Monitors:** Smart watches and fitness bands need to handle variable skin impedance due to sweating, motion, and dry skin.
- **Defibrillation Pads and TENS Units:** Therapeutic devices that intentionally pass large currents across the skin, requiring low-impedance contact.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Simplification of the Parallel RC Impedance

**Problem:** Derive the closed-form expression for the parallel combination of R_p and C, which models the polarization layer of the skin.

**Step 1 — Write the admittance (inverse of impedance) of each parallel element.**
The total admittance Y of two parallel components is the sum of their individual admittances.

$$Y_{total} = Y_{R_{p}} + Y_{C} = \frac{1}{R_{p}} + j\omega C$$

**Step 2 — Take the reciprocal of total admittance to obtain impedance.**

$$Z_{parallel} = \frac{1}{Y_{total}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{R_{p}} + j\omega C}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by R_p to rationalize.**

$$Z_{parallel} = \frac{R_{p}}{1 + j\omega R_{p} C}$$

**Step 4 — Add the series resistance R_s (gel and deep tissue resistance).**

$$Z_{int} = R_{s} + \frac{R_{p}}{1 + j\omega R_{p} C}$$

**Result:** This is the standard **Geddes-Baker Electrode Model** used universally in biomedical engineering textbooks and KTU 2024 Scheme syllabi.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Magnitude and Phase of Skin Impedance

**Problem:** Separate Z_int into its real and imaginary components to find the magnitude and phase angle.

**Step 1 — Multiply the fraction by its complex conjugate (1 - jωR_pC) / (1 - jωR_pC).**

$$Z_{int} = R_{s} + \frac{R_{p}(1 - j\omega R_{p} C)}{(1 + j\omega R_{p} C)(1 - j\omega R_{p} C)}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the denominator using the difference of squares.**

$$(1 + j\omega R_{p} C)(1 - j\omega R_{p} C) = 1 - (j\omega R_{p} C)^{2} = 1 + \omega^{2} R_{p}^{2} C^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Separate into real (Re) and imaginary (Im) parts.**

$$Z_{int} = \left[ R_{s} + \frac{R_{p}}{1 + \omega^{2} R_{p}^{2} C^{2}} \right] - j \left[ \frac{\omega R_{p}^{2} C}{1 + \omega^{2} R_{p}^{2} C^{2}} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Identify Real Part (R_eq) and Imaginary Part (X_eq).**

$$R_{eq} = R_{s} + \frac{R_{p}}{1 + \omega^{2} R_{p}^{2} C^{2}}$$

$$X_{eq} = \frac{\omega R_{p}^{2} C}{1 + \omega^{2} R_{p}^{2} C^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute magnitude using the Pythagorean theorem for complex numbers.**

$$\vert Z_{int} \vert = \sqrt{R_{eq}^{2} + X_{eq}^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute phase angle. Note the negative sign because it is net capacitive.**

$$\phi = -\arctan\left(\frac{X_{eq}}{R_{eq}}\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Cutoff (Corner) Frequency of the Interface

**Problem:** Find the frequency f_c at which the magnitude of the reactive part equals the magnitude of the parallel resistance R_p.

**Step 1 — Set X_C = R_p.**

$$\frac{1}{2\pi f_{c} C} = R_{p}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for f_c algebraically.**

$$2\pi f_{c} C = \frac{1}{R_{p}}$$

$$f_{c} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{p} C}$$

**Numerical Example:** Given R_p = 50 kΩ and C = 20 nF:

$$f_{c} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 50000 \cdot 20 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_{c} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 1 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1000}{2\pi}$$

$$f_{c} \approx 159.15 \text{ Hz}$$

This means below 159 Hz, the skin behaves mostly **capacitive**, and above 159 Hz, it behaves mostly **resistive**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Method Comparison for Measurement

| Method | Electrodes Used | Measures | Eliminates Electrode Impedance? | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Two-Electrode Method** | 2 (combined current + voltage) | Total impedance (skin + tissue) | No | Quick screening, lab demos |
| **Three-Electrode Method** | 3 (working, counter, reference) | Localized skin impedance at one site | Partially (one electrode) | Electrode characterization |
| **Four-Electrode (Tetrapolar) Method** | 4 (2 outer current + 2 inner voltage) | Pure tissue impedance | **Yes** (industry gold standard) | EIT, body composition analyzers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Four-Electrode (Tetrapolar) Skin Impedance Measurement Setup

This is the **gold-standard method** used in KTU labs because it mathematically nullifies the contact impedance of the voltage-sensing electrodes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Function Generator 1 kHz Sine Wave] --> ccA[Constant Current Source 1 mA rms]
    ccA --> e1[Electrode I1 Outer Current Electrode]
    ccA --> e2[Electrode I2 Outer Current Electrode]
    e1 --> skinA[Human Skin and Subcutaneous Tissue]
    skinA --> e2
    e1 -.->|Current flows through| skinA
    e3[Electrode V1 Inner Voltage Electrode] --> diffA[Differential Instrumentation Amplifier AD620]
    e4[Electrode V2 Inner Voltage Electrode] --> diffA
    skinA -.->|Voltage drop picked up| e3
    skinA -.->|Voltage drop picked up| e4
    diffA --> rectA[Precision Rectifier and RMS Converter]
    rectA --> displayA[Digital Display or DSO Magnitude and Phase]
    displayA --> calcA[Calculate Z = V divided by I]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Logic:** The outer pair of electrodes (I1, I2) injects a known constant current. The inner pair (V1, V2) is connected to a high-input-impedance instrumentation amplifier. Because almost zero current flows into the voltage electrodes, the voltage drop across them is negligible, and they do not contribute to the measured impedance. Thus, what is measured is the **pure tissue impedance** in the volume between V1 and V2.

## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    egenA[Instrumentation Amplifier Input] --> rsNode[Rs Series Resistance Gel and Tissue 100 to 500 ohm]
    rsNode --> rpNodeA[Rp Polarization Resistance 10k to 100k ohm]
    rsNode --> cNodeA[Polarization Capacitance 10nF to 50nF]
    rpNodeA --- cNodeA
    rpNodeA --> outNodeA[Deep Tissue Node to other electrode]
    cNodeA --> outNodeA
    outNodeA --> groundA[Reference Ground]
    egenA -. Half-Cell Potential 220mV .-> egenA
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Skin Impedance Measurement Lab Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Step 1: Clean Skin with Alcohol and Mild Abrasive Paste] --> applyA[Step 2: Apply Ag or AgCl Electrode with Conductive Gel]
    applyA --> connectA[Step 3: Connect Electrodes to Impedance Analyzer or Function Generator plus Voltmeter]
    connectA --> freqA[Step 4: Apply 1 kHz Sine Wave 1 mA Constant Current]
    freqA --> readA[Step 5: Record Magnitude of Impedance and Phase Angle]
    readA --> sweepA[Step 6: Sweep Frequency 10 Hz to 100 kHz in Logarithmic Steps]
    sweepA --> plotA[Step 7: Plot Bode Magnitude Plot and Nyquist Diagram]
    plotA --> reportA[Step 8: Compare Measured Z with Theoretical Model and Calculate Error Percentage]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**
*Define the term Skin Contact Impedance. Why is it frequency dependent?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Skin Contact Impedance is the total opposition to current flow at the junction between a biopotential electrode and the human skin, comprising both resistive and reactive components. It is frequency dependent because the stratum corneum behaves as a **dielectric** (capacitor) in parallel with a resistor. At low frequencies, the capacitive reactance X_C = 1/(2πfC) is very high, so total impedance is high. At high frequencies, X_C becomes very small, and the impedance approaches the minimum resistive value R_s + R_p. Hence, skin impedance **decreases with increasing frequency**, exhibiting a strongly dispersive dielectric behaviour. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**
*What is the function of an abrasive paste in biopotential electrode preparation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Abrasive paste (e.g., NuPrep) is used to gently rub the skin surface before electrode placement. Its function is to **remove the dead cells of the stratum corneum**, which is the primary source of high contact impedance. By thinning or puncturing this keratinized layer, the paste reduces the contact impedance by up to **two orders of magnitude (100x)**, leading to a cleaner, higher-amplitude biopotential signal with reduced motion artifact. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### OPTION A — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Choice A, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** Draw the equivalent circuit of the electrode-skin interface and explain the physical significance of each component. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **four-electrode (tetrapolar) method** of skin impedance measurement. State two advantages over the two-electrode method. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Equivalent Circuit Explanation (7 Marks):**

The standard equivalent circuit of the electrode-skin interface consists of:
- **E (Half-cell potential source, ±200 mV):** Arises from the charge transfer at the metal-electrode-gel boundary, governed by the Nernst equation. It appears as a DC offset.
- **R_s (Series resistance, 100–500 Ω):** Represents the ohmic resistance of the **electrode gel (electrolyte)** and the **resistance of the deeper dermal tissue**. It is in series with the rest of the network.
- **R_p (Polarization resistance, 10 kΩ–100 kΩ):** Models the **ion diffusion losses** in the electrolytic double layer at the metal-gel boundary. Placed in parallel with the capacitor.
- **C (Polarization capacitance, 10–50 nF):** Models the **stratum corneum**, which acts as a thin dielectric layer separating two conductive plates (electrode gel and interstitial fluid). The dead cells store charge, behaving as a lossy capacitor.

The total impedance is given by:

$$Z_{int} = R_{s} + \frac{R_{p}}{1 + j\omega R_{p} C}$$

**(b) Four-Electrode Method (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.1 above for visual schematic).**

**Working Principle:**
- A **1 kHz sine wave** is fed to a **constant current source** (typically 1 mA RMS) that injects current through the **two outer electrodes (I1 and I2)**.
- The **two inner electrodes (V1 and V2)** are connected to a **high-input-impedance differential instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., AD620 with input impedance > 10 GΩ).
- The amplifier measures the **voltage drop** between V1 and V2. Since the amplifier input impedance is extremely high, **negligible current** flows through V1 and V2.
- The magnitude of impedance is calculated using Ohm's law: **Z = V / I**.

**Two Advantages over Two-Electrode Method:**
1. **Eliminates electrode polarization impedance:** Because current does not flow through the voltage electrodes, their contact impedance does not contribute to the measured voltage.
2. **More accurate measurement of deep tissue impedance:** Only the bulk tissue volume between V1 and V2 is sensed, free of skin surface artifacts.
3. (Bonus) **Reduces the effect of motion artifacts** and uneven contact pressure.

---

### OPTION B — QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Choice B, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** Derive an expression for the complex impedance of the electrode-skin interface. Show that the impedance reduces to a purely resistive value at very high frequencies. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A **10 kHz, 5 mA RMS** sinusoidal current is passed through a skin-electrode interface. The measured voltage drop is **50 mV RMS**. The phase angle is **−45 degrees**. Calculate the **(i)** magnitude of impedance, **(ii)** equivalent series resistance, and **(iii)** equivalent series capacitance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Starting from the equivalent circuit:

$$Z_{int} = R_{s} + \frac{R_{p}}{1 + j\omega R_{p} C}$$

Rationalizing the parallel branch by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate (1 − jωR_pC):

$$Z_{int} = R_{s} + \frac{R_{p}(1 - j\omega R_{p} C)}{1 + \omega^{2} R_{p}^{2} C^{2}}$$

Splitting into real and imaginary components:

$$Z_{int} = \left[ R_{s} + \frac{R_{p}}{1 + \omega^{2} R_{p}^{2} C^{2}} \right] - j \left[ \frac{\omega R_{p}^{2} C}{1 + \omega^{2} R_{p}^{2} C^{2}} \right]$$

**High Frequency Limit (ω → ∞):**

As ω → ∞, the term ω²R_p²C² in the denominator becomes very large. The fractions R_p/(1 + ω²R_p²C²) and ωR_p²C/(1 + ω²R_p²C²) both tend to **zero**. Therefore:

$$Z_{int}(\omega \to \infty) \approx R_{s}$$

This proves that at very high frequencies, the skin contact impedance becomes **purely resistive** and equal to the series resistance R_s (the gel and deep tissue resistance). The capacitor behaves as a short circuit, bypassing R_p entirely. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Frequency f = 10 kHz
- Current I = 5 mA RMS
- Voltage V = 50 mV RMS
- Phase angle φ = −45°

**(i) Magnitude of Impedance |Z|:**
Using Ohm's law for AC:

$$\vert Z \vert = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{50 \text{ mV}}{5 \text{ mA}} = 10 \text{ Ω}$$

**(ii) Equivalent Series Resistance R_eq:**
Since φ = −45°:

$$R_{eq} = \vert Z \vert \cos(\phi) = 10 \times \cos(-45°) = 10 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 7.07 \text{ Ω}$$

**(iii) Equivalent Series Capacitance C_eq:**
First, find the capacitive reactance:

$$X_{C} = \vert Z \vert \sin(\phi) = 10 \times \sin(-45°) = -7.07 \text{ Ω}$$

The negative sign confirms capacitive behaviour. The magnitude of reactance is 7.07 Ω.

$$X_{C} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C_{eq}} \implies C_{eq} = \frac{1}{2\pi f X_{C}}$$

$$C_{eq} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 10000 \cdot 7.07} = \frac{1}{444291.5}$$

$$C_{eq} \approx 2.25 \text{ μF}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- |Z| = **10 Ω**
- R_eq = **7.07 Ω**
- C_eq ≈ **2.25 μF**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of Phase Angle:** Students often forget that the phase angle of a capacitive circuit is **negative** (−45°), not positive. The negative sign MUST be carried through every computation.
> 2. **Units of Current and Voltage:** Mixing up mA with A, or mV with V, is the single most common reason for losing 2 marks in numericals. Always convert to SI base units (A, V, Hz) before substituting into the formula.
> 3. **Confusing R_p and R_s:** In the equivalent circuit, R_p is in **parallel** with C, while R_s is in **series** with the entire parallel combination. Do not interchange them.
> 4. **Forgetting the imaginary unit j:** When writing the final expression for Z, the term jωR_pC must be present, otherwise the model is purely resistive and physically incorrect.
> 5. **Effect of Abrasive Paste:** If the question is "How is skin impedance reduced in practice?", many students write "apply more gel" instead of the correct "use abrasive paste to remove stratum corneum."

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Skin Contact Impedance** is the total opposition at the electrode-skin boundary, modelled as **R_s + (R_p ∥ C)**.
- The **stratum corneum** is the single largest contributor to skin impedance and behaves as a **lossy dielectric (capacitor)**.
- Skin impedance is **inversely proportional to frequency** — high at low frequencies, low at high frequencies.
- **Abrasive paste** reduces skin impedance by mechanically removing the dead stratum corneum cells.
- The **half-cell potential** is a DC offset (~220 mV for Ag/AgCl) that must be blocked by the amplifier's high-pass filter.
- The **four-electrode (tetrapolar) method** is the industry gold standard because it eliminates electrode contact impedance from the measurement.
- The **corner frequency** of the interface is given by $f_{c} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{p} C}$, typically around **100 Hz to 200 Hz** for dry skin.
- The **phase angle** of a capacitive skin interface is **negative** (current leads voltage).
- **Gelled Ag/AgCl electrodes** are preferred over stainless steel for low noise and low half-cell potential.
- The standard measurement excitation is a **1 kHz to 10 kHz sine wave** with a constant current of **1 mA RMS**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LDR Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** LDR Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Light Dependent Resistor (LDR)**, also referred to as a **photoresistor**, **photoconductor**, or **photocell**, is a passive two-terminal semiconductor device whose internal electrical resistance varies inversely as a function of the incident luminous flux (light intensity) falling upon its photosensitive surface. The LDR operates on the principle of **photoconductivity**, wherein photons of sufficient energy striking the semiconductor material liberate valence electrons into the conduction band, thereby increasing the free charge carrier density and reducing the bulk resistance.

In the context of **biomedical instrumentation**, LDRs serve as transducers that convert an optical signal (light reflected from or transmitted through biological tissue) into a measurable electrical parameter (resistance), enabling non-invasive diagnostic procedures such as **pulse oximetry**, **photoplethysmography (PPG)**, and **blood glucose estimation via optical methods**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> LDR characteristics experiment aims to plot the variation of resistance versus incident light intensity (measured in **Lux**) and to verify the inverse exponential relationship between resistance and illuminance. Students must record readings, plot the V-I characteristics, and determine the **dark resistance** and **bright resistance** of the device.

### 1.2 Construction & Material Composition

An LDR is typically fabricated using a high-resistivity semiconductor material deposited on an insulating substrate (such as ceramic). The two most common photoconductive materials used are:

| Material | Chemical Symbol | Peak Spectral Sensitivity | Typical Application |
|----------|----------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Cadmium Sulfide | **CdS** | ~550 nm (visible green) | General purpose light sensing |
| Cadmium Selenide | **CdSe** | ~690 nm (visible red) | Biomedical red-light applications |
| Lead Sulfide | **PbS** | ~2700 nm (infrared) | Infrared pulse oximetry |

A **zig-zag or meander-shaped track** of the photoconductive material is patterned onto the substrate to maximize the active surface area exposed to incident light and to optimize the electrical contact geometry.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Automatic Stadium Dimmer":**
> Imagine a corridor light in a stadium tunnel that automatically adjusts itself. When it's pitch dark (night/no light), the light is extremely dim because very few photons are entering the corridor. As sunlight starts pouring in through the windows, the corridor light gets brighter and brighter because more photons flood in.
>
> **The LDR behaves exactly opposite!** In darkness, the LDR's resistance is **maximally high** (like a nearly closed valve blocking electrical current). When light falls on it, photons act like "little hammers" knocking electrons free from their atoms, opening multiple pathways for current to flow — the resistance **drops dramatically**. So, **more light → less resistance**, mimicking a valve that opens wider as light intensity increases.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Unit of Illuminance:** **Lux (lx)** — defined as 1 lumen per square meter ($1 \text{ lx} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^2$)
- **Unit of Luminous Flux:** **Lumen (lm)**
- **Planck's Constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- **Speed of Light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Standard Dark Resistance** (typical CdS LDR): **> 1 MΩ**
- **Standard Bright Resistance** (at 1000 lux): **~ 100 Ω to 1 kΩ**
- **Spectral Response Range (CdS):** **400 nm – 700 nm** (visible spectrum)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Photoconductivity Explained

The conduction mechanism inside an LDR can be understood in five sequential physical steps:

1. **Photon Absorption:** When photons of energy $E = h\nu$ strike the photoconductive material, they are absorbed by valence electrons. The photon energy must exceed the **band gap energy** $E_g$ of the semiconductor to liberate an electron.

2. **Electron-Hole Pair Generation:** A bound valence electron absorbs the photon and transitions to the conduction band, leaving behind a hole in the valence band. This creates an **electron-hole pair (EHP)**.

3. **Charge Carrier Injection:** Both the free electron (in conduction band) and the free hole (in valence band) become mobile charge carriers that contribute to electrical conduction.

4. **Conductivity Enhancement:** The conductivity $\sigma$ of the material increases proportionally with the number of photogenerated carriers:
$$\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$$

5. **Resistance Reduction:** Since $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A} = \dfrac{L}{\sigma A}$, an increase in conductivity $\sigma$ directly produces a decrease in resistance $R$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** The photon must have energy $h\nu \geq E_g$. If the photon energy is below the band gap, it passes through the material without interacting, and no EHPs are generated. For CdS, $E_g \approx 2.42 \text{ eV}$, which corresponds to a wavelength of $\lambda \approx 512 \text{ nm}$ — explaining its peak sensitivity in the green region of the visible spectrum.

### 2.2 Mathematical Model — The Characteristic Equation

The relationship between LDR resistance $R$ and incident illuminance $L_x$ (in lux) follows an **inverse power law (Steinhart-Hart-like empirical model)**:

$$R = A \cdot L_x^{-\alpha}$$

where:
- $R$ = resistance of the LDR (in Ohms, $\Omega$)
- $A$ = constant dependent on material and geometry (resistance at 1 lux)
- $L_x$ = incident illuminance (in Lux)
- $\alpha$ = dimensionless exponent (typically **0.7 to 0.9** for CdS, ideally close to **1.0** for ideal behavior)

A **linearized logarithmic form** is obtained by taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(R) = \ln(A) - \alpha \cdot \ln(L_x)$$

This is a straight-line equation of the form $y = mx + c$, where:
- Slope $m = -\alpha$
- Intercept $c = \ln(A)$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This $\ln(R)$ vs. $\ln(L_x)$ plot is the standard graphical representation used in KTU laboratory reports. A straight line on log-log axes confirms the inverse power-law behavior, and the slope yields the device-specific exponent $\alpha$.

### 2.3 V-I Characteristic (Static Behavior)

Under constant illumination, the LDR behaves as an **ohmic (linear) resistor** obeying Ohm's law:

$$V = I \cdot R(L_x)$$

where $R(L_x)$ is the resistance value corresponding to a fixed illumination level. The V-I curve under constant light is a straight line passing through the origin, with the slope equal to $\dfrac{1}{R(L_x)}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Validity / Range |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules (J) | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | Resistance from resistivity | $\Omega$ | Fundamental resistor law |
| 3 | $R = A \cdot L_x^{-\alpha}$ | LDR characteristic equation | $\Omega$ | Empirical, CdS: $\alpha \approx 0.7-0.9$ |
| 4 | $\ln(R) = \ln(A) - \alpha \ln(L_x)$ | Linearized log-log form | Dimensionless | Used for plotting |
| 5 | $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ | Photoconductivity | S/m | $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C |
| 6 | $V = I \cdot R$ | Ohm's law at fixed $L_x$ | V, A, $\Omega$ | Linear V-I region |
| 7 | $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{R_{dark}}{R_{light}}$ | Contrast ratio | Dimensionless | Higher = better sensor |
| 8 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\log(R_2/R_1)}{\log(L_{x1}/L_{x2})}$ | Slope of $\log R$ vs $\log L_x$ | Dimensionless | Equals $-\alpha$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Applications

1. **Pulse Oximeter (SpO₂ Sensor):** A dual-wavelength LED (660 nm red + 940 nm infrared) transmits light through a fingertip; a photodetector (LDR or photodiode) on the opposite side measures transmitted light intensity to compute arterial oxygen saturation.

2. **Photoplethysmograph (PPG):** Detects volumetric blood flow changes in tissue by monitoring light absorption variations.

3. **Automated Infant Incubator Lighting:** LDR-based feedback systems regulate phototherapy lamps used to treat neonatal jaundice.

4. **Endoscopy Illumination Control:** Maintains constant image brightness as the endoscope moves through body cavities with varying reflectivity.

5. **Sleep Study (Polysomnography):** Detects ambient light conditions during sleep cycles for circadian rhythm research.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linearized LDR Equation for Graphical Plotting

**Given:**
The empirical relationship governing LDR resistance is:
$$R = A \cdot L_x^{-\alpha} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 1 — Apply natural logarithm to both sides:**

Taking $\ln$ on both sides of equation (i):
$$\ln(R) = \ln(A \cdot L_x^{-\alpha})$$

**Step 2 — Apply logarithmic product rule:**

Using the identity $\ln(xy) = \ln(x) + \ln(y)$:
$$\ln(R) = \ln(A) + \ln(L_x^{-\alpha})$$

**Step 3 — Apply power rule of logarithms:**

Using the identity $\ln(x^k) = k\ln(x)$:
$$\ln(R) = \ln(A) + (-\alpha) \cdot \ln(L_x)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into linear (slope-intercept) form:**

$$\ln(R) = -\alpha \cdot \ln(L_x) + \ln(A)$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the standard straight-line equation $y = mx + c$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
y &= m \cdot x + c \\
\underbrace{\ln(R)}_{y} &= \underbrace{(-\alpha)}_{m} \cdot \underbrace{\ln(L_x)}_{x} + \underbrace{\ln(A)}_{c}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:**
- **Slope of $\ln(R)$ vs. $\ln(L_x)$ plot** = $-\alpha$ (a negative number, indicating decreasing resistance with increasing light)
- **Y-intercept** = $\ln(A)$
- A **straight-line plot** on log-log axes confirms the inverse power-law behavior.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Calculation of the Exponent $\alpha$ from Experimental Data

**Given two experimental observations:**
- At illuminance $L_{x1}$, the resistance is $R_1$
- At illuminance $L_{x2}$, the resistance is $R_2$

**Step 1 — Write the characteristic equation for both points:**

$$R_1 = A \cdot L_{x1}^{-\alpha} \quad \text{...(a)}$$
$$R_2 = A \cdot L_{x2}^{-\alpha} \quad \text{...(b)}$$

**Step 2 — Divide equation (a) by equation (b) to eliminate constant $A$:**

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{A \cdot L_{x1}^{-\alpha}}{A \cdot L_{x2}^{-\alpha}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify using exponential quotient rule:**

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \left(\frac{L_{x1}}{L_{x2}}\right)^{-\alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Take logarithm on both sides:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) = -\alpha \cdot \ln\left(\frac{L_{x1}}{L_{x2}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = -\frac{\ln(R_1/R_2)}{\ln(L_{x1}/L_{x2})} = \frac{\ln(R_2/R_1)}{\ln(L_{x1}/L_{x2})}$$

This is the **practical formula used in KTU lab record evaluations** to compute $\alpha$ from any two tabulated readings.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:**
An LDR exhibits the following readings in a laboratory experiment:

| Illuminance $L_x$ (Lux) | Resistance $R$ (kΩ) |
|-------------------------|---------------------|
| 10                      | 500                 |
| 100                     | 70                  |
| 500                     | 18                  |
| 1000                    | 9                   |

**Task:** Verify the inverse power-law behavior and determine the exponent $\alpha$.

**Solution — Step 1: Convert all values to logarithmic scale.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(L_x): \quad &\ln(10) = 2.303, \quad \ln(100) = 4.605, \quad \ln(500) = 6.215, \quad \ln(1000) = 6.908 \\
\ln(R): \quad &\ln(500) = 6.215, \quad \ln(70) = 4.248, \quad \ln(18) = 2.890, \quad \ln(9) = 2.197
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Apply the slope formula between the first and last points.**

$$\alpha = \frac{\ln(R_1/R_2)}{\ln(L_{x2}/L_{x1})}$$

Using $R_1 = 500$ kΩ at $L_{x1} = 10$ lux and $R_2 = 9$ kΩ at $L_{x2} = 1000$ lux:

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \frac{\ln(500/9)}{\ln(1000/10)} \\
&= \frac{\ln(55.56)}{\ln(100)} \\
&= \frac{4.017}{4.605} \\
&\approx 0.872
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Step 3: Compute the constant $A$.**

Using $R = A \cdot L_x^{-\alpha}$ with $R = 9$ kΩ and $L_x = 1000$ lux:

$$A = R \cdot L_x^{\alpha} = 9000 \times (1000)^{0.872}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(1000)^{0.872} &= 10^{3 \times 0.872} = 10^{2.616} \approx 413.0 \\
A &\approx 9000 \times 413.0 \approx 3.72 \times 10^6 \; \Omega \cdot \text{lux}^{\alpha}
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Step 4: Verify by substitution at $L_x = 100$ lux.**

$$R = 3.72 \times 10^6 \times (100)^{-0.872} = 3.72 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-1.744} \approx 67.8 \; \text{k}\Omega$$

This matches the experimentally observed value of **70 kΩ** within reasonable measurement tolerance — confirming the validity of the power-law model.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Computation

**Sensitivity** is defined as the ratio of dark resistance to bright resistance:

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{R_{dark}}{R_{light}}$$

For a typical CdS LDR with $R_{dark} = 5 \text{ M}\Omega$ and $R_{light}(1000\text{lx}) = 500 \text{ }\Omega$:

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{500} = 10000$$

This high sensitivity ratio (>10⁴) explains why LDRs are preferred in biomedical applications requiring wide dynamic range detection of optical signals.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — LDR Experimental Setup

The following block diagram depicts the signal flow and interconnections of a typical KTU laboratory experiment for studying LDR characteristics. The setup interfaces an LDR with a regulated DC source, a variable light source, and measurement instrumentation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDC[DC Regulated Power Supply 0 to 12V] --> nodeR1[Series Resistor R1 1kOhm Protection]
    nodeR1 --> nodeLDR[LDR Photoresistor CdS Cell]
    nodeLDR --> nodeGND[Ground Reference Node GND]
    nodeSRC[Variable Light Source Tungsten Halogen Lamp] -. Illuminance .-> nodeLDR
    nodeLUX[Lux Meter Calibrated Photodiode Sensor] -. Measures .-> nodeSRC
    nodeV[Voltmeter Across LDR Digital DMM] --> nodeLDR
    nodeA[Ammeter in Series Digital DMM] --> nodeR1
    nodeSW[Selector Switch SPDT] --> nodeV
    nodeSW --> nodeA
    nodeLDR --> nodeRcalc[Compute R from V divided by I Ohm Law]
    nodeRcalc --> nodePlot[Plot R versus Lx Graph Log Log Scale]
    nodePlot --> nodeAlpha[Determine Slope to find alpha]
```

### 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Model of LDR

This schematic represents the equivalent electrical model of an LDR, illustrating how the photogenerated conductance appears in parallel with the dark (intrinsic) conductance of the device.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeTermA[Terminal A Anode] --> nodeRdark[Dark Resistance Rdark MOhm range]
    nodeRdark --> nodeTermB[Terminal B Cathode]
    nodeTermA --> nodeRphoto[Photoconductance Path Rlight kOhm range]
    nodeRphoto --> nodeTermB
    nodePhoton[Incident Photons h nu greater than Eg] -. Activates .-> nodeRphoto
    nodeNote1[Dark Resistance Always Present Fixed by Material Geometry]
    nodeNote2[Photoresistance Decreases as Light Intensity Increases]
    nodeNote1 --- nodeRdark
    nodeNote2 --- nodeRphoto
```

### 4.3 Experimental Circuit Schematic — Series Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVsrc[VCC 0 to 12V Variable DC Source] --> nodeAmm[Ammeter A]
    nodeAmm --> nodeRfixed[Fixed Resistor Rf 1kOhm Current Limiting]
    nodeRfixed --> nodeLDR2[LDR CdS Device Under Test]
    nodeLDR2 --> nodeGND2[Ground GND]
    nodeVsrc -. Parallel Branch .-> nodeVmeter[Voltmeter V Measures VLDR]
    nodeVmeter --> nodeLDR2
    nodeLightSrc[Tunable Light Source Lamp] -. Light .-> nodeLDR2
    nodeLux2[Lux Meter] -. Reads .-> nodeLightSrc
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Pulse Oximeter Using LDR (Biomedical Application)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFinger[Patient Fingertip Tissue] --> nodeLED1[Red LED 660nm]
    nodeLED1 --> nodeLED2[Infrared LED 940nm]
    nodeFinger --> nodeLDRapp[LDR Photodetector]
    nodeLDRapp --> nodeAmp[Amplifier Stage]
    nodeAmp --> nodeADC[ADC Converter]
    nodeADC --> nodeMCU[Microcontroller Processing]
    nodeMCU --> nodeDisp[Display SpO2 and Heart Rate]
```

### 4.5 Characteristic Curve Visualization (R vs Lx)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeAxis[Graph Layout] --> nodeY[Vertical Axis R in Ohms Logarithmic Scale]
    nodeAxis --> nodeX[Horizontal Axis Lx in Lux Logarithmic Scale]
    nodeCurve[Curve Shape Inverse Power Law R decreases as Lx increases]
    nodeCurve --> nodeP1[At Low Light 10 lux R very high 500 kOhm]
    nodeCurve --> nodeP2[At Medium Light 500 lux R moderate 18 kOhm]
    nodeCurve --> nodeP3[At High Light 1000 lux R very low 9 kOhm]
    nodeP1 --- nodeY
    nodeP2 --- nodeY
    nodeP3 --- nodeY
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define an LDR. State any two biomedical applications of LDR.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Light Dependent Resistor (LDR) is a passive semiconductor device whose resistance decreases with an increase in incident light intensity. It is fabricated using photoconductive materials like Cadmium Sulfide (CdS).

Biomedical applications:
1. **Pulse oximeter** – used as a photodetector to measure oxygen saturation (SpO₂) by detecting transmitted light through the fingertip.
2. **Photoplethysmograph (PPG)** – used to monitor blood volume changes in tissue by detecting variations in reflected or transmitted light intensity.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by dark resistance and bright resistance of an LDR?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Dark Resistance:** The resistance of an LDR when no light falls on its surface (i.e., in complete darkness), typically in the range of **1 MΩ to 10 MΩ**.
- **Bright Resistance:** The resistance of an LDR when illuminated at a standard reference illuminance of **1000 lux**, typically in the range of **100 Ω to 1 kΩ**.

The ratio $R_{dark}/R_{light}$ is called the **sensitivity** of the LDR, indicating its dynamic range.

*(Award 1.5 marks each for the two definitions)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the experimental procedure to obtain the **V-I and R-Lx characteristics of an LDR**. List the apparatus required. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Apparatus Required (2 Marks):**
- LDR (CdS type)
- DC regulated power supply (0–12 V)
- Digital multimeter (used as voltmeter and ammeter)
- Series resistor (1 kΩ for current limiting)
- Variable light source (tungsten halogen lamp with dimmer)
- Lux meter (calibrated photodiode-based)
- Connecting wires and breadboard

**Circuit Diagram Description (2 Marks):**
The LDR is connected in **series** with a fixed resistor $R_f = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ across a variable DC source. A voltmeter is connected in parallel across the LDR to measure $V_{LDR}$, and an ammeter is connected in series to measure the circuit current $I$.

**Procedure (3 Marks):**
1. Place the LDR at a fixed distance from the light source.
2. Set the supply voltage to a constant value (say, 10 V).
3. Vary the distance of the light source to obtain different illuminance levels; measure the corresponding $V_{LDR}$ and $I$.
4. Calculate $R = V_{LDR}/I$ for each illuminance level.
5. Repeat steps for at least 5–6 illuminance values from 0 lux (dark) to 1000 lux (bright).
6. Tabulate the results and plot two graphs:
   - **V-I graph** at constant $L_x$ (should be linear).
   - **R-Lx graph** on log-log scale (should be a straight line with negative slope).

---

**(b)** From the following experimental data of an LDR, verify the inverse power-law relationship and calculate the exponent $\alpha$. **(7 Marks)**

| Illuminance $L_x$ (Lux) | 10  | 50  | 100 | 500 | 1000 |
|--------------------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|
| Resistance $R$ (kΩ)      | 600 | 130 | 70  | 18  | 9    |

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Logarithmic conversion (2 Marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(L_x):\quad & 2.303,\; 3.912,\; 4.605,\; 6.215,\; 6.908 \\
\ln(R):\quad & 6.397,\; 4.868,\; 4.248,\; 2.890,\; 2.197
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Plotting on log-log graph (1 Mark)**
The $\ln(R)$ vs. $\ln(L_x)$ plot yields a straight line with negative slope.

**Step 3: Slope calculation using extreme points (3 Marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= -\frac{\ln(R_1/R_2)}{\ln(L_{x1}/L_{x2})} \\
&= -\frac{\ln(600 \times 10^3 / 9 \times 10^3)}{\ln(10/1000)} \\
&= -\frac{\ln(66.67)}{\ln(0.01)} \\
&= -\frac{4.200}{-4.605} \\
&\approx 0.912
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 Mark)**
Since $\alpha \approx 0.912$ lies in the expected range of 0.7–0.9 for CdS, the **inverse power-law relationship is verified**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of an LDR** with reference to the photoconductivity mechanism and energy band diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Principle (2 Marks):**
The LDR operates on the principle of **photoconductivity** — the phenomenon in which the electrical conductivity of a semiconductor increases when exposed to light of suitable wavelength.

**Energy Band Diagram Explanation (3 Marks):**
1. In the valence band, electrons are tightly bound to atoms.
2. The forbidden energy gap ($E_g$) separates the valence band from the conduction band.
3. When a photon with energy $E = h\nu \geq E_g$ strikes the semiconductor, it transfers its energy to a valence electron.
4. The electron absorbs this energy and jumps from the valence band to the conduction band, leaving behind a hole.
5. This **electron-hole pair (EHP)** contributes to electrical conduction.

**Mathematical Treatment (2 Marks):**
The conductivity enhancement is given by:

$$\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$$

where $n$ and $p$ are photogenerated electron and hole concentrations respectively. Since resistance $R = L/(\sigma A)$, an increase in $\sigma$ directly reduces $R$.

For CdS, $E_g \approx 2.42$ eV, corresponding to $\lambda \approx 512$ nm (green light), explaining its peak spectral sensitivity.

---

**(b)** Describe the use of an LDR in a **pulse oximeter circuit**. Explain the role of dual-wavelength LEDs and how SpO₂ is computed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Working of Pulse Oximeter using LDR (7 Marks):**

A pulse oximeter is a non-invasive biomedical instrument used to measure arterial oxygen saturation (SpO₂).

**Components and Operation:**
1. **Dual LED Source (2 Marks):** Two Light Emitting Diodes are mounted on one side of the fingertip clip:
   - **Red LED** at 660 nm wavelength
   - **Infrared LED** at 940 nm wavelength
2. **LDR as Detector (2 Marks):** An LDR is mounted on the opposite side of the fingertip to detect the transmitted light. The resistance of the LDR varies with the transmitted light intensity.
3. **Time-Division Multiplexing (1 Mark):** The two LEDs are switched ON alternately in rapid succession (typically 100–1000 times per second), allowing the LDR to measure absorption at each wavelength separately.
4. **Computation of SpO₂ (2 Marks):**
   - Oxygenated hemoglobin (HbO₂) absorbs more infrared light.
   - Deoxygenated hemoglobin (Hb) absorbs more red light.
   - The ratio $R = \dfrac{AC_{red}/DC_{red}}{AC_{IR}/DC_{IR}}$ is computed, where AC and DC are the pulsatile and steady components of the LDR signal.
   - This ratio is fed into an empirical lookup table to compute SpO₂ percentage.

**Block Diagram Reference:** Refer to Section 4.4 above.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in LDR Characteristic Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students frequently write "Lumen" instead of "Lux" for illuminance. The correct unit is **Lux (lx) = lm/m²**. Examiner deducts **0.5 mark**.
>
> 2. **Plot Axes Mislabeling:** When plotting R vs. Lx, both axes MUST be on a **logarithmic scale** to obtain a straight line. Plotting on linear scale yields a hyperbolic curve that is harder to analyze. **Lose up to 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Sign of Slope Error:** The slope of the $\ln(R)$ vs. $\ln(L_x)$ plot is **negative**, and $\alpha$ is defined as a **positive** quantity. Students often report $\alpha$ as negative — wrong sign, **0.5 mark deduction**.
>
> 4. **Missing Directionality:** Students forget to state that "R decreases as Lx increases" explicitly in the conclusion. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
>
> 5. **Confusing LDR with Photodiode:** LDR is a **resistive** device (passive, two-terminal), whereas a photodiode is a **junction** device (active, requires reverse bias). Examiners deduct marks for interchange.
>
> 6. **Forgetting Series Resistance:** In experimental setups, students often forget to include the current-limiting series resistor. This is essential to prevent the LDR from drawing excessive current in bright light.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — LDR Characteristics:**

- **LDR Full Form:** Light Dependent Resistor (also called photoresistor/photocell).
- **Material:** Most commonly **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)** with band gap $E_g \approx 2.42$ eV.
- **Core Principle:** **Photoconductivity** — light energy liberates electron-hole pairs, increasing conductivity.
- **Fundamental Equation:** $R = A \cdot L_x^{-\alpha}$, where $\alpha \approx 0.7$–$0.9$ for CdS.
- **Linearized Form:** $\ln(R) = \ln(A) - \alpha \ln(L_x)$ — yields a straight line on log-log plot.
- **Slope Formula:** $\alpha = -\dfrac{\ln(R_1/R_2)}{\ln(L_{x1}/L_{x2})}$ (positive value expected).
- **Unit of Illuminance:** **Lux (lx)**.
- **Typical Dark Resistance:** **1 MΩ – 10 MΩ** (at 0 lux).
- **Typical Bright Resistance:** **100 Ω – 1 kΩ** (at 1000 lux).
- **Sensitivity Ratio:** $R_{dark}/R_{light} > 10^4$ for good CdS LDRs.
- **Photon Energy Condition:** $h\nu \geq E_g$, i.e., $\lambda \leq \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$.
- **V-I Behavior:** Linear (ohmic) at constant illumination — passes through origin.
- **Response Time:** Slow (50–200 ms) — not suitable for high-speed pulse detection in critical care; photodiodes preferred there.
- **Biomedical Applications:** Pulse oximeter, PPG, phototherapy monitor, incubator lighting control.
- **Experimental Setup:** Series LDR + fixed resistor across DC source; lux meter for illuminance measurement.
- **Graphs Required in Lab Record:** (1) V vs. I at constant $L_x$, (2) $\ln(R)$ vs. $\ln(L_x)$ to verify power law.
- **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always explicitly write the conclusion "inverse power-law relationship verified" with computed value of $\alpha$ near 0.9.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Pacemaker Circuit and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Pacemaker Circuit and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Pacemaker?

A **cardiac pacemaker** is an electronic bio-medical instrument that delivers precisely timed, low-energy electrical pulses to the heart muscle (myocardium) through electrodes placed in contact with the cardiac tissue, in order to initiate and regulate the heartbeat in patients suffering from cardiac arrhythmias such as **bradycardia** (abnormally slow heart rate), **heart block**, or **sick sinus syndrome**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A pacemaker circuit is essentially a **pulse generator** designed to mimic the natural electrical activity of the **Sino-Atrial (SA) node** — the heart's natural pacemaker — which normally fires at 60–100 beats per minute (bpm) for a healthy adult at rest.

### 1.2 Classification of Pacemakers (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **External (Transcutaneous) Pacemaker** | Pads placed on chest; pulses delivered through skin | Emergency, ICU, **Lab experiments** |
| **Internal (Implantable) Pacemaker** | Battery-powered, surgically placed under skin | Long-term therapy |
| **Fixed-Rate (Asynchronous)** | Pulses at constant rate, ignores natural rhythm | Rarely used today |
| **Demand (Synchronous) Pacemaker** | Fires only when natural rate falls below threshold | Most clinically common |
| **Rate-Responsive Pacemaker** | Adjusts rate based on activity (uses accelerometer) | Modern implants |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Heart's Conductor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine an orchestra where the lead violinist (the SA node) has fallen sick and is playing too slowly. A pacemaker acts as an **external conductor with a metronome**, tapping the violinist's shoulder (the heart muscle) with a tiny electrical cue at the correct tempo, restoring the rhythm of the entire orchestra. The cue must be **just strong enough** (enough amplitude) and **just brief enough** (narrow pulse width) to trigger the next beat without causing continuous contraction (tetanus).

## 1.4 Key Cardiac & Electronic Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Clinical Specifications (memorize for KTU viva):**
> - **Pacing rate (frequency):** **60–100 bpm** (1–1.67 Hz) for normal sinus rhythm
> - **Pulse width:** **0.5 ms – 2 ms** (must be short to avoid tetanic contraction)
> - **Pulse amplitude:** **5 V – 10 V** (external); **2.5 V – 5 V** (implantable)
> - **Output energy:** **25 – 50 μJ** per pulse
> - **Threshold sensitivity:** ~2 mV (for sensing natural R-waves)
> - **Heart rate conversion:** $f_{Hz} = \dfrac{bpm}{60}$

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Monostable + Astable Combination?

The output of a pure astable multivibrator has nearly a **50% duty cycle**, meaning the heart would be stimulated continuously — leading to **tetanus** (a sustained, non-rhythmic contraction that is fatal). Therefore, a practical pacemaker must generate **narrow pulses** at a **slow repetition rate**.

The standard KTU lab design uses **two cascaded stages**:

$$\text{Stage 1 (Astable)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Stage 2 (Monostable)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Output to Load (Heart model)}$$

- **Astable:** Sets the **pacing frequency** (e.g., 1 Hz = 60 bpm)
- **Monostable:** Sets the **pulse width** (e.g., 1 ms)
- **Output stage:** Transistor driver for current amplification

## 2.2 Circuit Topology — 555 Timer–Based Pacemaker

The **IC 555 timer** is the workhorse of this experiment. We use it in **two modes**:

### A. Astable Mode (Frequency Generator)

The 555 in astable mode charges capacitor C through (R_A + R_B) and discharges it through R_B only.

### B. Monostable Mode (Pulse-Width Generator)

The 555 in monostable mode produces a single pulse of fixed width when triggered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two 555 ICs?** One runs in astable mode (clock generator), the second in monostable mode (pulse-shaper). The astable output (falling edge) triggers the monostable, which then produces the narrow stimulation pulse.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

### 555 Timer in Astable Mode

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{HIGH} &= 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C \\
t_{LOW} &= 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C \\
T &= t_{HIGH} + t_{LOW} = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C \\
f &= \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C} \\
\text{Duty Cycle} \; D &= \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 555 Timer in Monostable Mode

$$
T_W = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C
$$

### Op-Amp Astable Multivibrator (Square-Wave Generator)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \\
T &= 2RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right) \\
\text{For } R_1 = R_2 \Rightarrow T &= 2RC \cdot \ln(3) \approx 2.197 \cdot RC
\end{aligned}
$$

### Op-Amp Monostable Multivibrator

$$
T_W = RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)
$$

### Heart-Rate Conversion (Critical for Design Verification)

$$
f_{Hz} = \frac{\text{Heart Rate (bpm)}}{60}
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Pacing rate | $f$ | 1.0 – 1.67 | Hz |
| Pulse width | $T_W$ | 0.5 – 2.0 | ms |
| Amplitude | $V_{out}$ | 5 – 10 | V |
| Cycle period | $T$ | 600 – 1000 | ms |
| Duty cycle | $D$ | 0.05 – 0.3 | % |
| Astable timing resistor A | $R_A$ | 1 – 10 | k$\Omega$ |
| Astable timing resistor B | $R_B$ | 10 – 100 | k$\Omega$ |
| Astable timing capacitor | $C$ | 1 – 100 | $\mu$F |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same pulse-generation principles used in the lab are applied in:
- **Defibrillators** (high-energy version of the same circuit)
- **TENS units** (Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation)
- **Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES)** for paralyzed muscles
- **Insulin pumps** and other implantable drug-delivery systems

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — 555 Timer Astable Frequency Equation

Consider the standard 555 astable circuit. Capacitor C charges from $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ toward $V_{CC}$ through resistors $R_A$ and $R_B$ in series, until it reaches $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, at which point the internal flip-flop resets and discharge begins.

**Step 1:** During charging, capacitor voltage follows:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{V_{CC}}{3}\right) e^{-t/\tau_1}
$$

where $\tau_1 = (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$.

**Step 2:** The capacitor charges from $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$. Substituting the boundary conditions:

$$
\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{V_{CC}}{3}\right) e^{-t_{HIGH}/\tau_1}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplifying:

$$
\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} e^{-t_{HIGH}/\tau_1}
$$

$$
\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} - V_{CC} = -\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} e^{-t_{HIGH}/\tau_1}
$$

$$
-\frac{V_{CC}}{3} = -\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} e^{-t_{HIGH}/\tau_1}
$$

$$
e^{-t_{HIGH}/\tau_1} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Taking natural log on both sides:

$$
-\frac{t_{HIGH}}{\tau_1} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)
$$

$$
t_{HIGH} = \ln(2) \cdot \tau_1 = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C
$$

**Step 5:** Similarly, during discharge, the capacitor discharges from $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ through $R_B$ only:

$$
t_{LOW} = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C
$$

**Step 6:** Total period:

$$
T = t_{HIGH} + t_{LOW} = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C
$$

**Step 7:** Frequency:

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **1.44** comes from $\dfrac{1}{0.693} = 1.443$, where 0.693 is the standard value of $\ln(2)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — 555 Timer Monostable Pulse Width

When a negative trigger pulse is applied (below $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$), the internal flip-flop SETS the output HIGH, and the discharge transistor turns OFF. Capacitor C charges through R toward $V_{CC}$.

**Step 1:** Initial voltage on capacitor: $V_C(0) = 0$

**Step 2:** Charging equation toward $V_{CC}$:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** The monostable resets when $V_C$ reaches $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$:

$$
\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-T_W/RC}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Simplifying:

$$
e^{-T_W/RC} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 5:** Taking natural log:

$$
T_W = RC \cdot \ln(3) = 1.0986 \cdot RC \approx 1.1 \cdot RC
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Design Example

**Design Problem (KTU style):** Design a 555-timer astable pacemaker to deliver pulses at **75 bpm** with a pulse width of **1.5 ms**. Use a monostable second stage.

### Step 1: Convert heart rate to frequency

$$
f = \frac{75}{60} = 1.25 \text{ Hz}
$$

### Step 2: Compute period

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.8 \text{ s} = 800 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3: Choose $R_B$ and compute $C$ for astable

Choose $R_A = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 68 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard values).

$$
800 \times 10^{-3} = 0.693 \cdot (1000 + 2 \cdot 68000) \cdot C
$$

$$
0.8 = 0.693 \cdot 137000 \cdot C
$$

$$
C = \frac{0.8}{94941} = 8.43 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} \approx 8.43 \;\mu\text{F}
$$

**Use standard value: $C = 10\;\mu\text{F}$**

### Step 4: Recompute frequency with $C = 10\;\mu\text{F}$

$$
f = \frac{1.44}{(1\text{k} + 136\text{k}) \cdot 10\mu} = \frac{1.44}{1.37} = 1.051 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
\text{Heart rate} = 1.051 \times 60 = 63 \text{ bpm} \quad \text{(acceptable for a bradycardia patient)}
$$

### Step 5: Compute monostable components for 1.5 ms pulse width

Choose $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$
C_{mono} = \frac{T_W}{1.1 \cdot R} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10000} = 136.4 \text{ nF}
$$

**Use standard value: $C = 150 \text{ nF}$** → Recompute $T_W = 1.1 \times 10\text{k} \times 150\text{nF} = 1.65 \text{ ms}$

---

## 3.4 Design Verification — Output Stage

The 555 output can source/sink only **200 mA**. For an external pacemaker (and even more so for any current-injecting application), a **transistor driver (e.g., BD139)** is used as a current amplifier:

$$
I_{load} = \beta_{DC} \cdot I_B
$$

where $I_B$ is the base current supplied via a series resistor from the 555 output. A typical base resistor:

$$
R_B = \frac{V_{OUT} - V_{BE}}{I_B}
$$
