# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a cardiac pacemaker. List any two clinical conditions that necessitate its use. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A cardiac pacemaker is an electronic pulse-generating medical device that delivers timed low-energy electrical stimuli to the myocardium through electrodes, in order to initiate or regulate the heartbeat when the natural SA-node activity is inadequate.

**Two clinical conditions:**
1. **Bradycardia** — sustained heart rate below 60 bpm
2. **Complete (third-degree) AV heart block** — atria and ventricles beat independently

*(Acceptable alternatives: Sick Sinus Syndrome, Stokes–Adams attacks, post-cardiac-surgery conduction defects.)*

---

### Q2. Why is the pulse width of a pacemaker kept narrow (typically 0.5–2 ms)? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The pulse width must be kept narrow for two primary reasons:

1. **To avoid tetanic contraction of the myocardium:** A long pulse would cause continuous stimulation, leading to a sustained (tetanic) contraction rather than a rhythmic beat, which is fatal.

2. **To conserve battery energy (in implantable units):** Narrow pulses minimize charge delivery per stimulus, extending battery life from years to decades. The typical threshold charge is only **0.5–1 μC**, achieved with pulse widths below 2 ms at amplitudes of 5 V.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must attempt **ONE full question out of two internal choices**.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a 555-timer based astable multivibrator pacemaker circuit. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**

The 555 timer IC consists of two internal comparators, an SR flip-flop, a discharge transistor, and a resistive voltage divider. In astable mode, it has **no stable state** — it continuously switches between HIGH and LOW, generating a square wave.

**Circuit Operation:**

1. Initially, capacitor C charges through resistors R_A and R_B toward V_CC.
2. When V_C reaches $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ (upper threshold), the upper comparator triggers the flip-flop, which turns ON the discharge transistor.
3. Capacitor C now discharges through R_B only, until V_C falls to $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ (lower threshold).
4. The lower comparator resets the flip-flop, turning OFF the discharge transistor.
5. The cycle repeats indefinitely, producing a continuous square wave at Pin 3.

**Frequency Derivation (already derived in Section 3.1):**

$$
f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}
$$

**For a pacemaker application:** This astable clock triggers the next stage (a monostable 555) that generates the narrow stimulation pulse.

---

**(b) Design a 555-timer pacemaker to deliver pulses at a rate of 72 bpm. Assume a pulse width of 1 ms for the monostable second stage. Show all component calculations.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Heart-rate to frequency conversion**

$$
f = \frac{72}{60} = 1.2 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2: Period**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = 0.833 \text{ s} = 833 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Choose standard resistor values**

Let $R_A = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_B = 68 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 4: Compute timing capacitor C**

$$
T = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C
$$

$$
0.833 = 0.693 \cdot (1000 + 136000) \cdot C
$$

$$
0.833 = 0.693 \cdot 137000 \cdot C
$$

$$
C = \frac{0.833}{94941} \approx 8.77 \;\mu\text{F}
$$

**Adopt standard value $C = 10\;\mu\text{F}$ (electrolytic, tantalum preferred for stability)**

**Recomputed rate:**

$$
f_{actual} = \frac{1.44}{137000 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.44}{1.37} = 1.051 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
\text{Heart rate} = 1.051 \times 60 = 63 \text{ bpm}
$$

*(Acceptable for bradycardia patient simulation; alternatively, retune $R_B$ to achieve 72 bpm.)*

**Step 5: Monostable pulse-width design**

Given $T_W = 1 \text{ ms}$, choose $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$
C_{mono} = \frac{T_W}{1.1 \cdot R} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^4} = 90.9 \text{ nF}
$$

**Use standard value $C_{mono} = 100 \text{ nF}$** → $T_W = 1.1 \times 10\text{k} \times 100\text{nF} = 1.1 \text{ ms}$ ✓

**Step 6: Output stage** — Add BD139 driver with base resistor $1 \text{ k}\Omega$ and coupling capacitor $10 \;\mu\text{F}$.

**Final Component List:**

| Component | Value | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R_A$ | 1 k$\Omega$ | Astable charge resistor |
| $R_B$ | 68 k$\Omega$ | Astable discharge resistor |
| $C_1$ | 10 $\mu$F | Astable timing capacitor |
| $R_{mono}$ | 10 k$\Omega$ | Monostable timing resistor |
| $C_{mono}$ | 100 nF | Monostable timing capacitor |
| Coupling C | 10 $\mu$F | DC blocking |
| BD139 | NPN | Current driver |

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the need for a monostable multivibrator stage in a pacemaker circuit. Describe the working of an op-amp based monostable multivibrator with a neat circuit diagram.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Need for monostable stage:**

A pure astable multivibrator produces a square wave with approximately 50% duty cycle. If used directly as a pacemaker output, the heart would receive continuous stimulation, leading to **tetanic contraction** — a sustained, non-physiological, and potentially fatal state. Therefore, the pacing signal must be a **brief, narrow pulse** (0.5–2 ms) repeated at the desired heart-rate interval.

The monostable multivibrator has **one stable state** and one **quasi-stable state**. When triggered, it switches to the quasi-stable state for a precisely defined duration $T_W$, then automatically returns to its stable state. This makes it ideal for generating narrow stimulation pulses from the slow astable clock.

**Op-amp Monostable Circuit Operation:**

Consider an op-amp with non-inverting input driven by a fraction $\beta V_{out}$ of the output (through a feedback divider $R_1, R_2$), where

$$
\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

- In the **stable state**, output is saturated at $+V_{sat}$, and the non-inverting input sits at $+\beta V_{sat}$.
- A **negative trigger pulse** applied to the inverting input (via a diode-clamped path) drives the op-amp output to $-V_{sat}$.
- The capacitor C, previously charged to $+V_{sat}$, now discharges through R toward $-V_{sat}$.
- When the capacitor voltage falls to $-\beta V_{sat}$, the op-amp output flips back to $+V_{sat}$, and the cycle ends.

**Pulse-width formula:**

$$
T_W = RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)
$$

For $R_1 = R_2$ (i.e., $\beta = 0.5$):

$$
T_W = RC \cdot \ln(3) \approx 1.0986 \cdot RC
$$

---

**(b) An op-amp based monostable multivibrator is to be used as the pulse-shaping stage of a pacemaker. Calculate the value of C required to obtain a pulse width of 1.5 ms. Assume R = 10 k$\Omega$ and the feedback resistors are equal ($R_1 = R_2 = 100$ k$\Omega$).** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_W = 1.5 \text{ ms} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$
- $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^4 \;\Omega$
- $R_1 = R_2 = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$

**Step 1: Compute the feedback fraction $\beta$**

$$
\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{100\text{k}}{100\text{k} + 100\text{k}} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2: Apply the pulse-width formula**

$$
T_W = RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)
$$

$$
1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 10^4 \cdot C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1.5}{0.5}\right)
$$

$$
1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 10^4 \cdot C \cdot \ln(3)
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\ln(3)$**

$$
\ln(3) = 1.0986
$$

**Step 4: Solve for C**

$$
C = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^4 \times 1.0986}
$$

$$
C = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{10986}
$$

$$
C = 1.366 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F} = 136.6 \text{ nF}
$$

**Step 5: Select nearest standard capacitor value**

**Use $C = 150 \text{ nF}$** (E12 series standard value)

**Verification:**

$$
T_W = 10^4 \times 150 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.0986 = 1.648 \text{ ms}
$$

This is within the acceptable 0.5–2 ms range for pacemaker pulse width. ✓

**Final Answer:** $C \approx 136 \text{ nF}$, use **150 nF** standard.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this question type:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\ln(2)$ derivation** — Examiners often award 2–3 marks purely for deriving the 0.693 factor in the 555 frequency equation. Students who just "write down the formula" lose these derivation marks.
> 2. **Confusing $R_A$ and $R_B$ roles** — $R_A$ only charges, $R_B$ both charges and discharges. Wrong formula gives wrong period.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion** — Converting **bpm to Hz** incorrectly (forgetting to divide by 60). This single error cascades through the entire design.
> 4. **Using non-standard capacitor values** — Always pick from the **E12 (10, 12, 15, 22, 33, 47, 68)** standard series and recompute the final value.
> 5. **Skipping the output stage** — Drawing the 555 alone and forgetting the transistor driver. Examiners explicitly test for **current-driving capability**.
> 6. **Not labelling axes/levels on waveforms** — When asked to draw waveforms, always mark $V_{CC}$, $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$, $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, $T_W$, and $T$ on the time axis.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pacemaker = Astable + Monostable** — Slow clock (1–2 Hz) drives a narrow pulse (0.5–2 ms).
- **555 Timer Astable Frequency:** $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$
- **555 Timer Monostable Pulse Width:** $T_W = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$
- **Op-Amp Monostable Pulse Width:** $T_W = RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$; for $\beta = 0.5$, $T_W \approx 1.1 \cdot RC$
- **Normal heart rate:** 60–100 bpm → 1.0–1.67 Hz; conversion factor is **÷ 60**.
- **Pulse width must be narrow** to prevent **tetanus** and conserve battery.
- **Output driver (BD139 / BC547)** is mandatory — 555 output current is insufficient for electrode coupling.
- **Coupling capacitor** blocks DC offset, ensuring only the pulse reaches the patient (patient safety).
- **Standard resistor series to memorize:** 1 k$\Omega$, 10 k$\Omega$, 47 k$\Omega$, 68 k$\Omega$, 100 k$\Omega$.
- **Standard capacitor series to memorize:** 10 nF, 100 nF, 1 $\mu$F, 10 $\mu$F, 47 $\mu$F.
- **Design verification step is compulsory** — Always recompute the actual heart-rate after choosing standard component values.
- **Two test points to capture on CRO:** Astable output (full period) and Monostable output (zoomed pulse width).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of IC 4051 and its Applications
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of IC 4051 and its Applications

The **IC 4051 (CD4051B / HCF4051B)** is a **CMOS analog single-pole, 8-position (SP8T) bilateral switch** that functions as an **8-to-1 analog multiplexer** (in the MUX mode) or a **1-to-8 analog demultiplexer** (in the DEMUX mode). It belongs to the **4000-series CMOS logic family** and is widely used in biomedical signal acquisition systems to time-share a single expensive Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) or amplifier across multiple input channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Specification**: The IC 4051 has **8 analog input/output channels** (Y0–Y7), **1 common terminal (Z)**, **3 binary control inputs (A, B, C)**, and **1 active-HIGH inhibit input (INH)**. The device operates over a wide supply range of **VDD = +3V to +15V** and supports bipolar analog signals up to **±7.5V** when VDD = +5V.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

Imagine you are a doctor in an ICU monitoring **8 critically ill patients** simultaneously, but you have only **one ECG display monitor**. You cannot buy 8 separate displays. So, you hire a **"smart switching nurse"** who, based on a **3-bit code (ABC)** you provide, connects **only one patient's ECG lead at a time** to the single display, scanning them very rapidly (say, 1 patient every millisecond). Because the switching is so fast, your eyes perceive all 8 patients as being monitored "simultaneously."

**The IC 4051 IS that smart switching nurse.**

- The **8 patients** = the 8 input channels (Y0–Y7) carrying biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, temperature, etc.).
- The **3-bit selection code (A, B, C)** = the digital address you supply from a microcontroller (like Arduino/8051).
- The **single display** = the common output terminal **Z** (connected to ADC or amplifier).
- The **"pause" button (INH)** = when HIGH, the switch disconnects everything (used for safety/blanking).

> [!NOTE]
> The IC 4051 is **bilateral**, meaning signals can flow in **either direction** — from inputs to common (MUX mode) OR from common to outputs (DEMUX mode). This is why it is called a **"transmission gate"** array.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Boundary Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Operating Limits of IC 4051 (from Texas Instruments / ON Semiconductor datasheets):**
> - **Supply Voltage (VDD)**: **+3V to +15V** (single supply) or **±3V to ±7.5V** (dual supply).
> - **Analog Signal Range**: **0V to VDD** (single) or **VDD to VEE** (dual supply operation).
> - **On Resistance (RON)**: Typically **125 Ω** at VDD = +5V, dropping to **80 Ω** at VDD = +15V.
> - **Switching Time (tPLH, tPHL)**: **20 ns to 60 ns** typical.
> - **Crosstalk**: **−50 dB** at 1 MHz.
> - **Off-Isolation**: **−50 dB** at 1 MHz.
> - **Leakage Current**: **±0.1 nA** typical at 25 °C.
> - **Operating Temperature**: **−55 °C to +125 °C** (military grade).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture Breakdown

The IC 4051 contains three major functional blocks working in harmony:

### 2.1.1 3-to-8 Line Decoder (Digital Control Core)
This is the **"brain"** of the IC. It takes the 3 binary inputs **A, B, C** and decodes them into **8 unique select lines (S0 to S7)**. Only one select line goes HIGH at any instant.

### 2.1.2 Level Shifter (Threshold Translator)
This circuit **translates the TTL/CMOS logic levels** (0V to 5V) applied at A, B, C, INH into the **full VDD–VEE swing** required to drive the analog transmission gates. This allows **3V microcontrollers** to control **±7.5V analog signals** — a critical feature for biomedical instrumentation.

### 2.1.3 8 CMOS Transmission Gates (Analog Switch Matrix)
Each of the 8 channels is a **CMOS bidirectional switch** consisting of a parallel combination of an **NMOS** and a **PMOS** transistor. When selected, both transistors turn ON, providing a low-resistance analog path between the channel pin and the common Z pin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Inhibit (INH)** pin is **active HIGH**. When INH = **1**, **all switches open** regardless of the A, B, C inputs. When INH = **0**, the channel selected by ABC is connected to Z. This is essential for **synchronization** in multiplexed ADC sampling.

## 2.2 The CMOS Transmission Gate — Heart of the Switch

A single transmission gate is built from one **NMOS** and one **PMOS** transistor in parallel, with complementary gate control:

- **NMOS** conducts well for **positive (high) analog voltages** (V_signal > V_th,N).
- **PMOS** conducts well for **negative or low analog voltages** (V_signal < |V_th,P|).
- When **both are ON simultaneously**, the parallel combination provides an **almost constant low resistance** across the entire analog range — a feature neither transistor alone can achieve.

$$
R_{ON,\text{total}} = R_{ON,N} \parallel R_{ON,P} = \frac{R_{ON,N} \cdot R_{ON,P}}{R_{ON,N} + R_{ON,P}}
$$

## 2.3 Truth Table of IC 4051 (Channel Selection)

| INH | C | B | A | Channel Selected (ON) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **Y0** |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **Y1** |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **Y2** |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **Y3** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **Y4** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **Y5** |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | **Y6** |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **Y7** |
| **1** | X | X | X | **NONE (All OFF)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **X = Don't care** (the value does not matter). When INH = 1, all channels are deselected — a feature exploited for **fault-safe biomedical designs**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units / Notes |
|:--|:--------|:-------------------|:--------------|
| 1 | Number of channels selected by *n* address lines | $N = 2^n$ | For 4051: $N = 2^3 = 8$ |
| 2 | Channel address (decimal) | $D = 4C + 2B + A$ | C is MSB, A is LSB |
| 3 | Multiplexed sample rate per channel | $f_s = \dfrac{f_{\text{ADC}}}{N}$ | Hz — required for **Nyquist compliance** |
| 4 | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max,signal}}$ | Avoid **aliasing** in ECG / EEG |
| 5 | On-resistance of CMOS switch | $R_{ON} \approx 125\ \Omega$ @ VDD = 5V | Ω — degrades with temperature |
| 6 | Settling time to 0.1% accuracy | $t_s \approx k \cdot R_{ON} \cdot C_{\text{load}}$ | seconds — use small C_load |
| 7 | Bandwidth (3 dB) of switch | $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{ON} C_{\text{stray}}}$ | Hz — stray cap ~10–20 pF |
| 8 | Effective throughput | $V_{out}(t) = V_{in,Yk}(t) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{D=k}$ | Discrete-time selector |
| 9 | Crosstalk suppression (adjacent channel) | $XT = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{leak}}}{V_{\text{sig}}}\right)$ | dB |
| 10 | Off-isolation | $OI = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in,OFF}}}\right)$ | dB (at specified freq) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not insert absolute-value bars `|x|`** inside markdown tables. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` in LaTeX form to keep tables parseable. The table above already follows this rule.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Biomedical Field

The IC 4051 is a **workhorse in biomedical instrumentation** because of three biomedical-specific advantages:

1. **Bipolar signal handling** — ECG, EEG, EMG are AC signals swinging around 0V; the dual-supply mode (VEE = −5V, VDD = +5V) passes these faithfully.
2. **Low leakage (~0.1 nA)** — critical when amplifying **microvolt-level EEG signals** (10–100 µV); leakage of just 1 nA through 10 kΩ would create **10 µV of error** — comparable to the signal itself!
3. **Zero quiescent current (CMOS)** — essential for **portable, battery-powered patient monitors**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Channel Addressing Logic

The IC 4051 has 3 binary address lines. Each line carries a weight based on its position.

**Step 1 — Define bit weights.**
A is the **LSB** (Least Significant Bit) → weight = $2^0 = 1$.
B is the middle bit → weight = $2^1 = 2$.
C is the **MSB** (Most Significant Bit) → weight = $2^2 = 4$.

**Step 2 — Form the binary-weighted sum.**
The decimal channel number $D$ selected is given by:

$$
D = (C \times 2^2) + (B \times 2^1) + (A \times 2^0)
$$

**Step 3 — Verification with examples.**

For ABC = 101 (binary):

$$
D = (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5
$$

So channel **Y5** is selected. This matches the truth table.

For ABC = 111 (binary):

$$
D = (1 \times 4) + (1 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7
$$

So channel **Y7** is selected — the highest addressable channel.

For ABC = 000 (binary):

$$
D = (0 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (0 \times 1) = 0
$$

So channel **Y0** is selected — the lowest addressable channel.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Multiplexed Sampling Rate for an N-Channel System

When 8 biomedical signals are time-shared through a single ADC, the **effective sampling rate per channel** drops by a factor of N.

**Step 1 — State the assumption.**
The ADC performs one conversion in $T_{\text{conv}}$ seconds, and the multiplexer cycles through all 8 channels sequentially with no dead time.

**Step 2 — Total cycle time for 8 channels.**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 8 \times T_{\text{conv}}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-channel sampling period.**

$$
T_s = T_{\text{cycle}} = 8 \cdot T_{\text{conv}}
$$

**Step 4 — Per-channel sampling frequency.**

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{1}{8 \cdot T_{\text{conv}}} = \frac{f_{\text{ADC}}}{8}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Nyquist criterion.**
To avoid aliasing, each input signal must be band-limited to:

$$
f_{\text{max,signal}} \leq \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{f_{\text{ADC}}}{16}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real Example**: For a typical ECG signal with $f_{\text{max}} = 150$ Hz, if $f_{\text{ADC}} = 2.4$ kHz, then $f_s = 2.4/8 = 300$ Hz per channel, and $f_{\text{max,allowed}} = 150$ Hz. ECG fits perfectly with no aliasing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandwidth of an ON Channel

The ON resistance of the IC 4051 switch, combined with the stray/load capacitance at the output node, forms a **first-order RC low-pass filter**. This filter limits the **usable analog bandwidth** of the multiplexer.

**Step 1 — Equivalent circuit.**
At the Z (common) pin, we have:
- $R_{ON}$ in series with the signal path.
- $C_{\text{stray}}$ (typically 10–20 pF, including PCB + input capacitance of next stage) to ground.

**Step 2 — Time constant of the RC filter.**

$$
\tau = R_{ON} \cdot C_{\text{stray}}
$$

**Step 3 — 3 dB cutoff frequency (bandwidth).**

$$
f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{ON} C_{\text{stray}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation.**
Assume $R_{ON} = 125\ \Omega$ and $C_{\text{stray}} = 20$ pF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= 125\ \Omega \times 20 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F} \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s} = 2.5\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{3dB} &= \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 125 \times 20 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= \frac{1}{1.5708 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&\approx 63.66\ \text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
The bandwidth is far higher than any biomedical signal (EEG cutoff ≈ 100 Hz, EMG ≈ 500 Hz, ECG ≈ 150 Hz), so the **IC 4051 does not distort** any practical biomedical waveform.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Settling Time to 0.1% Accuracy

When the multiplexer switches from one channel to the next, the output voltage at Z must settle to the new channel's value before the ADC samples it.

**Step 1 — Charging equation.**

$$
V_Z(t) = V_{\text{new}} + (V_{\text{old}} - V_{\text{new}}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}
$$

**Step 2 — For 0.1% settling (error = 0.001):**

$$
e^{-t_s/\tau} = 0.001 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{t_s}{\tau} = \ln(1000)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_s &= \tau \cdot \ln(1000) \\
&= 2.303 \times \tau \times 3 \\
&\approx 6.9 \cdot \tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — With $\tau = 2.5$ ns:**

$$
t_s \approx 6.9 \times 2.5\ \text{ns} \approx 17.3\ \text{ns}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
The output settles within **~20 ns**, meaning the ADC has the full rest of the sample window to perform a clean conversion. This makes IC 4051 ideal for **high-speed multi-channel data acquisition** in patient monitors.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Biomedical Signals vs. IC 4051 Suitability

| Signal | Amplitude | Bandwidth | Bipolar? | IC 4051 Compatible? |
|:-------|:---------:|:---------:|:--------:|:-------------------:|
| ECG | 0.5–5 mV | 0.05–150 Hz | Yes | **Yes** (use VEE = −5V) |
| EEG | 10–100 µV | 0.5–100 Hz | Yes | **Yes** (pre-amp needed) |
| EMG | 50 µV–5 mV | 20–500 Hz | Yes | **Yes** |
| Body Temp (via thermistor bridge) | 0–5 V | DC | No | **Yes** (single supply OK) |
| SpO2 photoplethysmogram (PPG) | 0.1–2 V | 0.5–10 Hz | No | **Yes** |
| Blood Pressure (arterial) | 0–300 mmHg → 0–5V | DC–40 Hz | No | **Yes** |
| Respiratory waveform (chest impedance) | 0.1–1 V | 0.1–10 Hz | No | **Yes** |

> [!NOTE]
> For microvolt-level signals like EEG, **a pre-amplifier with gain ≥ 1000** must be placed **before** the multiplexer to raise the signal well above the switch's noise floor and leakage-induced offset.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: IC 4051 Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[A: LSB Input] --> DEC[3-to-8 Binary Decoder]
    B1[B: Middle Input] --> DEC
    C1[C: MSB Input] --> DEC
    INH1[INH: Inhibit] --> DEC
    DEC --> LS[Level Shifter VDD to VEE]
    LS --> TG0[CMOS TG for Y0]
    LS --> TG1[CMOS TG for Y1]
    LS --> TG2[CMOS TG for Y2]
    LS --> TG3[CMOS TG for Y3]
    LS --> TG4[CMOS TG for Y4]
    LS --> TG5[CMOS TG for Y5]
    LS --> TG6[CMOS TG for Y6]
    LS --> TG7[CMOS TG for Y7]
    Y0in[Y0 Channel] --> TG0
    Y1in[Y1 Channel] --> TG1
    Y2in[Y2 Channel] --> TG2
    Y3in[Y3 Channel] --> TG3
    Y4in[Y4 Channel] --> TG4
    Y5in[Y5 Channel] --> TG5
    Y6in[Y6 Channel] --> TG6
    Y7in[Y7 Channel] --> TG7
    TG0 --> Z[Z: Common Terminal]
    TG1 --> Z
    TG2 --> Z
    TG3 --> Z
    TG4 --> Z
    TG5 --> Z
    TG6 --> Z
    TG7 --> Z
    Z --> OUT[To ADC or Amplifier]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Biomedical Application — 8-Channel Patient Monitor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P[8 PATIENT SOURCES]
        P1[Patient 1 ECG Lead]
        P2[Patient 2 ECG Lead]
        P3[Patient 3 ECG Lead]
        P4[Patient 4 ECG Lead]
        P5[Patient 5 SpO2 Sensor]
        P6[Patient 6 BP Sensor]
        P7[Patient 7 Temperature]
        P8[Patient 8 Respiration]
    end
    P1 --> PRE[Pre-amplifier and Filter Bank]
    P2 --> PRE
    P3 --> PRE
    P4 --> PRE
    P5 --> PRE
    P6 --> PRE
    P7 --> PRE
    P8 --> PRE
    PRE --> IC[IC 4051 Multiplexer]
    MCU[Microcontroller 8051 / Arduino] -->|3 bit Address ABC| IC
    MCU -->|INH line| IC
    IC --> SH[Sample and Hold Amplifier]
    SH --> ADC[Single ADC]
    ADC --> MCU
    MCU --> LCD[LCD Display or PC]
    IC --> VDD[(VDD +5V)]
    IC --> VEE[(VEE -5V)]
    IC --> GND[(VSS 0V)]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Pin-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PINS[IC 4051 Pinout 16 Pin DIP]
        PIN1[Pin 1: Y4 Channel]
        PIN2[Pin 2: Y6 Channel]
        PIN3[Pin 3: Z Common]
        PIN4[Pin 4: Y7 Channel]
        PIN5[Pin 5: Y5 Channel]
        PIN6[Pin 6: INH Inhibit]
        PIN7[Pin 7: VEE Negative Supply]
        PIN8[Pin 8: VSS Ground]
        PIN9[Pin 9: A Address LSB]
        PIN10[Pin 10: B Address]
        PIN11[Pin 11: C Address MSB]
        PIN12[Pin 12: Y3 Channel]
        PIN13[Pin 13: Y0 Channel]
        PIN14[Pin 14: Y1 Channel]
        PIN15[Pin 15: Y2 Channel]
        PIN16[Pin 16: VDD Positive Supply]
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Timing Diagram: Cyclic Multiplexing of 8 Channels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[T0: ABC 000] -->|Y0 sampled| T1[T1: ABC 001]
    T1 -->|Y1 sampled| T2[T2: ABC 010]
    T2 -->|Y2 sampled| T3[T3: ABC 011]
    T3 -->|Y3 sampled| T4[T4: ABC 100]
    T4 -->|Y4 sampled| T5[T5: ABC 101]
    T5 -->|Y5 sampled| T6[T6: ABC 110]
    T6 -->|Y6 sampled| T7[T7: ABC 111]
    T7 -->|Y7 sampled| T0
```

> [!NOTE]
> In practical firmware (Arduino/8051), the microcontroller sweeps ABC from 000 to 111, holds INH low, performs ADC read, then increments the address. The cycle repeats at a frequency equal to $f_{\text{ADC}} / 8$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Exam Paper]**
**Define the function of IC 4051. List any three biomedical applications where it is used.**

**Model Answer:**

IC 4051 is a **CMOS analog 8-to-1 multiplexer/demultiplexer** belonging to the 4000-series logic family. It contains eight analog input/output channels (Y0–Y7) that can be selectively connected to a single common terminal (Z) using a 3-bit binary address applied at pins A, B, and C. An additional **INH (Inhibit)** input allows disabling all channels simultaneously. The device operates from a single supply (3–15 V) or a dual supply (±3 to ±7.5 V), enabling it to pass bipolar biomedical signals.

**Three biomedical applications:**
1. **Multi-bed ICU patient monitor** — multiplexing ECG leads from 8 patients to a single ADC/display.
2. **EEG acquisition system** — switching between 8 electrode sites to time-share one amplifier chain.
3. **Polysomnography (sleep study)** — sequentially selecting EEG, EOG, EMG, ECG, nasal airflow, chest effort, SpO2, and body position signals into a single recording channel.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 0.5 each for applications)*

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of the INH pin of IC 4051. Why is level shifting necessary inside the IC?**

**Model Answer:**

The **INH (Inhibit)** pin is an **active-HIGH enable input**. When INH = 1, **all eight analog switches open** regardless of the logic state of A, B, C, isolating the Z terminal from every Yk channel. When INH = 0, the channel selected by the address ABC is connected to Z. This pin is used for **synchronization** (e.g., to blank the output during ADC channel transitions to prevent cross-talk spikes) and for **fault-safe shutdown** of the multiplexer.

**Level shifting is necessary** because the digital control inputs A, B, C, INH are typically driven by a **low-voltage microcontroller (3.3 V or 5 V TTL)**, whereas the analog biomedical signals (ECG, EEG) may swing across the full VDD to VEE range (e.g., +7.5 V to −7.5 V). The level shifter translates the logic-low/high of the address into **complementary gate drive voltages** capable of fully turning ON both NMOS and PMOS transistors of each transmission gate, ensuring low and constant R_ON across the entire analog range.

*(3 marks: 1 for INH function + 1 for application + 1 for level-shifting rationale)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the internal block diagram of IC 4051 and explain the function of the **3-to-8 decoder**, **level shifter**, and **transmission gate** blocks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **multi-channel ECG acquisition system** using IC 4051 to monitor **4 patients simultaneously**. Draw the system block diagram, identify the components used, and explain the role of the sample-and-hold circuit. State the sampling rate if the ADC operates at 1 kHz. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Block Diagram:**

```
A,B,C (3-bit) → [3-to-8 Decoder] → 8 select lines
              ↘
INH ──────────→ [Level Shifter] → CMOS Transmission Gates
              ↗
Y0–Y7 channels → [Transmission Gates] → Z (common) → ADC
```

**Function of each block:**

1. **3-to-8 Decoder**: Receives the 3-bit binary address (A, B, C) and activates **exactly one of the 8 internal select lines (S0–S7)** at a time, while the others remain LOW. The activated select line enables the corresponding transmission gate. It also receives the INH signal; when INH = 1, all 8 select lines are forced LOW, disabling every switch simultaneously.

2. **Level Shifter**: Translates the logic-level voltages (e.g., 0–5 V) present at the digital input pins A, B, C, INH into **complementary gate-drive voltages** spanning the full VDD to VEE range. This is essential because the analog signals at the Yk pins can swing over the entire VDD–VEE range, and both the NMOS and PMOS of each transmission gate need proper V_GS to turn ON fully. Without the level shifter, the switches would have variable and high R_ON, distorting biomedical signals.

3. **CMOS Transmission Gate**: Each channel uses a parallel combination of an **NMOS** and a **PMOS** transistor. The level shifter applies VDD to the PMOS gate and VEE to the NMOS gate when the channel is selected, turning both ON. The parallel ON-resistance is:

$$
R_{ON,\text{total}} = \frac{R_{ON,N} \cdot R_{ON,P}}{R_{ON,N} + R_{ON,P}}
$$

This parallel structure gives a **nearly constant low resistance (~125 Ω at VDD = 5 V)** over the entire analog range — a feature no single transistor can provide. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram + 2 for decoder + 1.5 for level shifter + 1.5 for transmission gate)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**System Block Diagram:**

```
Patient 1 ECG ─→ [Pre-amp IA] ─→ Y0 ─┐
Patient 2 ECG ─→ [Pre-amp IA] ─→ Y1  │
Patient 3 ECG ─→ [Pre-amp IA] ─→ Y2  ├→ IC 4051 ─→ Z ─→ [S/H] ─→ ADC ─→ µC ─→ Display
Patient 4 ECG ─→ [Pre-amp IA] ─→ Y3 ─┘
                                  ↑
                          ABC & INH from µC
```

**Components used:**
- **4 ECG pre-amplifiers** (e.g., AD620, INA128) — instrumentation amplifiers providing differential gain of ~1000, common-mode rejection ≥ 80 dB, and patient isolation via optocouplers.
- **Band-pass filter** (0.05–150 Hz) after each pre-amp to remove baseline wander and high-frequency noise.
- **IC 4051** — selects one patient's ECG at a time.
- **Sample-and-Hold (LF398)** — freezes the multiplexer output during ADC conversion to prevent channel-to-channel cross-talk and aperture errors.
- **12-bit ADC (ADC0808 / ADS1115)** — digitizes the held voltage.
- **Microcontroller (8051 / Arduino)** — generates the 3-bit address ABC, controls INH timing, reads ADC, displays waveforms.
- **LCD / PC display** — final visualization.

**Role of Sample-and-Hold:**
When the multiplexer switches from one channel to the next, the Z terminal voltage undergoes a **transient step** (settling time ~20 ns from earlier derivation). The S/H circuit is placed in **"hold" mode** during this transition and switches to **"sample" mode** only after settling. This guarantees that the ADC reads a **stable, accurate voltage** representing the selected channel, eliminating aperture error and preventing the ADC from sampling a mixture of two channels.

**Sampling rate calculation:**
With $f_{\text{ADC}} = 1$ kHz and $N = 4$ channels:

$$
f_s = \frac{f_{\text{ADC}}}{N} = \frac{1000}{4} = 250\ \text{Hz per channel}
$$

The maximum ECG frequency that can be captured without aliasing:

$$
f_{\text{max}} = \frac{f_s}{2} = 125\ \text{Hz}
$$

This comfortably covers the standard ECG bandwidth of **0.05–150 Hz** (with slight aliasing risk above 125 Hz, easily solved by adding a 100 Hz anti-aliasing filter). *(7 marks: 2 for block diagram + 1.5 for components + 1.5 for S/H role + 2 for sampling rate derivation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain how IC 4051 can be used as a **1-to-8 demultiplexer** to route a single timing/trigger pulse from a microcontroller to **8 different biomedical instruments** sequentially. State the role of the INH pin in this configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital ICU wants to monitor **8 patients' SpO2** signals using a single ADC. The ADC has a conversion time of **100 µs**. Calculate: **(i)** the per-channel sampling rate, **(ii)** the Nyquist frequency for the multiplexed system, and **(iii)** the percentage of time the multiplexer spends on each channel per cycle. Comment on whether this design is suitable for capturing the PPG waveform (bandwidth 0.5–10 Hz). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Circuit Diagram (IC 4051 as 1-to-8 DEMUX):**

```
                        IC 4051 (DEMUX mode)
   Trigger Pulse ──────→ Z (Common Input, Pin 3)
                          │
                          │ (selected channel routes it out)
                          ↓
   µC (8051) ──A,B,C──→ Pins 9, 10, 11
   µC ──INH────────────→ Pin 6
                          │
                          ↓
   Y0 ──→ Defibrillator 1 trigger
   Y1 ──→ Infusion Pump A trigger
   Y2 ──→ Ventilator sync
   Y3 ──→ Patient alarm 1
   Y4 ──→ Patient alarm 2
   Y5 ──→ Nurse station buzzer
   Y6 ──→ Data logger enable
   Y7 ──→ Spare
```

**Explanation:**
In demultiplexer mode, the **single input signal** (e.g., a 1 kHz trigger pulse from the microcontroller) is fed to the **Z (common) pin**. The 3-bit address A, B, C (driven by the microcontroller) determines **which of the 8 output pins (Y0–Y7)** receives the signal at any moment. Because the IC 4051 contains **bidirectional transmission gates**, the signal flows from Z → selected Yk. By rapidly sweeping the address from 000 to 111, the microcontroller can **time-multiplex the trigger** to up to 8 biomedical instruments in a round-robin fashion.

**Role of INH pin:**
The INH pin acts as a **synchronous blanking/gating control**. When the microcontroller is transitioning the address lines from one binary code to the next, **both the old and new channels can momentarily conduct** due to address-line skew, creating **"glitches"** or **shoot-through currents**. To prevent this, the microcontroller:
1. Drives **INH = 1** (high) **before** changing A, B, C — disconnecting all channels.
2. Updates A, B, C to the new address.
3. Drives **INH = 0** (low) — connecting only the new desired channel.

This guarantees **clean, glitch-free triggering** of sensitive medical instruments like defibrillators and infusion pumps. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram + 2 for explanation + 1.5 for trigger pulse routing logic + 1.5 for INH role)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- $N = 8$ channels (SpO2 for 8 patients)
- $T_{\text{conv}} = 100\ \mu\text{s}$
- $f_{\text{ADC}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{conv}}} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 10\ \text{kHz}$

**(i) Per-channel sampling rate:**

$$
f_s = \frac{f_{\text{ADC}}}{N} = \frac{10{,}000}{8} = 1250\ \text{Hz per channel}
$$

**(ii) Nyquist frequency of the multiplexed system:**

$$
f_{\text{Nyquist}} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{1250}{2} = 625\ \text{Hz}
$$

**(iii) Percentage of time per channel per cycle:**
The total cycle period for 8 channels is:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N \times T_{\text{conv}} = 8 \times 100\ \mu\text{s} = 800\ \mu\text{s}
$$

The time spent on each channel is $100\ \mu\text{s}$. The duty cycle percentage is:

$$
\text{Duty cycle} = \frac{100\ \mu\text{s}}{800\ \mu\text{s}} \times 100\% = 12.5\%
$$

**Comment on PPG waveform suitability:**

The PPG (photoplethysmogram) signal has a bandwidth of **0.5–10 Hz**. For faithful capture, the Nyquist criterion requires:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times f_{\text{max,PPG}} = 2 \times 10 = 20\ \text{Hz}
$$

Our design provides $f_s = 1250$ Hz per channel, which is **62.5 times higher** than the minimum required 20 Hz. Therefore, the design is **excellent** — it captures the PPG waveform with vast oversampling, providing:
- High signal-to-noise ratio (oversampling gain).
- Clean anti-aliased reconstruction.
- Headroom for digital filtering of motion artifacts and ambient light interference.

**Conclusion:** The IC 4051-based multiplexing scheme is **highly suitable** for 8-channel SpO2 monitoring. *(7 marks: 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 for the three calculations + 2.5 for suitability comment with Nyquist check)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing MUX and DEMUX pin assignments** — Students often forget that in MUX mode, **multiple inputs go to Y0–Y7** and output is taken from **Z**, but in DEMUX mode, the **input is at Z** and **outputs are Y0–Y7**. The IC is bidirectional, so always mention the *direction of signal flow* explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the INH pin role** — Many answers describe A, B, C addressing but **never mention INH**. Examiners specifically test the INH function in 3-mark and 7-mark questions. Always include it.
> 3. **Wrong bit-weight assignment** — Writing the address equation as $D = A + 2B + 4C$ but then **applying it in reverse** during the truth table. Always verify: A is LSB (weight 1), C is MSB (weight 4).
> 4. **Not addressing bipolar operation** — Biomedical signals swing both positive and negative. Failing to mention **dual supply (VEE = −5V)** when discussing ECG/EEG is a 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Forgetting the level shifter** — Calling IC 4051 "just a switch" without explaining the level-shifting block loses 1.5 marks in 7-mark descriptive questions.
> 6. **Slew rate / settling time not computed** — In 14-mark design questions, examiners expect a numerical calculation of $f_s$ and the Nyquist check. Skipping this is a 2-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IC 4051 (KTU Module 2)**

- **IC 4051** is a **CMOS 8-to-1 analog MUX** (or 1-to-8 DEMUX) — **SP8T switch** with **bidirectional** signal flow.
- **16-pin DIP** package. Pin 3 = **Z (common)**, Pins 9/10/11 = **A/B/C (address)**, Pin 6 = **INH (inhibit, active HIGH)**.
- **Power pins**: Pin 16 = VDD (+3 to +15 V), Pin 7 = VEE (0 V for single supply, −ve for bipolar), Pin 8 = VSS (ground).
- **Address equation**: $D = 4C + 2B + A$ → selects channel Y0 through Y7.
- **INH = 1** → all channels OFF; **INH = 0** → selected channel connected to Z.
- **Transmission gate** = NMOS ∥ PMOS, providing **low constant R_ON ≈ 125 Ω** at VDD = 5 V.
- **Level shifter** inside the IC allows 3.3 V/5 V logic to control ±7.5 V analog biomedical signals.
- **Bandwidth ≈ 60 MHz** (with C_stray = 20 pF) — far above any biomedical signal.
- **Settling time ≈ 20 ns** — allows fast channel-to-channel switching without ADC corruption.
- **Sampling rate per channel** = $f_s = f_{\text{ADC}} / 8$ for 8-channel MUX; must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$.
- **Leakage ≈ 0.1 nA** — safe for EEG-grade signals (µV level) when preceded by a pre-amp.
- **Biomedical applications**: ICU multi-bed ECG, EEG channel selection, polysomnography, SpO2/PPG multiplexing, body-temperature scanner, blood-pressure cycling, ventilator parameter logging.
- **Always use a Sample-and-Hold (LF398)** between IC 4051 and ADC to prevent transient sampling errors.
- **Bipolar supplies (VEE = −5V, VDD = +5V)** must be used for ECG/EEG/EMG; **single supply (VEE = 0, VDD = +5V)** is sufficient for SpO2, temperature, respiration.
- **Toggling INH before changing ABC** prevents address-skew glitches — a critical design practice for medical instruments.
- The IC 4051 is a **4000-series CMOS** device — distinct from the **74HC4051** (TTL/CMOS hybrid, similar function). Always verify the exact part number during PCB design.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Medical Equipment: ECG Machine, Sphygmomanometer, Patient Monitor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML408 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Medical Equipment: ECG Machine, Sphygmomanometer, Patient Monitor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBML408)**
> The study of standard clinical medical equipment forms the foundation of biomedical engineering practice. The three cornerstone instruments — **ECG Machine**, **Sphygmomanometer**, and **Patient Monitor** — represent electrical, mechanical, and hybrid bio-signal acquisition systems respectively, and are universally tested in the **Module Experiment 2** of the Biomedical Electronics Laboratory.

## 1.1 Electrocardiograph (ECG Machine)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The Electrocardiograph is a bio-electronic instrument that records the electrical activity of the heart over a period of time using electrodes placed on the skin. The graphical output is termed an **Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)**.

- **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine a stadium full of 100,000 fans doing "the wave." Each person is a heart cell. They don't all stand up at the exact same instant — a *wave* of standing-up travels around the stadium. The ECG machine is like an overhead camera placed on the stadium's edge; it cannot see individual people, but it can detect the electrical shadow of the wave as it sweeps past. The **P wave** = atria standing up, the **QRS complex** = ventricles standing up (the big, fast wave), and the **T wave** = everyone sitting back down. The rhythm of the wave tells you if the heart is healthy.
- **Standard Reference Values:**
  - **Heart Rate (Normal Adult): 60 – 100 BPM**
  - **Amplitude of R-wave: 1.0 – 1.5 mV**
  - **Paper Speed: 25 mm/s** (KTU standard)
  - **Calibration: 10 mm = 1 mV**

## 1.2 Sphygmomanometer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A Sphygmomanometer is a non-invasive medical device used to measure **arterial blood pressure (BP)**, comprising the systolic and diastolic pressures, expressed in **mmHg (millimeters of mercury)**.

- **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Picture a garden hose with water flowing through it. If you squeeze the hose (like a blood-pressure cuff squeezing the arm), the water pressure inside climbs. The moment the squeezing force becomes *less than* the water pressure inside, you hear a faint hissing/thud — that is the **systolic pressure** (peak pressure of each heartbeat). As you keep releasing the squeeze, eventually the hose is completely open and the thuds vanish — that silence point is the **diastolic pressure** (resting pressure). The doctor's stethoscope "listens" to those thuds, which are medically called **Korotkoff sounds**.

- **Standard Reference Values (Adult):**
  - **Systolic BP: 120 mmHg (Normal range: 90 – 120)**
  - **Diastolic BP: 80 mmHg (Normal range: 60 – 80)**
  - **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): ~ 93 mmHg**
  - **Hypertension Threshold: > 140/90 mmHg**

## 1.3 Patient Monitor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A Patient Monitor is a multi-parameter clinical device that continuously tracks and displays vital physiological signs of a patient in real time, integrating sensors, signal conditioning, microcontrollers, and a display unit into a single bedside console.

- **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of a Patient Monitor as the **"black box" of a hospital bed** — similar to the flight-data recorder in an airplane. Just as a pilot needs altitude, speed, and fuel level *continuously*, a clinician needs heart rate, blood oxygen (SpO₂), blood pressure, temperature, and respiration **without ever taking their eyes off the patient**. The monitor does this watching, sounding an alarm the instant any value crosses a safe threshold — its *autopilot safety net*.

- **Standard Monitored Parameters (KTU Standard 5-Lead Bedside Monitor):**
  - **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** → HR in **BPM**
  - **SpO₂ (Pulse Oximetry)** → **% saturation (95 – 100% normal)**
  - **NIBP (Non-Invasive Blood Pressure)** → **mmHg**
  - **Temperature** → **°C (36.1 – 37.2)**
  - **Respiration Rate (RR)** → **breaths/minute (12 – 20)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ECG Machine — Operational Logic Breakdown

The ECG machine operates on the principle of **bioelectric potential detection**. The heart, being a syncytium of electrically active cells, generates a measurable dipole potential at the body surface (typically **0.5 – 5 mV**).

**Sequential Logic of Operation:**

1. **Electrode–Skin Interface (Transduction Stage):** Ag/AgCl disposable electrodes form a **half-cell** with the skin's electrolyte layer. The electrolyte gel (typically saturated KCl) ensures low and stable **electrode-skin impedance (target < 5 kΩ)**.
2. **Lead Wire Network (Vector Selection):** Standard **12-lead system** uses 10 electrodes to compute 12 different "viewpoints" of the cardiac vector (3 bipolar: I, II, III; 3 augmented unipolar: aVR, aVL, aVF; 6 precordial: V1–V6).
3. **Patient Cable & Defibrillation Protection:** Series **10 kΩ current-limiting resistors** and gas-discharge tubes protect the input from defibrillator shocks.
4. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA):** **CMRR ≥ 100 dB**, gain of **1000×** to amplify mV-level signals.
5. **Filtering Block:** HPF (0.05 Hz) to remove DC baseline drift; LPF (~150 Hz) to remove EMG/mains noise; **50 Hz notch filter** to suppress power-line interference.
6. **ADC & Display:** Sampling rate of **≥ 500 samples/sec/channel**; printout on thermal array printer at **25 mm/s paper speed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 25 mm/s paper speed?** At this speed, **1 small box (1 mm) = 0.04 s** and **1 large box (5 mm) = 0.20 s**. This makes paper-based time calculations trivial: **Heart Rate = 1500 / (number of small boxes between two R-waves)**.

## 2.2 Sphygmomanometer — Theory Breakdown

The Sphygmomanometer operates on the **Riva-Rocci auscultatory principle** (for manual types) or **oscillometric principle** (for digital/automated types).

**Auscultatory Method (Mercury / Aneroid):**

- **Phase 1 (Systolic):** Cuff pressure equals systolic arterial pressure → first appearance of **Korotkoff sound** as blood begins to spurt through the occluded brachial artery.
- **Phase 2 – 4:** Turbulent flow sounds (murmurs, thuds, tapping) as cuff pressure decreases.
- **Phase 5 (Diastolic):** Sounds completely disappear — cuff pressure is below diastolic → laminar flow restored.

**Oscillometric Method (Digital):**

- The cuff acts as the sensor itself. As it deflates, **pressure oscillations** are detected in the cuff.
- Maximum oscillation amplitude correlates with **MAP**.
- Systolic and diastolic are algorithmically derived (typically at **0.55 × MAP amplitude** for systolic, **0.85 × MAP amplitude** for diastolic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cuff size rule (KTU clinical standard):** The bladder length must be **≥ 80%** and width **≥ 40%** of the arm circumference. A wrong-sized cuff gives a **falsely elevated reading** if cuff is too small.

## 2.3 Patient Monitor — Architecture Breakdown

A bedside patient monitor follows a **multi-channel sensor-fusion architecture**:

1. **Front-End Sensor Array:** ECG electrodes, SpO₂ finger probe (photoplethysmography), NIBP cuff, thermistor, impedance pneumography belts.
2. **Analog Signal Conditioning:** INA → filters → isolation amplifiers (for patient safety, **isolation ≥ 4000 V RMS**).
3. **Multiplexer + ADC:** Time-division multiplexed sampling of all channels.
4. **DSP / Microcontroller:** Computes HR, SpO₂ (Beer-Lambert law), RR, MAP.
5. **Display Driver:** TFT/LCD with waveform traces and numeric readouts.
6. **Alarm Engine:** Compares each parameter to user-set thresholds; triggers **audible (≥ 65 dB)** and visual alarms (red = high priority, yellow = medium, cyan = technical).
7. **Communication Ports:** RS-232, Ethernet, HL7 to central nursing station.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Governing Equation | Standard Value / Unit | KTU Notes |
|---|---------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Heart Rate from ECG | $HR = \frac{1500}{N_{small\ boxes}}$ | beats/min (BPM) | $N$ = small boxes between R-R |
| 2 | Heart Rate (alt) | $HR = \frac{60}{RR_{interval\ (s)}}$ | BPM | Used when digital |
| 3 | Paper Calibration | $10\ \text{mm (vertical)} = 1\ \text{mV}$ | 1 mV amplitude | Standard gain |
| 4 | Paper Time | $25\ \text{mm/s}$ | 0.04 s/small box | Standard speed |
| 5 | Mean Arterial Pressure | $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ | mmHg | Auscultatory formula |
| 6 | MAP (Oscillometric) | $MAP \approx P_{osc,max}$ | mmHg | At max oscillation |
| 7 | SpO₂ (Beer–Lambert) | $I_{trans} = I_0 \cdot e^{-\epsilon c l}$ | Ratio-based % | Uses 660 nm & 940 nm LEDs |
| 8 | SpO₂ Computation | $R = \frac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$ | Dimensionless | Empirical lookup table |
| 9 | Body Surface Area | $BSA = \sqrt{\frac{H(cm) \cdot W(kg)}{3600}}$ | m² | Mosteller formula |
| 10 | Lead-II Vector | $II = \text{Leg}^+ - \text{RA}^-$ | mV | Most common diagnostic lead |
| 11 | Systolic Normal | $SBP \le 120\ \text{mmHg}$ | mmHg | Adult resting |
| 12 | Diastolic Normal | $DBP \le 80\ \text{mmHg}$ | mmHg | Adult resting |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown. No step is skipped. Equations are typeset in LaTeX with one blank line before and after every display block.

## 3.1 Derivation: Heart Rate from R–R Interval on ECG Paper

**Given:** A patient's ECG strip recorded at the standard **25 mm/s** paper speed. The distance between **two consecutive R-waves** is measured as **20 small boxes** (each small box = 1 mm).

**Step 1 — Convert measured distance to time:**

$$1\ \text{small box} = 1\ \text{mm} = \frac{1}{25}\ \text{seconds} = 0.04\ \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate R–R interval in seconds:**

$$RR_{interval} = 20\ \text{small boxes} \times 0.04\ \frac{\text{s}}{\text{box}}$$

$$RR_{interval} = 0.80\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Heart Rate formula:**

$$HR = \frac{60}{RR_{interval}\ (\text{in seconds})}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and evaluate:**

$$HR = \frac{60}{0.80}$$

$$HR = 75\ \text{BPM}$$

**Conclusion:** The heart rate is **75 beats per minute**, which lies in the normal adult range of **60 – 100 BPM**. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) using the Auscultatory Method

**Given:** Sphygmomanometer reading shows **SBP = 120 mmHg** and **DBP = 80 mmHg**.

**Step 1 — Recall the clinically accepted MAP formula:**

$$MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}\,(SBP - DBP)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the pulse pressure term:**

$$SBP - DBP = 120 - 80 = 40\ \text{mmHg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the one-third component:**

$$\frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP) = \frac{1}{3} \times 40 = 13.33\ \text{mmHg}$$

**Step 4 — Add to DBP:**

$$MAP = 80 + 13.33$$

$$MAP = 93.33\ \text{mmHg} \approx 93\ \text{mmHg}$$

**Conclusion:** The MAP is approximately **93 mmHg**, which is within the autoregulation range (**60 – 100 mmHg**) of cerebral and renal perfusion. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: SpO₂ from the Beer–Lambert Law

**Step 1 — Beer–Lambert Law (monochromatic):**

$$I_{trans} = I_0 \cdot e^{-\epsilon(\lambda)\, c\, l}$$

**Step 2 — Take natural log and rearrange to absorbance $A$:**

$$A(\lambda) = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_{trans}}\right) = \epsilon(\lambda)\, c\, l$$

**Step 3 — Pulse oximeter uses two wavelengths: Red (660 nm) and Infrared (940 nm).** The pulsatile (AC) component represents arterial blood; the non-pulsatile (DC) component represents tissue, venous blood, and bone.

**Step 4 — Define the ratio of ratios $R$:**

$$R = \frac{\left(\frac{AC}{DC}\right)_{660\ \text{nm}}}{\left(\frac{AC}{DC}\right)_{940\ \text{nm}}}$$

**Step 5 — Empirical calibration (industry-standard curve):**

$$SpO_2 \approx 110 - 25 \times R \quad (\%)$$

**Step 6 — Worked example:** If $\left(\frac{AC}{DC}\right)_{660} = 0.02$ and $\left(\frac{AC}{DC}\right)_{940} = 0.04$:

$$R = \frac{0.02}{0.04} = 0.50$$

$$SpO_2 = 110 - 25(0.50) = 110 - 12.5 = 97.5\%$$

**Conclusion:** SpO₂ = **97.5%**, which is within the normal range (**95 – 100%**). ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary (KTU Exam Ready)

| Feature | ECG Machine | Sphygmomanometer | Patient Monitor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parameter measured** | Electrical activity of heart | Arterial blood pressure | Multi-parameter (HR, SpO₂, NIBP, Temp, RR) |
| **Sensor** | Ag/AgCl skin electrodes | Inflatable cuff + stethoscope / pressure transducer | Multi-sensor (ECG, PPG, NIBP, thermistor) |
| **Signal type** | Electrical (mV) | Mechanical / acoustic | Mixed (electrical + optical + mechanical) |
| **Output** | Paper strip / digital | mmHg reading | TFT display + alarms |
| **Principle** | Bio-potential recording | Auscultatory / Oscillometric | Sensor fusion + DSP |
| **Range (normal)** | 0.5 – 5 mV, 60 – 100 BPM | 90/60 – 120/80 mmHg | HR 60–100, SpO₂ 95–100, RR 12–20 |
| **Calibration** | 10 mm = 1 mV | Mercury column zero / manometer check | Simulator / NIBP test rig |
| **KTU experiment activity** | Connect leads, observe P-QRS-T on CRO/PC | Measure BP of 5 subjects, tabulate | Identify all parameters, set alarm limits |
| **Hazards** | Microshock, defibrillation | Skin abrasion, mercury toxicity | Alarm fatigue, electrical isolation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid** with strict adherence to the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards: alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords as node names, no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels.

## 4.1 ECG Machine — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[HEART_DIPOLE_SOURCE] --> B1[SKIN_ELECTRODE_INTERFACE]
    B1 --> C1[LEAD_WIRE_NETWORK_12_LEAD]
    C1 --> D1[DEFIBRILLATION_PROTECTION_10K]
    D1 --> E1[INSTRUMENTATION_AMPLIFIER_GAIN_1000]
    E1 --> F1[FILTER_BLOCK_HPF_LPF_NOTCH]
    F1 --> G1[ADC_SAMPLING_500_SPS]
    G1 --> H1[CPU_DSP_AND_DISPLAY]
    H1 --> I1[THERMAL_PRINTER_OR_LCD]
    H1 --> J1[ALARM_ENGINE]
```

## 4.2 Sphygmomanometer — Operating Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[ARM_WITH_BRACHIAL_ARTERY] --> B2[INFLATABLE_CUFF_BLADDER]
    B2 --> C2[RUBBER_BULB_AND_VALVE]
    B2 --> D2[PRESSURE_TRANSDUCER_OR_MERCURY_COLUMN]
    D2 --> E2{OPERATING_MODE}
    E2 -->|MANUAL| F1[STETHOSCOPE_AUSCULTATION]
    E2 -->|DIGITAL| F2[OSCILLOMETRIC_SENSOR_AMPLIFIER]
    F1 --> G2[OBSERVER_READS_KOROTKOFF_SOUNDS]
    F2 --> H2[MICROCONTROLLER_ALGORITHM]
    H2 --> I2[LCD_DISPLAY_SBP_DBP_MM_HG]
    G2 --> I2
```

## 4.3 Patient Monitor — Top-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSING_LAYER
        S1[ECG_ELECTRODES]
        S2[SPO2_FINGER_PROBE]
        S3[NIBP_CUFF]
        S4[TEMP_THERMISTOR]
        S5[IMPEDANCE_PNEUMOGRAPH]
    end
    SENSING_LAYER --> X1[SIGNAL_CONDITIONING_AND_ISOLATION]
    X1 --> X2[MULTIPLEXER_AND_ADC]
    X2 --> X3[DSP_AND_MICROCONTROLLER]
    X3 --> X4[DISPLAY_DRIVER_TFT]
    X3 --> X5[ALARM_ENGINE_WITH_THRESHOLDS]
    X3 --> X6[COMMUNICATION_RS232_ETHERNET_HL7]
    X4 --> X7[CLINICIAN_BEDSIDE_VIEW]
    X5 --> X7
    X6 --> X8[CENTRAL_NURSING_STATION]
```

## 4.4 ECG Waveform — Component Identification (P-QRS-T)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[ISOELECTRIC_BASELINE] --> W2[P_WAVE_ATRIAL_DEPOLARIZATION]
    W2 --> W3[PR_SEGMENT_AV_NODE_DELAY]
    W3 --> W4[QRS_COMPLEX_VENTRICULAR_DEPOLARIZATION]
    W4 --> W5[ST_SEGMENT_VENTRICULAR_REPOLARIZATION_PAUSE]
    W5 --> W6[T_WAVE_VENTRICULAR_REPOLARIZATION]
    W6 --> W7[U_WAVE_OFTEN_INVISIBLE_PAPILLARY_MUSCLE]
```

## 4.5 Korotkoff Sound Phases — Sphygmomanometer Auscultation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[CUFF_INFLATED_ABOVE_SBP_SILENCE] --> K2[PHASE_1_FIRST_TAPPING_SBP_READ]
    K2 --> K3[PHASE_2_SOFT_MURMURS]
    K3 --> K4[PHASE_3_LOUDER_THUDS]
    K4 --> K5[PHASE_4_MUFFLING]
    K5 --> K6[PHASE_5_SILENCE_DBP_READ]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
> **Q1.** What are the five standard leads of an ECG and what is the purpose of the **Right Leg (RL)** electrode?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The five standard leads in a basic ECG system are: **Lead I (LA–RA), Lead II (LL–RA), Lead III (LL–LA), and the augmented leads aVR, aVL, aVF** (with Right Leg as the common reference/ground electrode). The **Right Leg electrode** is not used as a sensing lead but serves as the **driven-right-leg (DRL) ground reference**. It actively drives common-mode noise back into the patient, thereby increasing the **CMRR** of the instrumentation amplifier and reducing **50 Hz power-line interference**. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **Q2.** List the **five phases of Korotkoff sounds** and state which phases correspond to **systolic** and **diastolic** readings.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The five phases are: **Phase 1** – first appearance of clear tapping sounds → corresponds to **Systolic BP**; **Phase 2** – softer murmurs; **Phase 3** – louder, crisper thuds; **Phase 4** – distinct muffling of sounds; **Phase 5** – complete disappearance of sounds → corresponds to **Diastolic BP**. Clinically, Phase 1 onset marks SBP and Phase 5 disappearance marks DBP. (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Module 2 Question Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the working of a **single-channel ECG machine**. Mention the standard value of **paper speed** and **vertical calibration**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A patient has an R–R interval of **0.75 seconds** on a standard ECG strip. Calculate the **heart rate in BPM** and comment on whether the value is **normal or abnormal**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 Marks]** — Block Diagram & Working (as in §4.1 above)

1. **Electrodes (1 mark):** Ag/AgCl electrodes pick up cardiac potentials (~0.5 – 5 mV) from body surface.
2. **Lead Selector (1 mark):** Routes the desired lead pair to the amplifier (e.g., Lead II for routine monitoring).
3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (1.5 marks):** High CMRR (≥ 100 dB), gain of 1000× amplifies mV signals to volt level; differential input rejects common-mode noise.
4. **Filters (1.5 marks):** HPF (0.05 Hz) removes baseline wander; LPF (150 Hz) blocks EMG/mains HF noise; 50 Hz notch filter suppresses power-line interference.
5. **ADC + DSP + Display/Printer (1 mark):** Digitized signal drives thermal array printer.
6. **Calibration standards (1 mark):** **Paper speed = 25 mm/s**; **Vertical gain = 10 mm/mV** (i.e., 1 cm deflection = 1 mV).

**(b) [7 Marks]** — Heart Rate Calculation

$$RR_{interval} = 0.75\ \text{s}$$

**Step 1 — Apply formula:**

$$HR = \frac{60}{RR_{interval}} = \frac{60}{0.75}$$

$$HR = 80\ \text{BPM}$$

**Step 2 — Clinical interpretation:** Since 80 BPM lies within the **normal adult range of 60 – 100 BPM**, the patient's heart rate is classified as **NORMAL SINUS RHYTHM (NSR)**. (2 marks for interpretation)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **auscultatory method** of blood pressure measurement with a **neat diagram of a mercury sphygmomanometer**. State the **five phases of Korotkoff sounds**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A patient records a blood pressure of **150/95 mmHg**. Calculate the **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)** and state whether the patient is **normotensive, pre-hypertensive, or hypertensive**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 Marks]** — Auscultatory Method

1. **Cuff placement (1 mark):** Wrapped around the upper arm, **2 – 3 cm above the antecubital fossa**, with stethoscope diaphragm over the **brachial artery**.
2. **Inflation (1 mark):** Cuff inflated to **~ 30 mmHg above the palpatory SBP** to fully occlude the artery → no sound.
3. **Deflation (1.5 marks):** Pressure released slowly at **2 – 3 mmHg per second**. As cuff pressure crosses SBP, blood spurts through → tapping sound (Phase 1).
4. **Auscultation (1.5 marks):** Sounds evolve through Phases 2, 3, 4 and disappear at Phase 5. SBP is read at Phase 1 onset, DBP at Phase 5 disappearance.
5. **Korotkoff phases listed (2 marks):** (i) Phase 1 — first tapping; (ii) Phase 2 — murmurs; (iii) Phase 3 — louder thuds; (iv) Phase 4 — muffling; (v) Phase 5 — silence.

**(b) [7 Marks]** — MAP Calculation

**Step 1 — Given:** SBP = 150 mmHg, DBP = 95 mmHg.

**Step 2 — Pulse pressure:**

$$PP = SBP - DBP = 150 - 95 = 55\ \text{mmHg}$$

**Step 3 — MAP formula:**

$$MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP) = 95 + \frac{55}{3} = 95 + 18.33$$

$$MAP = 113.33\ \text{mmHg} \approx 113\ \text{mmHg}$$

**Step 4 — Classification (2 marks):** Per WHO/AHA guidelines, SBP ≥ 140 and/or DBP ≥ 90 → **Stage 2 Hypertension**. The patient is therefore **HYPERTENSIVE** and the MAP being > 100 mmHg indicates significant cardiovascular risk requiring medical intervention.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit mmHg** in blood pressure answers → **–0.5 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Lead I, II, III polarity** (e.g., writing Lead II = LA – RA, which is wrong; Lead II = LL – RA) → **–1 mark**.
> 3. **Omitting paper speed (25 mm/s)** in ECG block diagram answers → **–1 mark**; this is a KTU favorite.
> 4. **Writing "blood pressure = average of SBP and DBP"** in MAP question → **2 marks deducted**; correct formula is the 1/3 weighted formula.
> 5. **Confusing Korotkoff Phase 1 with Phase 5** — must clearly state SBP at *first appearance* and DBP at *disappearance*, not at muffling.
> 6. **SpO₂ question trap:** writing the Beer–Lambert law but forgetting the empirical $110 - 25R$ lookup equation → **–1 mark**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this before exam!)**

- **ECG:**
  - Paper speed = **25 mm/s**; Vertical gain = **10 mm = 1 mV**.
  - 1 small box = 1 mm = **0.04 s**; 1 large box = 5 mm = **0.20 s**.
  - Einthoven's Triangle: **I + III = II** (vector law).
  - Lead II is the **rhythm lead** of choice (parallel to cardiac axis).
  - Normal R–R interval: **0.6 – 1.0 s** (60 – 100 BPM).
  - ECG machine bandwidth: **0.05 – 150 Hz**; CMRR ≥ **100 dB**.

- **Sphygmomanometer:**
  - **SBP** = first Korotkoff tap; **DBP** = Phase 5 silence.
  - **MAP = DBP + 1/3(SBP − DBP)** (≈ 93 mmHg normal).
  - Cuff width = **40% of arm circumference**; length = **80%**.
  - Mercury sphygmomanometer is the **gold standard**; aneroid requires **6-monthly calibration**.
  - Oscillometric devices measure **MAP directly**; SBP/DBP are algorithmically derived.

- **Patient Monitor:**
  - Five standard parameters: **ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, Temperature, Respiration**.
  - **SpO₂** uses **660 nm (red)** and **940 nm (IR)** LEDs; normal = **95 – 100%**.
  - **Patient isolation ≥ 4000 V RMS** for defibrillation protection.
  - Alarm priority: **Red = high**, **Yellow = medium**, **Cyan = technical/advisory**.
  - HL7/Ethernet enables **central nursing station** monitoring.

- **Universal Clinical Constants to Memorize:**
  - Normal HR: **60 – 100 BPM**
  - Normal SpO₂: **95 – 100%**
  - Normal RR: **12 – 20 /min**
  - Normal body temp: **36.1 – 37.2 °C**
  - Normal BP: **120/80 mmHg** (MAP ≈ 93 mmHg)
  - Hypertension cut-off: **≥ 140/90 mmHg**
